chapters.text:
1            Internal Structure of Earth

The earth has three main layers — the crust, mantle, and core. Crust is the outermost and thinnest layer, made of Sial (land) and
Sima (ocean), and ends at the Moho. Mantle, below the crust, is made of heavy rocks and includes the asthenosphere. The core is
made of iron and nickel (Nife), with a liquid outer and solid inner layer, separated by the Lehmann Discontinuity.
 Liquid Outer Core: Molten iron–nickel layer driving the geodynamo; recent evidence shows regional flow reversals influencing
    magnetic-field dynamics.
 Solid Inner Core: Solid iron–nickel sphere generating planetary stability; recent studies reveal about slowed rotation relative
    to the surface, refining geodynamic understanding.

                                                UPSC Mains PYQs
      1.    What are aurora australis and aurora borealis? How are these triggered? (2024)
      2.    Define mantle plume and explain its role in plate tectonics. (2018)


Note: Modern geophysics prefers identifying crustal types as Granitic/Continental and Basaltic/Oceanic based on mineralogy
and density in place of Sial/Sima.

1.1 Sources of Earth’s Interior

                   Direct Sources                                                      Example
 Mining: Deep mines provide rock samples and               Mponeng Gold Mine (South Africa) shows crustal rock behavior
 temperature data, helping understand composition          up to ~4 km depth
 and conditions of the Earth's crust.
 Volcanic Eruptions: Magma brings up material from         Hawaiian basalts and kimberlite pipes (South Africa) bring mantle-
 deep layers, helping us study mantle composition.         derived xenoliths, indicating olivine–pyroxene composition.
 Deep Ocean Drilling: Provides insights on crust           Kola Superdeep Borehole in Russia uncovered unexpected heat,
 structure, heat flow, and even life in deep rocks.        water, and 2-billion-year-old microfossils.

                  Indirect Sources                                                    Examples
 Seismic Waves: Earthquake waves (P and S waves)           Large earthquakes like the 2011 Tohoku Earthquake improved
 reveal solid and liquid layers and important              understanding of S-wave shadow zone and Earth's interior
 boundaries inside Earth.                                  structure.
 Gravitational Anomalies: Changes in gravity point to      Himalayan gravity surveys revealed crustal thickening beneath the
 uneven mass distribution, helping locate dense            mountains, consistent with continental collision and isostasy.
 rocks or crustal variations.


 Meteorite Composition: Meteorites contain materials        Iron meteorites like Canyon Diablo (Arizona) contain iron-nickel
 like iron and nickel, similar to earth’s core, offering    alloys, supporting theories of planetary differentiation and core
 clues about its early composition.                         formation.
 Magnetic fields: Earth’s magnetic field is generated       Magnetic reversals recorded in basalt rocks show that the field has
 by geodynamo effect (movement of molten iron in            changed direction many times.
 outer core); studying its changes helps understand core
 dynamics.

1.2 Seismic Waves
Seismic waves are energy waves generated during an earthquake that travel through and along the Earth. Based on their path
and behavior, they are classified into Body Waves and Surface Waves, both playing a crucial role in understanding earth's
internal structure.


                                                           Body Waves
 P-Waves           y Particle displacement parallel to wave direction →move in a compressional (push-pull) manner
 (Primary)         y Fastest waves and arrive first after an earthquake.
                   y Can travel through solids, liquids, and gases→Can travel through Earth's core
 S-Waves           y Particle displacement perpendicular to wave direction→ move the ground side-to-side
 (Secondary)       y Slower than P-waves; Second to arrive after an earthquake
                   y Travel only through solids → Cannot travel through Earth's liquid outer core

                                                       Surface Waves
 Love              These move the ground horizontally, causing high structural damage. They are confined to the surface
 Waves             and fade with depth.
 Rayleigh          They produce a rolling motion (both vertical and horizontal), travel slowest, and are often the most
 Waves             destructive during earthquakes.

                                        Role in Understanding Earth’s Interior
 Identification    Seismic waves change speed and direction as they pass through materials of different density and
 of Layers         elasticity, helping identify earth's main layers – the crust, mantle, outer core, and inner core.
 Shadow Zone       y S-waves do not pass through the outer core, confirming it is liquid.
 Evidence          y P-waves refract strongly at the core boundaries, creating shadow zones, which helped scientists infer the
                     liquid outer core and solid inner core.
 Material          The reflection and refraction of seismic waves at internal boundaries reveal the state (solid or liquid) and
 Properties        composition of materials inside the earth.
 Density and       Variations in wave velocity with depth help scientists infer the thickness, density differences, and overall
 Composition       structure of Earth’s internal layers.


1.3 Mantle Plumes
Mantle plumes are narrow, rising columns of anomalously hot solid mantle material that originate deep within the Earth’s mantle,
possibly near the core–mantle boundary. Many are believed to arise from the Dʺ layer, a thermally and chemically distinct zone
characterized by anomalous seismic velocities and altered P- and S-wave behaviour.


                                              Formation of Mantle Plume

         Stage                                                          Process
 Heat                      In the lower mantle, intense heat builds up due to radioactive decay and primordial heat from
 Generation                Earth’s formation.
 Buoyant                   Heat causes certain mantle regions to become anomalously hot and less dense, leading to buoyant
 Material Forms            upwelling.
 Rising Through            The hot, buoyant material begins to rise through the cooler, denser mantle above it.
 Mantle
 Partial                   As the material rises, pressure drops, causing partial melting—producing a mix of magma and
 Melting                   solid rock.
 Plume                     The hot material forms a narrow, vertical column called a mantle plume, which may reach the surface
 Formation                 and form volcanoes, especially in areas away from plate boundaries — called intraplate settings.

                                      Role of Mantle Plumes in Plate Tectonics
 Rifting and Plate         Mantle upwelling and localized hot material can help uplift and weaken the lithosphere,
 Breakup                   contributing to continental rifting. Example: East African Rift System.
 Flood Basalt              When a mantle plume head reaches the lithosphere, it causes massive decompression melting,
 Provinces                 producing large-scale basaltic eruptions (flood basalts). Example: Deccan Traps (India) and
                           Siberian Traps (Asia).
 Formation of              Mantle plumes create volcanic hotspots, where hot magma rises from deep mantle and produces
 Volcanic Hotspots         continuous volcanic activity. As tectonic plates move over these hotspots, chains of volcanic
                           islands or seamounts are formed. Example: Hawaiian Islands.
 Exchange of Material      Mantle plumes allow transfer of heat, material, and energy from Earth’s deep interior to the
 and Energy                surface, influencing volcanism and plate movement.
 Tectonic                  While plate tectonics explains activity at plate boundaries, mantle plumes help explain volcanism
 Complementarity           within plates, crustal doming, and other non-boundary tectonic features.


1.4. Geomagnetism
Geomagnetism is the study of earth’s magnetic field, created by the movement of molten iron and nickel in the outer core
(called the geodynamo process). Earth acts like a giant dipole magnet, with magnetic poles near the geographic poles.


                                         Impact of Shifting Geomagnetism
 Navigation Errors      y Changes in Earth’s magnetic field cause magnetic north to drift, leading to compass errors for
                          pilots, sailors, and military navigation.
                        y Example: Re-designation of airport runways.
 Power Cuts             y Big changes in the magnetic field can create unwanted electric currents in power lines, which
                          may lead to blackouts. Example: 1989 Quebec power failure in Canada.
 Communication          y Magnetic storms can disturb the upper atmosphere, causing radio blackouts and problems with
 Issues                   airline communication, especially near the poles.
                        y Example: In March 2026, A powerful X-class solar flare caused a high-frequency radio blackout,
                          impacting maritime and aviation communications.
 Climate and            y Magnetic field variations influence cosmic rays, affecting cloud formation and potentially altering
 Atmospheric              climate patterns.
 Changes                y Example: Increased cosmic rays linked with cloud formation changes.
 Biological and         y Many species rely on geomagnetism for navigation; shifts disturb migration and breeding
 Ecological Impacts       behavior.
                        y Example: Sea turtles lose nesting direction during magnetic field variations.

                                           Significance of Geomagnetism
 Protection from        Acts as a protective shield by deflecting harmful solar radiation and charged particles away from
 Solar Radiation        Earth’s surface, reducing radiation exposure.
 Van Allen              Forms belts of trapped charged particles that capture and contain much of the dangerous solar
 Radiation Belts        radiation.
 Auroras near the       Particle interactions with the magnetic field near polar regions produce auroras, safely releasing
 Poles                  energy and preventing damage to life, satellites, and communication systems.
 Insights into          Helps scientists understand Earth’s interior, tectonic activity, and provides paleomagnetic records
 Earth’s Interior       of past magnetic fields preserved in rocks.


A. Auroras
Auroras are natural light displays seen near the poles—Aurora Borealis in the northern hemisphere and Aurora Australis in the
southern hemisphere. They occur when charged solar particles interact with Earth’s magnetic field and atmosphere, exciting gases
like oxygen (green/red) and nitrogen (blue/purple). They offer scientific insights into space weather and solar wind dynamics.

                           Factors Triggering Formation of Auroras


    Solar Wind: Auroras are triggered by solar wind — streams of charged particles (electrons and protons) emitted from the
     Sun, especially during solar flares and coronal mass ejections (CMEs).
    Magnetic Field Interaction: When these particles reach Earth, the magnetic field guides them toward polar regions, making
     auroras more common near the Arctic and Antarctic circles.
    Excitation of Atmospheric Gases: Charged particles collide with oxygen and nitrogen in the upper atmosphere, transferring
     energy and exciting atoms to higher energy states.
    Emission of Light: Excited atoms release energy as visible light when returning to normal state.
           Atomic oxygen produces green auroras (~100–150 km) and red auroras at higher altitudes (>200 km)
           Molecular nitrogen produces blue and purple hues at lower altitudes.
    Geomagnetic Storms: During strong solar storms, more charged particles enter Earth’s atmosphere, producing brighter
     auroras visible at lower latitudes.
pyq: What are aurora australis and aurora borealis? How are these triggered? (2024)
Define mantle plume and explain its role in plate tectonics. (2018)

chapters.t: Forces Acting on Earth: Endogenic and Exogenic
chapters.text:
2               Forces Acting on Earth:
                         Endogenic and Exogenic
The Earth's surface is constantly changing due to a variety of natural forces. These forces are broadly classified into endogenic
(internal) and exogenic (external) forces. The interaction between these two sets of forces determines the formation, destruction, and
modification of landforms.


                                                         Types of Forces

   Dimension                        Endogenetic Forces                                        Exogenetic Forces
 Energy               Originate within Earth’s interior; powered by            Originate outside the Earth, driven by solar
 Source               radioactive decay, residual gravitational energy, and    energy and gravity.
                      internal heat.
 Main                 Construct landforms via folding, faulting, volcanism,    Modify or wear down landforms through
 Function             earthquakes, and mountain building.                      weathering, erosion, mass wasting, and deposition.
 Type of              Involves both slow (diastrophic) movements like          Processes are mostly gradual, but can include
 Movements            epeirogeny (vertical) and orogeny (horizontal), and      sudden events like landslides.
                      sudden (catastrophic) events like earthquakes.
 Agents               Driven by tectonic forces, magma movement, and           Carried out by wind, water, glaciers, waves, and
                      geothermal activity; creates mountains, rift             humans (Anthropogenic Geomorphology); forms
                      valleys, and volcanic landforms.                         river valleys, sand dunes, deltas, and coastal features.
 Significance         Also known as Primary landform constructive              Also known as Secondary landscape modifying
                      force essential for studying plate tectonics,            force key to understanding land degradation, soil
                      earthquakes, and volcanoes.                              erosion, and surface shaping.

2.1. Endogenic
                                              Endogenic Processes
  Originate within Earth’s interior; powered by radioactive decay, residual gravitational energy, and internal heat.


Diastrophism           y Gradual processes that may last for thousands of years, deforming the crust through folding or
(Slow Movements)         fracturing.
                       y They include:
                            ♦ Orogenic processes: mountain building through severe folding → severe deformation;
                               operate in a horizontal manner.
                            ♦ Epeirogenic processes: continental building process through uplift or warping of large
                               parts of the earth’s crust → simple deformation; operate in a vertical manner.
                       y Uplift examples: Colorado Plateau, Brazilian Highlands, Australian Shield
                       y Subsidence examples: Siberian Basin, Kachchh region
Sudden                 y High-intensity forces originating deep within the Earth's mantle due to tectonic movements
Movements                (convectional currents); Occur mostly at lithospheric plate margins.
                       y Volcanism and Earthquakes → Cause rapid changes in contours, river courses, and shorelines.
                         Example: 2004 Indian Ocean earthquake submerged Indira Point.

2.2. Exogenic

                                                 Exogenic Processes
y Originate from outside the Earth and act on its surface, derive energy from solar energy and gravity.
y Temperature and precipitation control various processes by inducing stress in earth materials.
y All exogenic processes collectively called Denudation → wearing down or stripping away of the Earth's surface layers
y Main processes: Weathering, Erosion, Deposition, Mass Movement
Weathering      y In-situ disintegration and decomposition of rocks and minerals caused by various elements of weather and
                  climate through physical, chemical, and biological processes.
                y Types:
                     ♦ Physical: Mechanical disintegration of rocks due to:
                           ♦ gravitation (load and shear stress)
                           ♦ expansion forces (freezing, thawing, salt formation, etc.)
                           ♦ water pressures controlled by wetting and drying cycles.
                     ♦ Chemical: Decomposition by chemical processes such as:
                           ♦ solution (e.g., nitrates, sulphates),
                           ♦ hydration (e.g. calcium sulphate),
                           ♦ carbonation (e.g., calcium carbonates),
                           ♦ oxidation and reduction (e.g., iron, manganese).
                     ♦ Biological: Disintegration due to growth and movement of organisms. E.g., burrowing and wedging
                        by earthworms, pressure exerted by roots of plants, decaying of plants and animals etc.
                y Significance:
                     ♦ Prepares rock material (regolith) for soil formation → supporting vegetation, biomes (depends upon
                        depth of weathering mantles) and biodiversity.
                     ♦ Forms different types of sedimentary rocks like sandstones and limestones and landforms (Karst
                        topography) and periglacial landforms.
                     ♦ Concentrates valuable ores like iron, bauxite, and releases essential nutrients → economically
                        viable to exploit.
                     ♦ Weathered rocks like the granite tors promote tourism. E.g., Bismarck rock in Mwanza, Tanzania.
                     ♦ Helps in Carbon sequestration (carbonation).
Mass            Downslope movement of rocks, soil, and debris under gravity. They can be slow (creep, solifluction) or
Movement        rapid (landslides, mudflows, and rockfalls).
Erosion         Removal and transport of weathered material by wind, water, ice, and gravity
Deposition      Occurs when transported materials settle in new locations, forming landforms.

3               Plate Tectonics

                                                 UPSC Mains PYQs
      1.    Discuss how the changes in shape and sizes of continents and ocean basins of the
            planet take place due to tectonic movements of the crustal masses. (2025)
      2.    Briefly mention the alignment of major mountain ranges of the world and explain their
            impact on local weather conditions, with examples. (2021)
      3.    Why is India considered as a subcontinent? Elaborate your answer. (2021)
      4.    Explain the formation of thousand of islands in Indonesian and Philippines archipelagos.
            (2014)
      5.    What do you understand by the theory of continental drift? Discuss the prominent
            evidence in its support. (2013)


3.1. Continental Drift Theory
Proposed by Alfred Wegener in 1912, the Continental Drift Theory suggests that continents in the Carboniferous Period were
joined in a supercontinent Pangaea (all earth) surrounded by Panthalassa (all water). Pangea broke down in the Mesozoic Era
into Laurasia (North) and Gondwanaland (South) and drifted apart under forces like Pole fleeing (centrifugal) force, tidal force
(Gravitational pull of the Sun and Moon), buoyant force of water. However, critics argued that these forces were not enough to cause
such massive land masses to move.


                Tectonic Movements Reshaping Continents and Ocean Basins


Current Dynamics: Seven major plates drive changes; Pacific contracts, Atlantic expands, Himalayas rise ~1 cm/yr → generate
earthquakes, volcanism, and isostatic adjustments, perpetually reconfiguring Earth's surface.

                           Evidence                                                           Criticisms
 Jig-saw Fit: The continental shelf margins of South America           Wegener wrongly assumed continents (SiAl) drifted
 and Africa fit more closely than the present coastlines,              through the ocean floor (SiMa) like a ship, rather than
 supporting the idea that they were once joined (Pangaea).             moving with the lithospheric plates.
 Fossil Correlation: Identical fossils (e.g., Mesosaurus,              Opponents proposed the existence of now-submerged land
 Glossopteris) are found on continents now separated by oceans.        bridges or oceanic dispersal as alternative explanations.
 Paleoclimatic Evidence: Glacial deposits in tropical regions          Climatic interpretations could be explained by changes in
 (such as India, Africa, and South America)) indicate these            Earth’s climate rather than continental movement.
 landmasses were once clustered near the South Pole.
 Geological Similarities: Matching rock formations and                 Geological evidence deemed insufficient; similarities
 mountain ranges (such as Appalachians and Caledonides)                possibly due to other processes, not drift.
 across continents.
 Paleomagnetic Data: Magnetic minerals in rocks show                   Early paleomagnetic data was limited, leading to doubts
 continents’ past positions, supporting their movement over            about global applicability.
 time.
 Placer Deposits: Ghana's rich gold placers have no local              Placer correlation between Ghana and Brazil could result
 source rocks; matching gold-quartz veins occur in Brazil,             from ancient river systems or dispersal, not continental
 showing these continents were formerly connected.                     connection.
This theory was revolutionary as it challenged the "Permanency Theory" of continents and oceans and provided the conceptual
foundation for Sea Floor Spreading and Plate Tectonics.

3.2. Convectional Current Theory
The Convectional Current Theory, propounded by Arthur Holmes in the 1930s, provided the first scientifically plausible motive
mechanism for the movement of continents.
 Idea: Convection currents in the mantle are driven by radioactive disintegration and earth’s residual primordial heat. These
    currents operate within the asthenosphere, moving horizontally beneath the lithosphere, dragging the tectonic plates along
    with them.
          At the Divergent Boundary, the convection cell limb rises → formation of Mid-Oceanic Ridges
          At the Convergent Boundary, the convection cell limb falls → formation of Oceanic Trenches and Fold Mountains.
 Evidence: Geothermal energy flow, Earthquakes deep in Earth; Seismic imaging shows mantle plumes rising.
 Significance: Provided the driving force behind continental drift (Wegener’s idea). Linked mantle activity to plate movement.
 Limitations: No clear proof of complete convection cells. Uncertainty if radioactive heat alone can move heavy plates.


                                                    Seafloor spreading
Based on Arthur Holmes' Convectional Current Theory, Harry Hess (1962) suggested that the ocean floor is not a static, vast
plain but a dynamic relief undergoing constant creation and destruction ("Conveyor Belt" Process). At Mid-Oceanic Ridges
(MORs), magma rises from the asthenosphere, solidifies, and forms a new oceanic crust. This new crust pushes the older crust
sideways—causing lateral movement of the sea floor. It is caused by mantle convection currents due to heat from Earth’s interior,
aided by gravity-induced slab pull and ridge push at spreading centers.


Geological       y Mid-Oceanic Ridges: Long, continuous submarine mountain ranges formed at divergent plate
Features           boundaries. Example: Mid-Atlantic Ridge, Carlsberg Ridge in the Indian Ocean.
                 y Rift Valleys: Narrow depressions at the crest of ridges due to crustal extension. Example:Þingvellir
                   (Thingvellir) National Park, Iceland is a visible example on land.
                 y Transform faults: Transform faults offset MORs and generate earthquakes, while fracture zones are
                   their inactive extensions
                 y Abyssal Plains: Broad, flat ocean floors far from ridges formed due to sediment accumulation on
                   older oceanic crust.
Evidence         y Magnetic Striping: Symmetrical magnetic polarity bands across Mid-Ocean Ridges (MORs) record
                   geomagnetic reversals, confirming continuous crust formation (Vine–Matthews Hypothesis).
                 y Radiometric Dating: Oceanic crust age increases away from ridges; youngest rocks at ridge crests,
                   oldest near continental margins (0–200 million years).
                 y Heat Flow Data: Highest geothermal heat flow at MORs indicates magma upwelling; heat flow
                   gradually decreases away from ridges.
                 y Seismicity Patterns: Earthquakes concentrated along ridges and transform faults, indicating active
                   crustal movement and seafloor spreading.
                 y Sediment Thickness: Thin sediments near ridges, thickening outward, supporting continuous crust
                   formation and outward plate movement.
Limitations      y Continental Features Ignored: Does not explain mountain building (Himalayas) or intraplate volcanism
                   such as Deccan Traps.
                 y Ocean-Focused Theory: Evidence largely from oceanic crust, limiting explanation of continental tectonic
                   processes.
                 y Asymmetrical Spreading: Some regions show uneven crustal spreading, challenging symmetrical
                   spreading assumption.
                 y Limited Exploration Data: Southern Ocean and deep oceans lack sufficient geological data to fully
                   validate spreading theory.
Significance     y Tectonic Activity: Spreading zones experience frequent earthquakes and volcanism, forming volcanic
                   islands such as Iceland and Barren Island.
                 y Crustal Age Variation: Explains why oceanic crust is younger (~200 million years) compared to
                   continental crust (up to 4 billion years).
                 y Resource Formation: Hydrothermal vents along MORs form polymetallic sulphides and support
                   chemosynthetic organisms, indicating future resource potential.
                 y Climatic Influence: Volcanic gas emissions, especially CO₂ from ridges, influence long-term climate
                   regulation and carbon cycle.
                 y Carbon Cycle Role: Faster spreading increases volcanic activity, injecting greenhouse gases into
                   atmosphere and affecting global climate.


3.3. Plate Tectonic Theory
Plate Tectonic Theory (1960s) explains the movement of Earth’s lithospheric plates over the semi-molten asthenosphere.
Driven by mantle convection, ridge push, and slab pull, it unifies continental drift and seafloor spreading theories. Plate
interactions at divergent, convergent, and transform boundaries shape major geological features like mountains, earthquakes,
and volcanoes.


                                                    Plate Boundaries
 Plate boundaries are the edges where two tectonic plates meet. The interaction between plates at these boundaries is
 responsible for most of the Earth’s major geological activities, including earthquakes, volcanoes, mountain formation, and
 seafloor spreading.

Types of Plate Boundary Interactions

      Divergent Boundary                         Convergent Boundary                       Transform Boundary


 y Plates move apart due to mantle          y Plates move towards each other.            y Plates slide past each other. No
   convection. Magma rises, cools, and        Depending on the crust type, one plate       crust is created or destroyed, but
   forms new crust, creating landforms        may subduct, forming trenches,               stress builds up, causing
   like mid-ocean ridges, and rift            volcanoes, volcanic islands or fold          frequent earthquakes.
   valleys.                                   mountains.                                 y Examples: San Andreas Fault,
 y Earthquakes here are usually shallow     y These zones are prone to strong              North Anatolian Fault.
   and mild, with moderate volcanic           earthquakes, volcanic eruptions, and
   activity.                                  tsunamis.
 y Examples: Mid-Atlantic Ridge, East       y Examples: Andes (Oceanic-
   African Rift, and Iceland.                 Continental), Himalayas (Continental-
                                              Continental), Mariana Trench, Japan
                                              Arc.


                                                    Evidence of theory
 y Paleozoic mountain belts of eastern North America and northwestern Europe match in structure, age, and
   stratigraphy, indicating once-connected landmasses.
 y Isotopic dating shows Precambrian rocks on opposite South Atlantic coasts share same age and composition,
   suggesting single continental origin.
 y Earthquakes and volcanoes are concentrated along plate boundaries, especially around the Ring of Fire, showing active
   plate interactions.
 y Magnetic stripes on the ocean floor are symmetrically arranged on both sides of mid-ocean ridges, proving seafloor
   spreading.
 y The oceanic crust is youngest at ridges and oldest near trenches, confirming new crust is formed and old crust is
   subducted.
 y Benioff zones (sloping zones of deep-focus earthquakes) prove that oceanic plates subduct into the mantle at convergent
   boundaries.
 y GPS measurements show that plates are still moving (such as Indian Plate moves ~5 cm/year northward), confirming
   ongoing tectonic activity.
 y Similar fossils, rock types, and mountain chains (e.g., Appalachians and Caledonides) on widely separated continents
   suggest they were once connected.

                                         Limitations of Plate Tectonic Theory
 y Early Earth Dynamics: Classical plate tectonics fails to explain Archean crust formation when higher heat flow and
   ductile lithosphere limited modern rigid plate boundaries.
 y Uniform Plate Boundary Assumption: Traditional model assumes uniform boundary behavior, but regions like North
   Anatolian Fault show complex micro-tectonic zones.
 y Crustal Heterogeneity Ignored: Rigid plate concept cannot explain lateral variations in crust thickness and
   composition shaped by rifting and basin formation.
 y Oversimplified Seismic Behaviour: Nearby fault segments show contrasting seismic behaviour—aseismic creep vs
   locked zones—controlled by local geology and fluid pressure.
 y Incomplete Fault Mechanics: Discovery of Episodic Tremor and Slip (ETS) challenges locked vs creeping fault model,
   showing complex intermittent stress release.


A. Africa’s Rift Valley (Divergent Plate Boundaries)
The East African Rift Valley is a major divergent plate boundary where the African continent is slowly splitting into the Nubian
Plate (west) and the Somalian Plate (east), a process that began around 22-25 million years ago.


                               Factors Responsible for East African Rift Valley Splitting

       Factor                              Description                                            Examples
 Divergent Plate        African Plate is splitting into the Nubian and           The African Plate is slowly splitting into two
 Boundary               Somalian Plates are slowly drifting apart, pulling the   at a rate of 6-7 mm/year.
                        crust in opposite directions.
 Mantle Plume           Upwelling of hot mantle material (plume) beneath         Afar Triple Junction is a hotspot where
 Activity               East Africa exerts upward pressure, causing crustal      mantle upwelling occurs beneath the rift
                        doming, thinning, and eventual fracturing →              zone.
                        encouraging rift formation.
 Magmatism and          Thinning crust allows magma upwelling, causing           Erta Ale (Danakil Depression, Afar Rift):
 Volcanism              volcanic eruptions, fissures, and deposition of molten   magma chambers feed persistent lava lake
                        material, further weakening continental crust.           through crustal fissures
 Crustal Faulting       Vertical movements and normal faulting along the rift    The Great Rift Valley features escarpments
 and Uplift             create grabens and horsts.                               and deep basins like Lake Tanganyika.

                                               Impact of the Plate Splitting

      Impact                               Description                                            Examples
 Volcanism &            Lithospheric stretching and faulting create zones of     Active volcanoes like Mount Nyiragongo
 Earthquakes            weakness, leading to frequent volcanic eruptions         (DRC), Erta Ale (Ethiopia); seismic zones like
                        and earthquakes.                                         Lake Tanganyika Rift, 2018 Kenya earth crack
 New Ocean              Continued rifting may allow seawater to flood            Afar region may become submerged; similar
 Formation              low-lying areas, forming a new ocean.                    to how the Red Sea formed.
 Geographical           East Africa may break off from the rest of the           Countries like Ethiopia, Kenya, Tanzania
 Change                 continent, forming an island continent.                  would lie on new coasts.
 Ecological             Unique landforms and isolation could promote new         Great Lakes Region (such as Lake Tanganyika
 Impact                 ecosystems and species evolution.                        and Malawi) rich in endemic fish species.
 Economic               New coastlines could boost trade, ports, fishing, and    Kenya’s Olkaria geothermal plants; potential
 Opportunities          marine resources.                                        future ports in Uganda, Zambia if coastlines
                                                                                 form.

B. Formation of Fold Mountains
Fold Mountains are large mountain ranges formed primarily by the collision of two tectonic plates, usually at convergent plate
boundaries. The immense compressional force at these boundaries causes the Earth’s crust to crumple and fold, pushing up layers
of rock into long, parallel mountain ranges.


    Stage                               Description                                            Example
Plate              Tectonic plates converge at plate boundaries,             The Himalayas formed when the Indian Plate
Collision          typically where an oceanic plate meets a continental      collided with the Eurasian Plate ~50 million
                   plate or two continental plates collide, initiating       years ago.
                   mountain formation.
Compression        Intense compressional forces cause the Earth's crust to   The folded Appalachian Mountains in North
and Buckling       buckle, folding sedimentary rock layers into anticlines   America, shaped by the collision of the North
                   (upward arches) and synclines (downward troughs).         American and African plates.
Sediment           Sediments like sandstone, limestone, and shale,           The Alps, where marine sedimentary rocks
Accumulation       deposited in ancient ocean basins, are compressed and     were folded during the African and Eurasian
                   folded due to their layered, pliable nature.              plate collision.
Thrust             Compressional forces cause rocks to fracture, forming     The Laramide Orogeny uplifted the Rocky
Faulting           thrust faults where older rock layers are pushed over     Mountains, characterized by thick-skinned
                   younger ones, adding structural complexity.               deformation, forcing basement rock upward
                                                                             along high-angle reverse and thrust faults.
Uplift and         Folding, faulting, and tectonic pressure uplift rock      The Andes, uplifted by the subduction of the
Mountain           layers, forming mountain ranges, later shaped by          Nazca Plate beneath the South American Plate.
Building           erosion.

                             Reasons for Location of Fold Mountains at Continental Margins

    Reason                                       Description                                           Example
Convergent Plate     Most fold mountains occur where continental and oceanic plates           Andes (Nazca Plate subducting
Boundaries           converge, leading to crustal compression and folding at the margins.     under South American Plate)
Presence of          Thick sedimentary deposits at continental margins (in geosynclines)      Himalayas (Tethys geosyncline
Geosynclines         are ideal for compression and folding during plate collision.            sediments folded)
                     Note: Modern academia uses terms "Foreland Basins" or
                     "Accretionary Prisms," in place of Geosynclines.
Subduction           Subduction of oceanic crust beneath continental crust causes             Rockies (Farallon Plate
Zones                immense compressive stress, folding sediments at the margin.             subduction)
Weakness Zones       Margins often have fractured or weaker crust, making them more           Appalachians (ancient margin
in Margins           prone to deformation and uplift during compressional tectonics.          zone)
Accumulation of      Rivers transport sediments from continental interiors and deposit them   Alps (sediment buildup
Sediments from       along the margins, adding material that can later be folded.             between African–Eurasian
Rivers                                                                                        Plates)
Tectonic             Margins experience long-term lateral compression due to ongoing          Zagros (compression from
Compression          plate movements, making them active mountain-building regions.           Arabian–Eurasian plate
Over Time                                                                                     collision)

                                        Major Mountain Ranges of the World
Major mountain ranges of
the world align
predominantly in east-
west, north-south, and
circum-Pacific patterns.
They are aligned along
tectonic plate boundaries.
They influence local
weather mainly through
orographic lift, rain
shadows, and wind
barriers, creating diverse
microclimates.


   Mountain                                                        Description
    Range
 The                y Extend about 2,500 km in an east–west arc across Asia; Syntaxial Bends at Nanga Parbat (West) and
 Himalayas            Namcha Barwa (East)
                    y Block frigid, dry winds from the Tibetan Plateau, moderating India's climate.
                    y Act as a barrier to monsoon winds, causing heavy rainfall in the Indian subcontinent.
                    y Their rain-shadow effect contributes to the formation of Taklamakan and Gobi deserts in Central Asia.
                    y Example: Heavy rainfall in Meghalaya (Mawsynram, Cherrapunji); cold deserts in Ladakh.
 The Alps           y Europe's highest mountain system, stretch 1,200 km east–west across eight countries including France,
                      Switzerland, and Italy.
                    y Influence precipitation patterns across Southern Europe and parts of Eurasia.
                    y Control local winds such as Foehn (warm, dry wind) and Mistral (cold, dry wind).
 The Atlas          y Extend southwest–northeast across Morocco, Algeria, and Tunisia; Separate the Mediterranean
                      region from the Sahara Desert.
                    y Moist Mediterranean winds cause high rainfall on northern slopes; Southern slopes experience
                      rain-shadow conditions, contributing to Sahara's aridity.
 The Andes          y Andes are the longest continental mountain range, extending north–south across seven South
                      American countries.
                    y The northern, eastern, and southwestern slopes receive heavy rainfall.
                    y The Andes create a rain-shadow effect, leading to the formation of the Atacama Desert, one of the
                      driest regions.
 The Rockies        y Extend from British Columbia (Canada) to New Mexico (USA).
                    y Influences the formation of warm Chinook winds (snow-eaters); intercept moist Pacific winds, causing
                      rainfall on windward slopes.
                    y The leeward side experiences rain-shadow deserts in southwestern North America.
                    y Example: Great Basin Desert (USA) formed due to rain shadow effect.
 The                y Run north–south in eastern North America.
 Appalachian        y Influence airflow and moderate continental air masses.
                    y Example: Eastern USA receives moderate rainfall and milder climate.
 The Urals          y Extend north–south for about 2,500 km between Europe and Asia.
                    y Act as a climatic divide, separating continental climates of Siberia from moderate European
                      climates.
                    y Westerly winds bring higher rainfall on western slopes, while eastern slopes remain drier.
                    y Example: Eastern Europe experiences milder winters compared to extremely cold Siberian regions.

C. Movement of Indian Plate
The Indian Plate, a part of the ancient Gondwana landmass, broke away and started drifting northwards around 120 million years
ago. Its rapid movement and collision with the Eurasian Plate have had major geological and climatic impacts, especially on the
Indian subcontinent and surrounding regions.


                         Tectonic Forces Driving the Northward Movement of the Indian Plate

        Factor                                   Description                                              Example
 Mantle Convection        Heat-driven convection currents in the mantle cause the         Drives the Indian plate at ~5 cm/year
 Currents                 Indian plate to drift northward, pushing against the            toward the Himalayas, causing continued
                          Eurasian plate.                                                 uplift.
 Slab Pull from           The Indo-Australian plate is subducting under the Sunda         Subduction near Java-Sumatra trench
 Subducting Plate         plate; gravity pulls the trailing Indian plate forward.         exerts forward pull on the Indian plate.
 Ridge Push from          Seafloor spreading at the Carlsberg Ridge exerts outward        Carlsberg Ridge in the Indian Ocean acts
 Mid-Ocean Ridge          force, pushing the Indian plate northward.                      as a tectonic push zone.
 Plate Tectonic           The Indian plate, once separated from Gondwana, retains         Initial high velocity (~20 cm/year)
 Momentum                 inertia and tectonic momentum, sustaining its                   slowed but still continues at ~5 cm/year.
                          movement northward.
 Collision with           Ongoing continental collision resists subduction, causing       Results in Himalayan uplift and
 Eurasian Plate           crustal compression, uplift, and lateral displacement of        earthquakes due to stress accumulation.
                          plates.
 Intra-plate Stress       Stresses from collision and uneven boundary forces cause        Linked to minor earthquakes in
 and Deformation          internal deformation, contributing to ongoing plate             Peninsular India and active faults in
                          motion.                                                         Himalayan region.

                                            Impacts of the Indian Plate Movement

    Impact Area                               Description                                              Example
 Frequent                  Ongoing convergence between Indian and                   2023 Sikkim Earthquake and regular tremors
 Earthquakes in            Eurasian plates causes stress accumulation,              in Uttarakhand highlight growing disaster
 Himalayan Belt            leading to high seismic activity.                        vulnerability.
 Increased                 Crustal instability from tectonic compression leads      Joshimath land subsidence (2023) was partly
 Landslides and            to landslides and ground sinking in fragile              linked to tectonic stress and infrastructure
 Subsidence                zones.                                                   overload.
 Shift in River            Plate motion alters slopes and gradients, causing        Kosi River has shifted courses multiple times,
 Courses                   river migration and increased flood risk in              earning the title "Sorrow of Bihar".
                           northern India.
 Glacial Retreat and       Tectonic uplift accelerates glacial melting,             Formation of GLOFs (Glacial Lake Outburst
 Lake Formation            forming unstable glacial lakes prone to outburst         Floods) in regions like Sikkim and Arunachal
                           floods.                                                  Pradesh.
 Geopolitical and          Plate boundaries often overlap with contested            India-China tensions in Ladakh are
 Border Instability        borders, heightening risks during disasters and          complicated by difficult terrain shaped by
                           strategic infrastructure development.                    ongoing tectonic activity.

D. India as a subcontinent
A subcontinent is a large, self-contained landmass that is part of a larger continent but is geographically isolated by natural features
such as mountains, seas, or plate boundaries. It also shows distinct cultural and environmental features.
India is considered a subcontinent because it is a large, distinct landmass with clear natural boundaries, major physical
diversity, and a strong cultural-historical identity within Asia. It is not a separate continent, but its size and uniqueness make
it “continent-like.”

     Reasons                                                           Description
 Geological            The region lies on the Indian Plate, which broke away from Gondwana and collided with the Eurasian
 History               Plate about 55 million years ago, creating the Himalayan mountain range.
 Geographical          Bounded by the Himalayas in the north, Hindu Kush and Arakan Yoma on the flanks, and the Indian
 Identity with         Ocean in the south (Arabian Sea & Bay of Bengal), isolating it from mainland Asia. The united landmass
 Natural               includes India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Nepal, Bhutan, Sri Lanka, and Maldives, forming a well
 Boundaries            defined region in South Asia.


 Climate               India experiences a wide range of climates, from tropical coastal climates to alpine conditions in the
 Distinctiveness       Himalayas and arid conditions in Rajasthan, producing rich biodiversity and different natural regions
                       within one landmass.
 Size and              With over 1.9 billion people, the subcontinent is larger than many continents and lies at the crossroads of
 Importance            Central, Southeast, and West Asia, making it important for trade, migration, and geopolitics.
 Cultural and          Despite many languages and religions, the region shares common roots through ancient civilizations,
 Historical Unity      historical empires (like Mauryas and Mughals), and similar cultural practices.

E. Formation of Islands in Indonesian and Philippine Archipelagos
The Indonesian and Philippine archipelagos are two of the largest island groups in the world. Indonesia has around 17,000 islands,
stretching between the Indian and Pacific Oceans. The Philippines includes over 7,600 islands, located in the western Pacific Ocean.
Both countries are located along the Pacific Ring of Fire, making them some of the most geologically active regions on Earth.
                       Process of formation of Islands in Indonesia and Philippine Archipelagoes

      Process                                  Description                                            Examples
 Plate Tectonics &        Oceanic plates push under other plates (subduction) at       Sunda Arc (Indonesia: oceanic-continental
 Subduction               convergent boundaries, forcing magma to rise and form        subduction), Luzon Trough/Philippine
                          curved chains of volcanic islands called island arcs.        Trench (Philippines: oceanic-oceanic).
 Volcanism                Repeated eruptions of magma build volcanic cones             Krakatoa Volcano formed new island
                          above sea level. These cones become volcanic islands.        Anak Krakatau.
                          Some form calderas after explosive eruptions.
 Folding and              Plate collisions cause uplift, folding, and faulting,        Sulawesi Island formed through complex
 Faulting                 creating fragmented landmasses and island                    tectonic uplift.
                          formation
 Coral Reef               Coral polyps grow in warm shallow seas around islands.       Raja Ampat (Indonesia), Palawan,
 Formation                Over time, they form fringing reefs, barrier reefs, or       Tubbataha Reefs (Philippines).
                          atolls on submerged volcanoes or island edges.
 Sedimentation            Rivers bring silt and sand to the sea. Over time, this       Mahakam Delta (Borneo coast,
                          material builds up and creates low-lying islands and         Indonesia), Cagayan Valley deltas
                          deltas near coastlines.                                      (Mindanao, Philippines).
 Location in Pacific      Region lies in Pacific Ring of Fire, with intense tectonic   Mount Mayon in Philippines.
 Ring of Fire             activity and island formation.
pyq: Discuss how the changes in shape and sizes of continents and ocean basins of the planet take place due to tectonic movements of the crustal masses. (2025)
Briefly mention the alignment of major mountain ranges of the world and explain their impact on local weather conditions, with examples. (2021)
Why is India considered as a subcontinent? Elaborate your answer. (2021)
Explain the formation of thousand of islands in Indonesian and Philippines archipelagos. (2014)
What do you understand by the theory of continental drift? Discuss the prominent evidence in its support. (2013)

chapters.text:
4               Rocks

                                                  UPSC Mains PYQs
      1.     Describe the characteristics and types of primary rocks. (2022)

Rocks are the solid, natural substances that make up the Earth’s crust. They are composed of one or more minerals and form the
foundation of all landforms. Rocks are classified based on their origin, composition, and formation process into igneous,
sedimentary, and metamorphic rocks.
Igneous rocks are classified as primary rocks because they are the original source from which all other rocks are derived. Secondary
rocks, which include sedimentary and metamorphic rocks, are formed through the transformation of these primary materials.


Characteristics of Different Types of Rocks
   Feature          Igneous Rocks (Primary)               Sedimentary Rocks                     Metamorphic Rocks
 Origin &          Solidification of magma/lava          Lithification: deposition       Metamorphism: transformation of
 Formation         (cooling)                             → compaction →                  existing rocks by heat, pressure, volume
                                                         cementation of sediments        (PVT) and chemicals (without melting)
 Nature &          Primary; crystalline, non-            Secondary; stratified           Secondary; foliated/banded, crystalline
 Structure         layered                               (layered), non-crystalline
 Types /           Intrusive cool slowly inside the      Clastic, chemical, organic;     Foliated (slate, gneiss); Non-foliated
 Texture           Earth (coarse-grained); Extrusive     generally granular              (marble); recrystallized
                   cool quickly on the surface
                   (fine/glassy)


 Fossils          Absent (high heat)                    Commonly present               Rare/absent (destroyed during
                                                                                       metamorphism)
 Permeability     Non-porous, impermeable; hard,        Often porous, permeable;       Low permeability; hard and compact
 & Hardness       dense                                 generally softer
 Examples         Granite (Intrusive), Basalt           Sandstone, Limestone,          Marble (from limestone), Slate(from
                  (Extrusive)                           Coal                           shale), Gneiss (from granite)

Types of Primary Rocks on the basis of Origin and Place of Cooling:
1. Plutonic (Intrusive) Rocks: These form when magma solidifies at great depth within the Earth's crust because they cool very
   slowly, they develop big-sized crystals and coarse grains (phaneritic -large crystals identifiable with an unaided human eye).
   Examples: Granite, Diorite, and Gabbro.
2. Volcanic (Extrusive) Rocks: These form when molten lava is poured out onto the surface and solidifies rapidly. This quick
   cooling prevents large crystallization, resulting in fine-grained, smooth textures (aphanitic). Example: Basalt
Major Intrusive Landforms (Plutonic Types): Magma frequently forces its way into planes of weakness in the crust, cooling into
distinct shapes:


   Batholiths: Huge, massive, and resistant upland masses of granite that represent the most spectacular intrusive landforms.
   Sills and Dykes: Sills are horizontal intrusions made along bedding planes, while Dykes are vertical, wall-like structures
    injected into cracks. Example: The Great Dyke (Zimbabwe)
   Laccoliths: Large, dome-shaped igneous mounds with a level base that arch up overlying sedimentary strata.
   Lopoliths: Igneous intrusions with a saucer shape, forming a shallow basin.
   Phacoliths: Lens-shaped masses that occupy the crest of an anticline or the bottom of a syncline.

Types of Sedimentary Rocks
       Type                      Mode of Formation                                  Characteristic Examples
 Mechanically         Derived from the accumulation of materials       Sandstone (often quartz fragments), Conglomerate
 Formed               from other rocks that have been cemented         (rounded pebbles), Breccia (angular fragments),
                      together.                                        Shale, Mudstone, and Loess (wind-deposited).
 Organically          Formed from the remains of living organisms      Limestone and Chalk (calcareous); Peat, Lignite, and
 Formed               (corals, shells) or vegetative matter (swamps,   Coal (carbonaceous/vegetative matter).
                      forests).
 Chemically           Precipitated chemically from mineral-rich        Rock Salt (Halite), Gypsum (calcium sulphate),
 Formed               solutions in seas or lakes.                      Potash, Nitrates, and Chert.


Significance of Sedimentary Rocks
    Energy Reservoirs: They are the primary source of petroleum, natural gas, and coal. Petroleum is formed from the decay
     of tiny marine organisms trapped in suitable sedimentary structures.
    Source of fossil records: They are the primary source of fossil records-because they form at low temperatures and
     pressures.
    Mineral Wealth: They contain valuable deposits like Hematite (iron ore), phosphates, and building stones.
    Industrial Feedstock: Limestone is a ubiquitous resource used extensively in the cement, building, and agricultural
     industries.
    Agricultural Base: Weathered sedimentary rocks yield some of the world's richest soils, which are vital for food security.

Types of Metamorphic Rocks
        Type of                   Key                                             Description
    Metamorphism                 Driver
Thermal (Contact)          High Temperature        Occurs when rocks come into contact with hot magma, gases, or are
                                                   subjected to intense geothermal heat.
                                                   y

                                                   y

                                                   y
Dynamic                    High Pressure           Results from the stress of high pressure, often accompanied by high
                                                   temperatures and chemically charged water, particularly during intense
                                                   earth movements.
                                                   y


Significance of Metamorphic Rocks
    Economic Value: These rocks are highly prized as building materials; marble is used for monuments and sculpture,
     while slate is widely used for roofing and flooring.
    Resistance: Metamorphic rocks like quartzite are extremely hard and resistant to weathering, often forming the "backbone"
     of high mountain peaks, such as Gunong Tahan in Malaysia.
pyq: Describe the characteristics and types of primary rocks. (2022)

chapters.text:
5               Landforms and their Evolution

                                                 UPSC Mains PYQs
      1.    How are the fjords formed? Why do they constitute some of the most picturesque areas
            of the world? (2023)
      2.    There is no formation of deltas by rivers of the Western Ghat. Why? (2013)

5.1. Glacial Landforms
Glacial landforms are shaped by glacial erosion and deposition, forming distinct features like cirques, fjords, moraines, and
drumlins in cold regions. They are evidence of past glaciation and help in paleoclimate reconstruction, , such as glacial boulders
in Talcher, Odisha, indicating ancient glacial activity.


                   Erosional Landforms                                            Depositional Landforms
 y Cirques (Corries): Steep-sided, bowl-shaped basins with          y Moraines: Accumulated glacial till (unsorted debris)
   concave walls, formed by headward erosion at glacier               deposited along sides (lateral), middle (medial), or end
   source; may contain tarn lakes.                                    (terminal) of a glacier.
 y Horns: Sharp, pyramid-shaped peaks formed when three             y Eskers: Long, winding ridges of sand and gravel
   or more cirques erode a mountain from different directions         deposited by subglacial meltwater streams inside ice
   (such as, Matterhorn).                                             tunnels.
 y Aretes: Narrow, knife-edged ridges between two cirques           y Outwash Plains: Flat areas formed by glacio-fluvial
   or glacial valleys, formed by headward erosion.                    deposits (gravel, silt, sand, clay) carried by meltwater
 y Fjords: Deep, U-shaped glacial troughs submerged by                beyond terminal moraines.
   rising sea levels, forming steep-sided inlets (e.g., Norway,     y Drumlins: Elongated, teardrop-shaped hills formed
   New Zealand).                                                      under glacial ice movement; composed of till and aligned
                                                                      in glacier flow direction.

A. Fjords
Fjords are long, narrow, and deep sea inlets with steep, U-shaped cliffs, formed by glacial erosion during ice ages, often
characterised by a shallow mouth (threshold–a terminal moraine left by the retreating glacier) and deep inner basins. They
are picturesque due to the contrast between calm waters, towering mountains, and waterfalls. Examples include Norway’s
Sognefjord and Greenland’s coastline.


Image: The different stages of fjord formation: a) Glacial valley b) U-shaped valley (trough-shaped profile) after glacier
melt c) Fluvioglacial sedimentation d) Sea level rise and valley submersion.

                                                    Process of Formation
 Glacial           During Ice Ages, large valley glaciers moved through coastal mountains, eroding deep U-shaped valleys by
 Erosion           plucking and abrasion. Example: Sognefjord, Norway
 Isostatic         After the glaciers melted, the heavy ice load was removed, and the Earth's crust started to rise (rebound).
 Rebound           However, the valleys remained over-deepened. Example: Drammensfjorden, Norway
 Modification      The weight and slow movement of the glacial mass blunted former interlocking spurs into truncated spurs,
 of Relief         resulting in a characteristic overdeepened, steep-sided, U-shaped valleys.
 Post-Glacial      Global melting of glaciers raised sea levels, which flooded the deep glacial valleys, turning them into
 Sea Rise          fjords. Example: Milford Sound, New Zealand
 Tectonic          In some regions, crustal movements (subsidence or faulting) helped lower the valley floor, allowing more
 Subsidence        seawater to enter. Example: Fjords in Alaska

5.2. Fluvial Landforms
Fluvial landforms are created by the erosional and depositional action of rivers and streams, shaping valleys, terraces, fans, and
deltas.They indicate river evolution stages, affect soil fertility, support agriculture and settlements, and help understand drainage
patterns and landscape development over time.


                     Erosional Landforms                                            Depositional Landforms
 y Waterfalls: Sudden vertical fall of river over steep slope,        y Alluvial Fans: Fan-shaped deposits formed when
   typically formed where hard rock overlies soft rock in               streams descend from hills and spread sediments on
   youthful stage.                                                      flatter plains.
 y Gorges: Straight, wall-like sides; narrower compared to its        y Natural Levees: Low ridges of coarse material formed
   depth; formed through rapid erosion in resistant rock; Indus         along riverbanks due to repeated flooding and
   Gorge (Ladakh); Brahmaputra Gorge (Namche Barwa).                    sediment deposition.
 y Canyons: Steep, step-like side slopes; typically wider at the      y Point Bars: Sediment accumulations on the inner
   top than at its bottom; Gandikota Canyon (Pennar River),             bends of meandering rivers, formed due to slower
   Grand Canyon (Colorado River)                                        water velocity.
 y River Valleys: Elongated lowlands formed between hills/            y Meanders: Loop-like river bends formed in mature
   mountains, shaped by downcutting and lateral erosion of              stage due to lateral erosion and deposition
   flowing water.                                                       (composite) on gentle slopes.
 y Potholes: Circular holes in riverbeds formed by abrasion as        y Oxbow Lakes: Cut-off meander loops formed when
   swirling water spins sediments in depressions.                       the river creates a new straight course, leaving behind
 y River Terraces: Step-like surfaces representing former               curved lakes.
   floodplain levels, formed due to vertical erosion into older       y Deltas: Triangular-shaped landforms at river mouths
   alluvium.                                                            formed by sediment deposition into lakes or seas (such
                                                                        as , Ganga Delta).

A. Formation of Duns
Duns are elongated structural valleys found between the Siwalik Hills and Lesser Himalayas, filled with alluvium. Notable
examples include Dehradun (Uttarakhand), Patli Dun (Himachal Pradesh), and Udhampur Dun (Jammu).
 Stages of         y Tectonic Collision: Collision of Indian and Eurasian plates→ Forms main Himalayan ranges (Greater,
 Formation           Lesser Himalayas).
                   y Shiwalik Uplift: Younger Shiwaliks rise later from thick Tertiary sediments → Creates barrier between
                     Lesser Himalayas & emerging Shiwaliks.
                   y River Blockage: Rivers from Higher Himalayas get dammed by rising Shiwaliks→ Temporary lakes form
                     in structural basins (synclinal valleys).
                   y Sediment Deposition: Rivers deposit massive alluvium (boulders, gravel, sand, silt) into lakes→Lakes fill
                     gradually with thick alluvial fans & debris.
                   y Valley Formation: Rivers erode softer Shiwalik rocks, breaching the dam→ Results in flat-floored,
                     alluvium-filled valleys called Duns.
 Significance      y Agricultural Value: Fertile alluvial soils support intensive farming, especially rice and wheat. Example:
                     Doon Basmati rice from Dehradun
                   y Human Settlements: Flat terrain and water availability attract urban and rural settlements. Examples:
                     Cities like Dehradun and Udhampur.
                   y Strategic Importance: Located in ecologically sensitive and geopolitically crucial Himalayan zones.
                   y Ecological Role: Act as buffer zones absorbing floods and maintaining biodiversity. Examples: Bugyals
                     around duns aid local ecology.
                   y Tourism Potential: Scenic beauty, rivers, and forests attract eco-tourism and religious tourism. Example:
                     Mussoorie-Dehradun circuit.

B. Deltas and Estuaries
Deltas
A delta is a fan-shaped or triangular alluvial tract formed at the mouth of a river where it empties into sea or lake. It represents
the seaward extension of a flood-plain, acting as a critical aggradational landform where the river’s constructive work reaches its
peak.
  Dimension                                                          Description
 Conditions         y Active Denudation: High rates of vertical and lateral erosion in the upper course.
 for Formation      y Tideless/Sheltered Coast: Strong tides or waves would disperse the sediment before it can accumulate.
 of Deltas          y Shallow Continental Shelf: Adjoining seas must be shallow to allow sub-aqueous buildup.
                    y Absence of Filtering Lakes: Large lakes in the river’s path act as "sediment traps".
                    y Weak Lateral Currents: Strong ocean currents running at right angles to the mouth wash away the
                      deposition.


 Process of         y Velocity Reduction: River's load-bearing capacity falls with drop in gradient
 Formation          y Bifurcation: Accumulated alluvium breaks the river into multiple distributaries.
                    y Stratification of Beds: top-set beds (coarse materials), fore-set beds (intermediate), and bottom-set beds
                      (finest particles carried farthest)
                    y Examples: River Po in Italy massive sediment deposition; Ganges-Brahmaputra Arcuate Delta:
                      Largest in the world
 Types              y Arcuate: Fan-shaped spread of distributaries; Nile, Ganga-Brahmaputra (Sundarbans)
                    y Bird’s Foot: Elongated distributaries into the sea→ Bird’s Claws; Mississippi
                    y Cuspate: Pointed projections into the sea with powerful wave projections → prevents spread of multiple
                      distributaries; Tiber (Italy)
                    y Lobate: Arcuate with more irregular, lobe-like extensions, river dominates slightly over wave action;
                      (Rajahmundry) Godavari.
 Significance       y Agricultural: “Granaries of the world" due to their thick mantles of fertile alluvial soil; Jute in Ganges
                      delta + Cotton in Nile delta
                    y Economic Hubs: Support major ports and industrial centers; Calcutta and New Orleans
                    y Logistical: Heavy silting makes deltas less suitable than estuaries for ports → require dredging; Kolkata
                      Port: Sediment obstruction in navigation
                    y Ecological Signatures: Represent the mineral composition of source rocks and act as biodiversity
                      hotspots; Sundarbans: Mangrove ecosystem within the Ganges-Brahmaputra delta.

Estuaries
An estuary is a semi-enclosed coastal body of water where freshwater from a river mixes with oceanic saline water, creating a unique
brackish water zone. They are funnel-shaped drowned river mouths characteristic of coastlines of submergence. Unlike deltas,
which are prograding depositional landforms, estuaries represent a high-energy transition zone where marine processes often
dominate over fluvial deposition.
 Mechanism of       y Submergence: During the post-glacial period, rising sea levels "drowned" the lower courses of river
 Formation            valleys, creating long, narrow marine inlets.
                    y Lack of Progradation: They form when the river’s ability to transport sediment is surpassed by the sea’s
                      ability to remove it, or when the river gradient is so steep that it carries its load directly into deep water
                      without coastal accumulation.
 Significance       y Nutrient Traps: Tidal currents transport nitrogen and phosphorus from the ocean, while rivers bring
                      organic matter, creating a highly productive environment.
                    y Breeding Grounds: Estuaries serve as vital spawning and feeding grounds for diverse marine species,
                      supported by mangrove ecosystems (Littoral/Swamp forests) that act as natural filters.
                    y Tidal Bores: In narrow, shallow estuaries, incoming tides can form a wall of water called a Tidal Bore
                      that surges upstream against the river flow. Example: The Hooghly River in India and the Qiantang River
                      in China.

     Feature                              Estuary                                                  Delta
 Primary Process       Marine dominance / Erosion / Submergence.         Fluvial dominance / Aggradation / Deposition.
 Sedimentation         Entrances are often sand and silt-free.           Characterized by heavy silting and distributaries.
 Motive Force          High energy, strong tides, and steep gradients.   Low energy, tideless coasts, and shallow shelves.
 Navigation            Ideal for deep-water ports; Thames                Requires continuous, expensive dredging.
                       (London), Hudson (New York)

Estuaries and Deltas are not merely "mouths of rivers" but are dynamic transition zones that bridge continental and marine
systems. They support maritime transportation which contribute 95% of India's foreign trade aiding India's Blue Economy
. However, fulfilling SDG 14 (Life Below Water) requires moving beyond exploitation toward an Integrated Coastal Zone
Management (ICZM) approach that balances port development with ecological preservation.

5.3. Coastal Landforms
Coastal landforms are shaped by wave, tide, wind, and current action, forming features like cliffs, beaches, spits, bars, and
lagoons.They influence coastal ecosystems, human settlement, navigation, and tourism, and help in understanding sea-level
changes and coastal geomorphology over time.


                    Erosional Landforms                                          Depositional Landforms
                      (By Wave Action)                                         (By Wave & Tidal Deposition)
y Cliffs: Steep rock faces formed by continuous wave erosion at       y Beaches: Gently sloping accumulations of sand,
  the base, leading to undercutting and eventual collapse of            shingle, or pebbles, formed by wave deposition
  overlying material.                                                   along the shoreline.
y Wave-cut Platforms: Flat, bench-like surfaces formed at the         y Spits: Narrow ridges of sand/shingle projecting from
  base of cliffs due to wave abrasion during high tide.                 land into the sea, formed by longshore drift and
y Sea Arches: Natural arches formed when waves erode through            deposition.
  a headland, creating a hollowed arch-like structure.                y Bars: Formed when a spit extends across a bay,
y Sea Caves: Hollow features at the cliff base created by               connecting two headlands and enclosing a lagoon
  hydraulic action and abrasion, especially along fault lines or        behind it.
  weak rocks.                                                         y Lagoons: Shallow water bodies formed behind
y Stacks: Isolated vertical columns of rock left behind after the       bars, which may gradually fill with sediment and
  collapse of sea arches due to continuous wave erosion.                become marshes.

A. Straits and Isthmus in International Trade

                                                  Straits and Isthmus
y Definition: A strait is a narrow body of water connecting two larger bodies of water, while an isthmus is a narrow strip of
  land connecting two larger landmasses. Geographically, they are "converses" of one another: one is a water bridge, and the
  other is a land bridge.
y Characteristics: Straits and isthmuses facilitate maritime trade and strategic connectivity; straits often act as choke
  points with strong currents, while isthmuses enable canal construction, reducing sea travel distance, such as the Suez
  Canal and Strait of Hormuz.

                                                       Significance

    Aspect                               Description                                            Examples
Reduced              Straits and isthmuses shorten trade routes, lowering     Suez Canal avoids circumnavigation of Africa
Distance             time and cost of shipping.                               for Europe-Asia trade.
Ports and            Natural ports at straits and isthmuses enhance           Malacca Strait hosts major ports like
Harbors              trade infrastructure.                                    Singapore.
Trade                Key routes handle significant global trade, boosting     Suez Canal and Bab el-Mandeb handle around
Volume               economic efficiency.                                     12% of global trade.
Resource             Facilitate transport of critical resources like oil      Strait of Hormuz links West Asian oil to global
Transport            and gas.                                                 markets.
Choke                Control over straits can influence global trade and      Strait of Malacca’s “Malacca Dilemma” for
Points               supply chains.                                           China.
Geopolitical         Nations controlling straits/isthmuses gain strategic     Iran’s control over Strait of Hormuz.
Leverage             advantages.
Security             Straits protect trade routes from piracy and ensure      The Strait of Gibraltar is a strategic maritime
Concerns             maritime security.                                       gateway.


                                                  Environmental Impacts

      Aspect                               Description                                             Examples
 Ecological            Straits and isthmuses are biodiversity hotspots,          Suez Canal region hosts fragile marine
 Fragility             vulnerable to trade-related impacts.                      ecosystems.
 Pollution             Shipping increases oil spills and marine debris,          Strait of Hormuz often faces oil spill risks from
 Risks                 harming ecosystems.                                       tankers.
 Sustainable           Environmentally friendly shipping reduces carbon          Green shipping initiatives in Malacca Strait.
 Shipping              footprint.
 Climate Change        Melting Arctic ice alters straits’ roles, affecting       Bering Strait’s increased use due to ice melt
 Impacts               ecosystems.                                               impacts Arctic species.

B. Strait of Hormuz Crisis and Implications for India’s Energy Security
The Strait of Hormuz has emerged as a critical flashpoint in 2026 amid escalating Iran-Israel tensions, Houthi Red Sea disruptions,
and Iran's threats to close the world's most vital oil chokepoint.


 Significance     y It carries oil from Gulf states such as Iran, Iraq, Kuwait, Qatar, Saudi Arabia and the UAE → ~ 20%
                    global LNG + 20% the world’s crude oil trade
                  y One-third of the world's seaborne fertilizer trade normally passes through the strait.
                  y Imports to the Middle East, including food, medicines and technological supplies.
 Strategic        y At its narrowest, it's just 33 km wide with dual shipping lanes (2-3 miles each direction). ~150 km long
 Nature             with no significant bypass routes. Its narrow bathymetry amplifies geopolitical leverage → a single
                    blockade could halt 30,000 tankers annually carrying $1 trillion in energy trade.
 Reasons          y US-Iran Nuclear Deal failure: Trump withdrew from JCPOA nuclear deal (2018) + oil sanctions cut Iran
 for crisis         exports → Iran responds with increased nuclear enrichment; failure of Geneva Talks (2026)
                  y Iran-Israel tensions: Ideological and historical tensions + Regional Power Rivalry
                  y Shadow Proxy Wars: Hezbollah (Lebanon), Houthis (Yemen), Hamas (Gaza) attacks escalate regional
                    tensions
                  y Strategic Island Control: Iran consolidates Abu Musa + Tunbs islands (1992 occupation); deploys
                    Ghader missiles threatening entire Strait


 Implications    y India imports about 60% of its LPG consumption and out of these imports about 90% come through the
 for India         Strait of Hormuz
                 y 25% of the Natural Gas procurement has been affected
                 y 45% of crude oil used to be transported earlier via strait→ with post crisis adjustments and diversification,
                   it had reduced to 30%.
 Alternatives    y Saudi's East-West Pipeline operated by Aramco/ Petroline → Abqaiq oil processing centre to the Yanbu
                   port on the Red Sea
                 y UAE’s Abu Dhabi Crude Oil Pipeline/ Habshan-Fujairah pipeline → Abu Dhabi (UAE) to the port of
                   Fujairah on the Gulf of Oman.
