chapters.text:
5              Deep Ocean Water Circulation

Deep Ocean Water Circulation, also known as thermohaline circulation, is the movement of dense water masses in the deep ocean,
driven by differences in temperature and salinity.


                                    Formation of Deep Ocean Water Circulation
 y Cooling and Salinity Increase: In polar regions, seawater cools significantly while sea ice formation increases salinity,
   making water denser.
 y Density Increase and Sinking: The cold, saline water becomes denser than surrounding water and sinks to the ocean
   depths, initiating descent.
 y Replacement by Surface Water: Sinking water pulls surface waters inward, which then cool and increase salinity,
   continuing the sinking process.
 y Formation of Deep Currents: This continuous sinking generates deep ocean currents that flow along basins, forming the
   global conveyor belt.
 y Global Overturning Circulation: These deep currents move cold water equatorward while surface currents transport
   warm water poleward, balancing heat distribution.
 y Nutrient and Gas Transport: Deep circulation circulates nutrients, oxygen, and carbon dioxide, sustaining marine life
   and regulating global ecosystems.

                                     Importance of Deep Ocean Water Circulation

      Aspect                                 Description                                          Example
 Heat                   Deep ocean circulation transfers warm surface waters       Gulf Stream and North Atlantic Drift keep
 Redistribution         toward higher latitudes and returns cold waters            Western Europe warmer than similar
                        equatorward, helping regulate global climate patterns.     latitudes in Canada.
 Carbon Storage         Deep circulation transports dissolved carbon dioxide       North Atlantic Deep Water formation
 and Climate            from surface waters to the deep ocean, acting as a         stores atmospheric CO₂ in deep ocean
 Regulation             long-term carbon sink and moderating climate change.       layers for centuries.
 Marine                 Upwelling associated with deep circulation brings          Peru–Chile coast upwelling supports one
 Productivity           nutrient-rich deep water to the surface, supporting        of the world's richest fisheries.
                        plankton growth and marine food chains.
 Global Ocean           Thermohaline circulation connects major oceans and         Global conveyor belt links Atlantic,
 Circulation            helps maintain large-scale ocean current systems           Indian, and Pacific Oceans influencing
 Control                affecting climate and weather patterns.                    global climate.
 Oxygen                 Deep water formation carries oxygen-rich surface water     Antarctic Bottom Water supplies oxygen
 Distribution in        to ocean depths, supporting deep-sea organisms and         to deep Pacific Ocean basins.
 Deep Ocean             maintaining marine ecosystems.


5.1 Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC)
The Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC) is a vast oceanic system that carries warm surface waters from the tropics
to higher northern latitudes while returning cold, deep waters from the North Atlantic toward the equator. This circulation is vital
for global heat redistribution, shaping both regional and global climate patterns and significantly moderating temperatures across
Europe, North America, and equatorial regions.


                                       Fig: Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation


                                                  Factors Affecting AMOC
 y Temperature Changes: Cooling increases water density and promotes sinking, while warming reduces density,
   weakening the overturning. A warming atmosphere also inhibits sinking, weakening AMOC.
 y Salinity Changes: Higher salinity in the tropical Atlantic due to less rainfall can intensify AMOC temporarily by
   aiding sinking.
 y Wind Patterns: Westerly winds, especially over the Southern Ocean, influence ocean mixing, Ekman transport, and
   upwelling, affecting AMOC strength indirectly.
 y Freshwater Influx: Melting Arctic and Greenland ice sheets add freshwater, lowering salinity and density, slowing
   downwelling.
 y Increased Precipitation: Increased precipitation over North Atlantic reduces salinity and weakens AMOC.


                                      Potential Impacts of AMOC Weakening
y Reduced Marine Productivity: Weaker vertical mixing limits nutrient upwelling, decreasing phytoplankton growth and
  impacting marine food chains and fisheries.
y Severe Winter in Europe: North-Western Europe could experience significant cooling and more intense winter extremes.
y Altered Rainfall Patterns: Weakening AMOC can shift tropical rainfall belts, reducing precipitation in parts of Africa,
  South Asia, and Amazon regions.
y Ice Melt: Greenland and Arctic ice melt may accelerate, increasing freshwater input into the ocean and further amplifying
  AMOC weakening.
y Monsoon Weakening: Reduced ocean-atmosphere heat exchange may weaken South Asian and West African monsoons,
  affecting agricultural productivity and water availability.
y Increased Climate Variability: AMOC weakening may alter storm tracks, intensify weather variability, and increase
  frequency of extreme climatic events.

6               Atlantification

Atlantification refers to the increasing influence of warmer, saltier Atlantic water on the Arctic Ocean. This process involves the
northward movement of Atlantic water, particularly through the Barents Sea and Fram Strait, leading to a warming of Arctic waters
and a reduction in sea ice cover.


                                                 Drivers of Atlantification
 y Global Warming: Rise in global temperatures→ the Atlantic currents flowing into the Arctic (particularly through the
   Barents Sea and the Fram Strait) are carrying significantly warmer water and at higher volumes than in the past.
 y Sea Ice Decline: Reduced ice cover allows more solar radiation absorption, warming surface waters and weakening the
   halocline → reduces barrier to heat from warm Atlantic water, increasing surface warming.
 y Breaching of the Arctic Halocline: Arctic Surface Mixed Layer (ASML) due to melting and mixing → Warm saline
   waters are increasingly reaching upper layers
 y Atmospheric Circulation: Arctic Dipole and Arctic Oscillation modulate wind patterns, influencing Atlantic water
   inflow. Positive Arctic Dipole (high pressure over North America, low over Eurasia) specifically accelerates Atlantic water
   transport.
 y Ocean Circulation: Enhanced cyclonic circulation in the Eurasian Basin increases warm water inflow.
 y Winter-Heat Flux: Atlantification is not constant; it is most intense during the winter, when sea ice formation should be at
   its peak but is instead inhibited by Atlantic heat.

                                                Impacts of Atlantification
 y Accelerated Ice Melt: Warm Atlantic water breaches the protective halocline, melting sea ice from below and triggering the
   ice-albedo feedback loop, causing rapid polar warming.
 y Marine Borealization: Atlantic species migrate northward, outcompeting native Arctic fauna like Polar cod and disrupting
   the traditional food web and local indigenous subsistence fishing.
 y Weather Pattern Instability: A warming Arctic reduces the temperature gradient with lower latitudes, causing the Jet
   Stream to become erratic, leading to extreme global weather.
 y Methane Hydrate Release: Warming seafloor temperatures may destabilize subsea permafrost and methane hydrates,
   potentially releasing potent greenhouse gases into the atmosphere, accelerating climate change.
 y Salinity Increase: Cold freshwater halocline (earlier insulating ice) disrupted → upward mixing of saline Atlantic water →
   increased surface salinity. Eurasian Basin has shown salinification since 2000.


y Positive Albedo Feedback: The loss of highly reflective sea ice exposes darker ocean waters, increasing solar heat
  absorption and triggering a self-reinforcing cycle of rapid melting.
y Arctic Amplification: The Arctic is now warming up to four times faster than the global average, destabilizing the polar
  vortex and causing erratic jet streams.
y Altered Carbon Cycling: The shift from ice algae to open-ocean phytoplankton, alongside rising temperatures,
  fundamentally alters the biological pump, potentially reducing the Arctic's carbon sink efficiency.

7            Ocean–Atmosphere Interactions

                                               UPSC Mains PYQs
     1.    Most of the unusual climatic happenings are explained as an outcome of the El-Nino
           effect. Do you agree? (2014)

7.1 ENSO Cycle
The El Niño–Southern Oscillation (ENSO) is a coupled ocean–atmosphere phenomenon that involves changes in sea surface
temperatures (SSTs) and trade winds in the tropical Pacific Ocean. It represents a significant form of interannual variability,
creating global teleconnections that disrupt typical weather patterns across continents, especially the Indian monsoon. Climate
change may increase the intensity of extreme ENSO events, though long-term frequency changes remain uncertain.

                                                     The Components
 Oceanic Component         Sea Surface Temperature anomalies in central & eastern Pacific.
 Atmospheric               Southern Oscillation (SO) – atmospheric pressure see-saw between Tahiti (Eastern Pacific) and
 Component                 Darwin (Western Pacific).
                           SOI (Southern Oscillation Index) → Negative: El Niño; Positive: La Niña

                                                     Normal Condition
 y Strong Trade Winds blow from east to west, piling up a "warm pool" of water near Indonesia (Western Pacific). This
   causes low pressure and heavy convection in the west, while the Eastern Pacific (off Peru) experiences cold water
   upwelling, supporting rich marine ecosystems.
 y Pacific-North American (PNA) pattern acts as the primary atmospheric bridge (teleconnection) carrying ENSO signals to
   higher latitudes


                                                      Phases of ENSO

      Feature                    El Niño (Warm Phase)                                 La Niña (Cool Phase)
 SST Pattern           Abnormal Warming in central/eastern Pacific        Abnormal cooling in central/eastern Pacific
                       (Eastern Pacific/Modoki variants)
 Winds                 Weakening/reversal of Trade Winds                  Strengthening of Trade Winds
 Convection            Shifts to central/eastern Pacific → rainfall in    Concentrated in W. Pacific → rainfall in Indonesia/
                       Peru; poor monsoon and droughts in India.          Australia; good monsoon rainfall in India.
 Ocean Dynamics        Suppressed upwelling → nutrient decline            Enhanced upwelling → nutrient-rich waters
 Marine Impact         Fish mortality, coral bleaching                    Boosted fisheries productivity


 Climate Change           Projected to cause more frequent extreme El          Can lead to rare "Triple Dip" events (persisting for
                          Niño events, causing global warming spikes           three years), as seen in 2020–2023.
 Example                  The 2015–16 El Niño led to one of the driest         The 2020–21 La Niña brought above-average
                          monsoon seasons in India.                            monsoon rains.

Super El Nino
The 2026 Super El Niño represents a "Climate Black Swan" event, characterized by an unprecedented spike in Sea Surface
Temperatures (SSTs) in the Central and Eastern Pacific. Surpassing the "Very Strong" threshold of +2.5°C, this event is being termed
a "Triple-Whammy" due to its interaction with record-high global ocean heat content and the ongoing positive phase of the
Interdecadal Pacific Oscillation (IPO).

                                         Indicative Signs and Factors Responsible
 Weakening of Trade           Weakening of easterly trade winds allows Westerly Wind Bursts (WWBs) to push the "Warm Pool"
 Winds                        from the Western Pacific toward the South American coast.
 Kelvin Wave                  Downwelling Kelvin waves traveled eastward across the Pacific, deepening the thermocline and
 Propagation                  suppressing the upwelling of cold, nutrient-rich water.
 Bjerknes feedback            A positive ocean-atmosphere coupling where weakened trade winds reduce cold water upwelling in
                              the eastern Pacific, warming sea surface temperatures (SSTs).
 The Global Heat              According to WMO (2026), 90% of excess heat from global warming is stored in oceans. This
 Surge                        "Ocean Heat Content" (OHC) acted as a multiplier effect, fueling the intensity of the El Niño.
 Polar Vortex                 A weakened Jet Stream allowed warm air to penetrate the Arctic, further disrupting global pressure
 Instability                  belts.
 Interdecadal Pacific         In this 15–30 year climate cycle, a positive IPO phase boosts tropical Pacific warmth, amplifying El
 Oscillation (IPO)            Niño events and increasing the likelihood of extreme global temperatures and weather shifts.

                                                      Impacts of ENSO cycle

      Impacts                                        Description                                           Examples

                                                          Global Impacts
 Floods during El           Eastward shift of warm water and convection brings heavy           1997–98 El Niño caused severe
 Niño                       rainfall and flooding in western coasts of South America and       floods in Peru and California.
                            North America.
 Droughts during El         Suppression of rainfall in the western Pacific due to subsiding    1982–83 El Niño caused a massive
 Niño                       air and high pressure leads to droughts in Australia,              drought in Australia.
                            Indonesia, southern Africa.
 Winter                     El Niño leads to milder winters in Canada and northern             1997-98 El Niño mild winter in
 Temperature                Europe; La Niña causes colder winters in North America and         Northern America.
 Variations                 northern Asia.
 Tropical                   El Niño reduces hurricanes in the Atlantic (due to high            Fewer hurricanes in the Atlantic
 Cyclones                   vertical wind shear); La Niña increases them.                      during El Niño years like 2015.
 Ocean & Fisheries          El Niño suppresses upwelling of nutrient-rich water along          Anchovy fisheries collapsed in Peru
 Impact                     Peru and Ecuador, damaging fisheries.                              during El Niño 1982–83.

                                                          Impacts on India

     Impact                               El Niño                                                La Niña
 Monsoon                y Weakens southwest monsoon, often               y Strengthens monsoon, leading to above-normal
 Rainfall                 causing droughts.                                rainfall.
                        y Example: 2015–16 severe rainfall deficit.      y Example: 2020–21 good monsoon season.
 Agriculture            y It leads to crop failure, especially in        y Supports healthy crop growth and better agricultural
                          kharif season.                                   output and boosting rural economy.
                        y Example: 2014–15 El Niño affected              y Example: 2020–21 registered record rice/wheat
                          pulses and oilseed output.                       output.


 Temperature          y Causes hotter summers and warmer             y Can bring cold waves during winter in North and
                        winters, particularly in northern India.       Northwest India.
                      y Example: 2015 summer heatwave (2,500         y Example: Colder-than-usual winters across Punjab,
                        deaths).                                       Delhi, and Bihar during 2020–21 La Niña.
 Disasters            y Increases risk of droughts, heatwaves,       y Increases risk of floods and landslides, especially in
                        and water scarcity.                            North East and Himalayan regions.
                      y Example: Marathwada drought.                 y Example: Assam 2020 floods
A Super El Niño–Southern Oscillation acts as a Systemic Multiplier of socio-economic instability. It triggers Agricultural
Contraction, causing Food Inflation and Stagflationary Pressures amid Water and Energy Shortages that suppress industrial
and rural demand. It accelerates the Spread of Vector-Borne Diseases such as Dengue fever and Malaria, weakens the ocean’s
Carbon Sink Function, and intensifies Coral Bleaching. By disrupting livelihoods and water security, it also drives Distress
Migration and expands climate-displaced populations.

7.2 Madden-Julian Oscillation (MJO)
The MJO is a major eastward-moving tropical atmospheric disturbance involving clouds, rainfall, winds, and pressure changes.
It travels near the equator and completes a full cycle in 30–60 days, passing through distinct phases across the Indian and Pacific
Oceans. The MJO consists of Enhanced Convective Phase (Wet Phase) and Suppressed Convective Phase (Dry Phase) moving
eastward along the equator every 30–60 days.


                                                Fig: Madden-Julian Oscillation
                                                        Phases of MJO
 y Two Main Phases: The MJO consists of Enhanced Convective Phase (Wet Phase) and Suppressed Convective Phase
   (Dry Phase) moving eastward along the equator every 30–60 days.
 y Enhanced Convective Phase: Surface winds converge and air rises, leading to cloud formation, heavy rainfall, and
   increased storm activity over tropical regions.
 y Upper-Level Divergence: Rising air spreads outward at upper levels, sustaining convection and strengthening rainfall
   systems.
 y Suppressed Convective Phase: Air sinks from upper atmosphere, becomes warm and dry, resulting in clear skies, weak
   convection, and reduced rainfall.
 y Eastward Movement: This wet–dry dipole shifts west to east across Indian and Pacific Oceans, influencing monsoon
   activity and tropical weather variability.

                                                  Factors Influencing MJO

     Factors                                                        Role in MJO
 Sea Surface         High SSTs (>28°C) in tropical oceans increase evaporation, which adds moisture to the atmosphere. This
 Temperature         fuels latent heat release, driving strong convective activity during the MJO.
 Atmospheric         Ample humidity in the lower and mid-troposphere supports deep cloud formation and sustained
 Moisture            rainfall. It helps maintain the enhanced convective phase of the MJO.


 Zonal Wind          Changes in easterly and westerly winds (called zonal wind anomalies) affect wind shear and guide the
 Anomalies           eastward movement of the MJO. They also influence how strong or weak the MJO becomes.
 Seasonal            Climate patterns like El Niño, La Niña, and Indian Ocean Dipole (IOD) affect background atmospheric
 Variability         circulation, which changes the timing, location, and strength of MJO convection.
                                                Impact of the MJO on India

     Impact                                     Description                                             Example
 Early Monsoon       When MJO is in phases 2–4 over the Indian Ocean, it enhances            In 2024 and 2025, Phase 4 MJO
 Onset               moist convection and triggers low-pressure systems, often leading       coincided with early Mumbai
                     to early onset of the southwest monsoon.                                monsoon.
 Rainfall            Strong MJO phases increase intra-seasonal monsoon variability,          Several Kharif season pulses are
 Intensity           intensifying active spells and improving rainfall distribution across   linked to active MJO-enhanced
                     India.                                                                  convection.
 Favourable for      MJO enhances low-level vorticity, moisture convergence, and             Cyclone Biparjoy (2023)
 Cyclogenesis        vertical instability, creating favorable conditions for cyclogenesis    coincided with strong MJO
                     in pre- and post-monsoon seasons.                                       convection.
 Interaction         If El Niño and MJO both favor Pacific convection, it increases          In 2015, weak rainfall due to El
 with El Niño        downward motion (subsidence) over India, weakening monsoon              Niño + MJO over the Pacific
                     flows and worsening drought risk.                                       Ocean.

7.3 Indian Ocean Dipole (IOD)
The IOD is a coupled ocean-atmosphere phenomenon in the equatorial Indian Ocean, marked by fluctuations in sea surface
temperatures (SSTs) between its eastern (Indonesia) and western (Somalia) poles. Often termed the "Indian Niño", it significantly
influences monsoon dynamics and global weather patterns.

                                              Formation Mechanism of IOD
 y Uneven Heating of Ocean Water: Sea surface temperatures (SSTs) become warmer in the western Indian Ocean (near
   Somalia) and cooler in the eastern side (near Indonesia), or vice versa.
 y Change in Air Pressure: Warm water lowers air pressure, which causes winds to shift — especially equatorial westerlies.
 y Shift in Wind Patterns: Winds either weaken or reverse direction, disrupting the normal flow of moist air and affecting
   cloud formation.
 y Rainfall Redistribution: Rainfall increases over warmer waters (due to rising air) and decreases over cooler areas,
   creating dry or wet conditions in different regions.
 y Positive Feedback Loop Begins: These changes in winds and rainfall further strengthen the SST contrast, making the
   IOD stronger (either positive or negative phase). Ekman Suction/Pumping off the coast of Sumatra/Java drives the
   upwelling of cold water during the Positive IOD phase.
 y Full IOD Phase Established: The pattern becomes stable for a few months, significantly influencing monsoons in India,
   East Africa, Southeast Asia, and Australia.

                                              Impacts of Indian Ocean Dipole

       Impact                                 Description                                          Example
 Influence on Indian      Positive IOD strengthens monsoon winds and                The 2019 Positive IOD contributed to very
 Monsoon                  increases rainfall over India, while negative IOD can     high rainfall in India
                          weaken them.
 Droughts in              Positive IOD leads to reduced rainfall in Australia,      The 2009 Positive IOD was linked to severe
 Australia                increasing drought risk, while negative IOD brings        drought in southeastern Australia.
                          wetter conditions.
 Impact on East           Positive IOD brings heavy rains and floods to East        The 1997 Positive IOD caused devastating
 African Rainfall         Africa, while negative IOD can cause droughts.            floods in Kenya and Somalia.
 Cyclone Activity         Positive IOD enhances cyclone formation in the            The 2019 Positive IOD coincided with
                          Arabian Sea, while negative IOD can promote               Cyclone Kyarr in the Arabian Sea.
                          cyclones in the Bay of Bengal.


Marine Ecosystem   IOD phases alter ocean temperatures and nutrient   During the 1997 Positive IOD, fish catch
Changes            availability, impacting fisheries.                 increased in the western Indian Ocean but
                                                                      declined near Indonesia.
Global Climate     IOD interacts with ENSO, influencing global        A strong Positive IOD in 1997 coincided
Linkages           climate variability.                               with a major El Niño event, amplifying
                                                                      global weather anomalies.
pyq: Most of the unusual climatic happenings are explained as an outcome of the El-Nino effect. Do you agree? (2014)

chapters.text:
8            Sea Level Changes

Sea level changes refer to variations in global, regional, or local mean sea levels due to natural and anthropogenic factors,
affecting coastal zones and climate systems. Global mean sea level is the average across ocean basins; regional sea level varies
due to currents and winds. Global mean sea level has risen 3.7 mm/year (1993–2023).
                                               UPSC Mains PYQs
     1.     How are climate change and the sea level rise affecting the very existence of many island
            nations? Discuss with examples. (2025)


                               Short-Term Causes of Sea Level Changes (Natural)
 y Astronomical Tides: Gravitational pull of the moon and sun causes predictable daily and seasonal sea level fluctuations.
 y Atmospheric Pressure: Low pressure (such as, in cyclones) raises sea level, high pressure lowers it. → Inverse
   Barometer Effect (1 mb drop ≈ 1 cm rise).
 y Ocean Currents: Currents like Gulf Stream or monsoon-driven flows pile water against coasts, raising local sea levels.
      ♦ Example: Bay of Bengal sea level is higher due to monsoon currents.
 y Wind Systems: Strong winds (such as, monsoon or cyclones) push water toward or away from coasts, causing temporary
   rises or falls.
      ♦ Example: Monsoon winds pile water along Kerala coast, causing upwelling.
 y River Discharges: Heavy river runoff during monsoons increases regional sea level. Example: Ganga and Brahmaputra
   floods raise sea level in North Bay of Bengal.
 y Salinity and Temperature: Changes in salinity (such as from evaporation) or temperature affect water density,
   influencing local sea level.
      ♦ Example: Arabian Sea has higher salinity due to evaporation; Bay of Bengal has lower salinity due to river input

                                      Long-Term Causes of Sea Level Changes
 y Thermostatic Expansion/ Eustatic Changes: Warming of ocean water increases volume, raising sea level (Eustacy is
   global sea-level changes driven by variations in the total volume).
 y Melting of Ice: Melting glaciers and ice sheets add water to oceans, increasing global sea level.
 y Tectonic Movements/ Isostatic Changes: Crustal uplift or subsidence alters relative sea level locally or regionally.
      ♦ Example: Kutch coast experiences uplift due to tectonic activity.
 y Post-Glacial Rebound: Land uplift after ice sheet melting reduces relative sea level in some areas.
 y Sediment Changes: Compaction or erosion of coastal sediments affects local sea level.
      ♦ Example: Dredging in deltaic regions increases vulnerability to inundation.
 y Human Activities: Dams, groundwater extraction, and deforestation alter runoff and sediment load, impacting sea level.
      ♦ Example: Dams reduce sediment to deltas, increasing inundation risk.

                                     Impacts on Ecosystems and Human Activities

      Impact                               Description                                           Examples
 Coastal                Mangroves, marshes, and coral reefs face                Sundarbans mangroves and Chilika lake
 Ecosystems             inundation and salinity changes, disrupting             ecosystems at risk with 100 cm rise.
                        habitats.
 Water                  Sea level rise causes salinization of groundwater       Kutch coast salinization reduces freshwater
 Quality                and surface water, affecting freshwater availability    access due to saline intrusion.
                        (Ghyben–Herzberg principle)
 Agriculture and        Salinization damages crops and aquaculture in           Ganga and Brahmaputra deltas face reduced
 Fisheries              deltaic regions.                                        agricultural productivity.


Socio-Economic         Loss of land and livelihoods impacts coastal               Prawn farming in deltaic regions endangered
Disruption             economies and trade.                                       by salinization.
International          Small island nations face severe socio-economic            1989 Male Declaration called for monitoring
Concerns               impacts, prompting global action.                          and aid for island states.

                                                  Impact on Island Nations

                  Impact                                                            Examples
Physical: Accelerated inundation and coastal       y Tuvalu “sinking nation” → loss of habitable land with Sea levels rising at
erosion                                              a rate roughly double the global average.
Hydrological: Saltwater intrusion into             y Kiribati’s atolls saw decline in potable groundwater
freshwater lenses and aquifers
Economic: Collapse of fisheries, tourism,          y Ocean warming and sea level rise disrupt coral reefs and fish stocks,
and EEZ revenues                                     impacting economies reliant on marine resources (eg. Maldives, Marshall
                                                     Islands, Fiji 70% reefs lost by 2030, IUCN)
Social & Demographic: Mass internal and            y Maldives with majority of land less than 1 m above sea →Hulhumalé
cross-border displacement                            artificial island development.
Cultural & Existential: Erosion of identity,       y Tuvalu as the first climate refugees of the 21st century → digital twin
heritage, and nationhood                             project launched 2023 (metaverse citizenship and land registry) to
                                                     preserve statehood even if physical territory vanishes
Geopolitical & Legal: Loss of maritime             y Australia-Tuvalu Falepili Union treaty (2023) →continuing statehood
boundaries and sovereignty under UNCLOS              and sovereignty with Tuvaluans to live/work/study in Australia
Health: Increased vector-borne diseases and        y Dengue outbreaks in Maldives
mental health crises                               y The Solomon Islands → rise in climate anxiety due to chronic fear of
                                                     environmental doom.

                                            Global Initiatives for Island Nations

            Initiative                                                       Description
Green Climate Fund (GCF)                 Dedicated climate fund prioritizing Small Island Developing States (SIDS) in
                                         adaptation, renewable energy, and resilience projects.
Infrastructure for Resilient             India-led initiative launched at COP26 (2021) with UK, Australia, Fiji; multi-hazard
Island States (IRIS)                     resilient infrastructure platform for 57 SIDS
SIDS Least Developed Countries           GEF-managed grant facility supporting adaptation in 50+ SIDS → aquifer protection,
Fund (SIDS LDC Fund)                     coastal defenses (Kiribati, Vanuatu).
COP28 Loss & Damage Fund                 Operationalized 2025 post-SIDS advocacy; $800 million initial pledges for irreversible
                                         climate impacts.
SAMOA Pathway                            UN Framework Convention for SIDS sustainable development; IRIS aligns as key
                                         implementation vehicle.

chapters.text:
9             Global Ocean Heat Content

Global Ocean Heat Content (OHC) measures the total heat energy stored in the ocean, serving as a key indicator of global
warming since oceans absorb over 90% of excess heat trapped by greenhouse gases. Measured in joules, it reflects the high
thermal inertia of oceans, which stabilizes Earth’s climate and delays the manifestation of climate impacts. Warming is
especially pronounced in the deep waters of the Atlantic, North Pacific, Western Pacific and Southern oceans at a faster
rate than the global average.
                                    Drivers of Rising Global Ocean Heat Content

       Driver                                                      Description
 Greenhouse Gas         y Increased CO2 traps heat, with oceans absorbing most of the excess.
 Emissions              y Human-induced emissions are the primary cause. 16 Zettajoule (ZJ) increase in OHC from 2023
                          to 2024 (energy gain equivalent to ~140 times the world’s total annual electricity production).
 Radiative Forcing      y Imbalance between incoming solar and outgoing infrared radiation leads to net heat gain in
                          oceans.
 Ocean Thermal          y Oceans store heat for decades, continuing to warm even if emissions stabilize.
 Inertia                y Long-term heat accumulation since 1955.
 Natural Climate        y El Niño allows the ocean to transfer stored heat to the atmosphere
 Variability            y La Niña promotes greater recharge/uptake of heat into the ocean.
 Reduced Heat Loss      y Changes in atmospheric circulation and cloud cover trap more heat in oceans; reduce heat loss.
 Melting Ice            y Freshwater from melting ice alters salinity and circulation, redistributing heat.
                        y Greenland ice melt contributes to OHC rise.

                                   Impacts of Rising Global Ocean Heat Content

       Impact                                                      Description
 Sea Level Rise         y Thermal expansion (Steric Sea Level Rise) and ice melt raise sea levels, threatening coastal
                          regions. 30–40% of 1900–2020 sea level rise due to OHC (2023 OHC equivalent to 4.6 billion
                          Hiroshima bombs).
 Extreme Weather        y Warmer oceans release heat and moisture, intensifying storms and flooding. Increased hurricane
                          intensity in Atlantic
                        y Marine Heatwaves in the Tropical Indian Ocean are reaching "permanent" status, fundamentally
                          altering the Indian Monsoon.
 Marine Ecosystem       y Enhanced Stratification prevents the mixing of nutrients, warmer waters affect ocean currents,
 Disruption               cause coral bleaching, species migration, and fishery declines.
                        y Coral bleaching in Great Barrier Reef due to OHC rise.
 Ocean Circulation      y OHC alters currents like AMOC, affecting global climate patterns.
 Changes                y Warming in Atlantic and Pacific oceans disrupts circulation.
 Carbon and Water       y OHC impacts Earth’s energy, water, and carbon cycles, affecting climate stability.
 Cycles                 y Warmer oceans reduce carbon uptake efficiency.


  India
Geography

1              Physiography of India

1. Himalayas
The Himalayas, the world’s youngest and highest mountain range, stretch over 2,500 km in an east–west arc from the Syntaxial
Bends at Nanga Parbat (West) in Jammu and Kashmir to Namcha Barwa (East) in Arunachal Pradesh. They spread across 13
Indian States/Union Territories (namely Jammu and Kashmir, Ladakh, Uttarakhand, Himachal Pradesh, Arunachal Pradesh, Manipur,
Meghalaya, Mizoram, Nagaland, Sikkim, Tripura, Assam and West Bengal).


                                               UPSC Mains PYQs

            resources of India? (2020)
      2.    "The Himalayas are highly prone to landslides." Discuss the causes and suggest suitable
            mitigation. (2016)

1.3 Rise in Himalayas and its Implications
The ongoing rise of the Himalayas is driven by Indian-Eurasian plate convergence, causing seismicity, unstable slopes, altered
hydrology, glacier retreat, and ecological zone shifts.

1.6 Shrinking Himalaya n Glaciers

 Factors                   making glaciers more susceptible to climate             Himalayas destabilize ice masses.
                           variability.

 Variability                and flooding; over time, reduced glacier mass lowers            now; reduced summer flow in the

2. Northern Plains
The Northern Plains of India, also known as the Indo-Gangetic-Brahmaputra Plains, are extensive alluvial lowlands formed by
the depositional action of the Himalayan river systems. They are characterised by fertile soils, flat terrain, and dense population
concentration and are regarded as the granary of India.


2.1 Features of Northern Plains


   Location: Extends from Punjab in the west to Assam in the east (approx. 2,400 km).
   Width: Varies between 150–300 km, width is not uniform.
   Elevation: Mostly flat, generally <300 m above sea level.
   River System: Formed by the Indus, Ganga, and Brahmaputra river systems; Delhi-Ambala Ridge (Aravalli extension)
    acts as the water divide between the Indus and Ganga river systems.
   Subdivisions: Bhabar, Terai, Bhangar (older alluvium), and Khadar (newer alluvium).
   Alluvial Features: Bhur (Elevated land formed by wind-blown sand), Reh/Usar (Saline/alkaline efflorescences due to
    over-irrigation), Karail (black-colored heavy clay)

3. Deserts
Deserts are arid landforms characterized by low precipitation, extreme temperature ranges, and sparse vegetation. In India,
both hot deserts (like the Thar) and cold deserts (like Ladakh) exhibit unique climatic and geomorphological features.

3.1 Thar Desert
The Thar Desert, also known as the Great Indian Desert, is a large arid region in northwestern India, primarily in Rajasthan. It
is one of the most densely populated deserts in the world with Luni is one of the significant integrated drainage system terminating
in the Rann of Kachchh. Barchans (crescent-shaped) and seif (longitudinal) dunes are the characteristic aeolian landforms of the
Thar Desert.

3.2 Ladakh Cold Desert
The Ladakh Cold Desert, located in the trans-Himalayan region of northern India, is one of the highest and driest inhabited areas
on Earth. It contains both Hamada (rocky desert) and Reg (stony desert) landforms, though it is predominantly famous for its Erg
(sandy desert) features like massive sand dunes.


Note: Cold Desert (Himachal Pradesh’s Lahaul–Spiti district) has been designated as India’s 13th UNESCO Biosphere Reserve
in 2025 under the Man and the Biosphere (MAB) Programme.

A. Factors for Formation of Ladakh Cold Desert
                                                            Factors
 y Rain Shadow Effect: The Great Himalayan Range blocks southwest monsoon winds, resulting in extremely low rainfall
   (<100 mm annually) in Ladakh.
 y High Altitude: Thin air at 3,000–5,000 m holds little moisture; low pressure reduces cloud formation.
 y Interior Location: Ladakh's continental position isolates it from oceanic influences, depriving it of moist winds from
   both the Arabian Sea and the Bay of Bengal.
 y Cold Climate: Low temperatures reduce evaporation but also limit moisture availability and vegetation growth.
 y Tectonic Uplift: The rise of the Zanskar, Ladakh, and Karakoram ranges formed high-altitude basins and valleys,
   creating enclosed arid zones.

4. Peninsular Plateau
Peninsular Plateau is an elevated landmass of central and southern India, forming one of the oldest and most stable parts of the Indian
Shield, composed of ancient crystalline rocks influencing minerals, drainage and agro-climate.


4.1 Key Features of the Peninsular Plateau
y Ancient Origin: Geologically, one of the oldest landmasses of the world, formed during the Precambrian era as part of
  Gondwanaland → amalgamation of 5 Precambrian Cratonic Nuclei: Dharwar, Bastar, Singhbhum, Bundelkhand, and
  Aravalli cratons
y Tectonic Stability: Structurally tectonically stable, however, its stability is relative, as intraplate earthquakes such as the
  1967 Koyna and 1993 Latur events reveal occasional reactivation of ancient faults.
y Hard Rocks: Dominated by igneous and metamorphic rocks like granite and gneiss, with basalt mainly in the Deccan Trap
  region.
y Dissected Relief: Characterized by peneplains, residual hills (inselbergs), and structurally controlled valleys due to long-
  term denudation.
y Sub-Units of the Plateau: The Central Highlands (Malwa, Bundelkhand), The Deccan Plateau, and The Northeastern
  Plateau. Northeastern Plateau (Meghalaya) is a structural extension separated by the Malda Fault (Garo-Rajmahal Gap).
y Mineral Wealth: Rich in ferrous and non-ferrous minerals, especially in the Chotanagpur Plateau; notable reserves
  include coal, iron ore, bauxite, mica, and uranium
y Drainage Pattern: Shows dendritic, trellis and superimposed drainage, with localized radial patterns; major rivers include
  Narmada, Tapi (west-flowing) and Godavari, Krishna (east-flowing).

4.3 Formation of the Deccan Plateau
   Geological Age: Formed during the Cretaceous–Paleogene (~66 million years ago), much younger than the Archean
    Peninsular Shield.
   Volcanic Origin: Result of fissure volcanism involving massive outpourings of flood basalts across western and central
    India. (Deccan Traps with step-like topography of lava layers.)
   Tectonic Setting: Linked to India’s northward drift over the Réunion mantle plume (hotspot) after breakup from
    Gondwanaland.
   Layered Basalt Rock: Successive lava flows solidified into horizontal basalt strata, forming flat-topped mesas and
    highly erosion-resistant terrain in Maharashtra and Karnataka.
   Geographic Spread: Extends over large parts of Maharashtra, Madhya Pradesh, Telangana, Chhattisgarh, Karnataka,
    and Andhra Pradesh.

5. Coastal Plains
The coastal plains of India are narrow strips of low-lying land adjacent to the coastlines of the Arabian Sea in the west (Western
Coastal Plain) and the Bay of Bengal in the east (Eastern Coastal Plains). Western coastal plains are more clearly associated with
submergence/submergent coast ("fault scarp”, resulting from the rifting of the Indian plate from Africa), while eastern coastal plains
are broader and dominated by deltaic deposition. These plains are of great importance due to their dense population, rich biodiversity,
deltaic agriculture, and strategic maritime advantages.

6. Islands of India
India has a total of approximately 1,382 officially recognized offshore islands, islets, and rocks, including both inhabited and uninhabited
ones. These are primarily divided between the Bay of Bengal and the Arabian Sea.
The Andaman & Nicobar archipelago consists of approximately 572 islands, mainly tectonic in origin (extension of Arakan Yoma
fold belt) with some volcanic islands like Barren Island. The Lakshadweep archipelago comprises 36 coral islands, atolls, and reefs,
representing classic examples of coral geomorphology.

chapters.text:
2               Drainage System of India

                                                 UPSC Mains PYQs
       1.    The interlinking of rivers can provide viable solutions to the multi-dimensional inter-
             related problems of droughts, floods and interrupted navigation. Critically examine.
             (2020)
       2.    There is no formation of deltas by rivers of the Western Ghat. Why? (2013)

Drainage pattern refers to the system of flow of surface water mainly through the forms of rivers and basins. The drainage system
depends upon factors such as slope of land, geological structure, amount of volume of water and velocity of water. India’s drainage
is primarily divided into Himalayan and Peninsular systems, with secondary classification based on drainage direction and basin
characteristics.


1. Himalayan vs Peninsular Drainage System
  Feature                        Himalayan Rivers                                         Peninsular Rivers
Origin            Originate from Himalayan glaciers and are mainly       Originate from Western Ghats, Eastern Ghats,
                  snow-fed sources. (Example: Indus River)               Vindhya-Satpura ranges or central highlands.
                                                                         (Example: Godavari River)
Geological        Mainly antecedent drainage, predating Himalayan        Mainly superimposed drainage, established on former
History           uplift.(e.g., Indus, Sutlej, Brahmaputra).             land surfaces.
Evolutionary      Youthful stage with active downcutting, high           Mature/graded stage, approaching base level; lateral
Stage             energy.                                                erosion dominant.
Flow Nature       Perennial, fed by both snowmelt and rainfall,          Seasonal, mainly rain-fed, with reduced flow in dry
                  ensuring year-round flow.                              months.
Course &          Long, meandering courses with gentle gradients;        Shorter courses with steeper gradients; flow directly
Length            flow through plains and form large deltas.             into the sea or form smaller deltas/estuaries.
Drainage          Typically dendritic (tree-like) or trellis patterns    Often rectangular, trellis, or radial patterns
Pattern           (Example: Ganges System)                               (Example: Krishna Basin)
Sediment          Carry heavy alluvial load, forming fertile             Carry less sediment, mainly rocky and sandy
Load              floodplains.                                           material.
Erosion &         Intense erosion in upper courses and extensive         Not predominantly erosional because these rivers flow
Deposition        deposition in lower courses.                           over ancient, stable, and highly resistant hard rocks.
Examples          Ganga, Yamuna, Brahmaputra.                            Godavari, Krishna, Narmada, Mahanadi.

​​2. No Delta formation by West Flowing Rivers
West-flowing rivers such as the Narmada, Tapi, Sharavati, and Mandovi generally form Estuaries, high-energy transition
zones, where marine processes dominate fluvial deposition. They do not form deltas as they drain into the Arabian Sea (Narmada
and Tapi are not Western Ghats rivers). This is due to a combination of physical, geological, and marine factors.

                              Reason for No Delta Formation by West Flowing Rivers
Steep Gradient         These rivers originate from the Western Ghats and descend rapidly toward the sea. The steep slope
                       causes high river velocity, allowing little time for sediments to settle, which is necessary for delta
                       formation.
Short Course           Since the Arabian Sea is close to the source, these rivers have short lengths and limited opportunity
                       to transport and deposit large quantities of sediments.
Hard Rock              The terrain through which they flow is made of resistant igneous and metamorphic rocks in Rift Valleys
Terrain                (confined course)→ reduces erosion, limiting the supply of fine alluvium needed to build deltas.
Strong Wave            The Arabian Sea experiences powerful wave currents that continuously wash away any deposited
Action                 sediments at the river mouths, preventing delta build-up.
Narrow                 The western continental shelf is narrow and drops sharply, which discourages sediment
Continental Shelf      accumulation near the coastline, unlike the eastern shelf which is broader and allows delta growth.

3. Challenges to India’s River Systems
  Challenge                                        Description                                             Example
Pollution            Discharge of industrial effluents, untreated sewage, and agricultural      Ganga → untreated urban and
                     runoff degrade water quality, harming ecosystems and human health.         industrial waste.
Over-extraction      Excessive withdrawal for irrigation, industry, and domestic use            Ganga's summer flow in the
of Water             reduces river flow and impacts aquatic life → Over-extraction of           plains is fed by groundwater
                     groundwater is drying the river from below.                                aquifers
Encroachment         Unregulated construction and illegal sand mining alter riverbeds,          Ken River in MP affected by
& Sand Mining        leading to habitat loss and bank erosion.                                  excessive mining.
Climate Change       Changing rainfall patterns, glacial melt, unstable glacial lakes           Brahmaputra experiencing
Impacts              (GLOFs) and extreme weather events cause floods and droughts.              higher flood frequency.


 Water                Competing demands for river water create political and legal               Cauvery Water Dispute
 Disputes             conflicts.                                                                 between Karnataka and TN.
 Loss of              Decline in aquatic species due to pollution, dry riverbeds and siltation   Indus River Dolphin
 Biodiversity         due to damming, and habitat fragmentation.                                 endangered due to flow
                                                                                                 obstruction.

4. Interlinking of Rivers
The National River Linking Project (NRLP) is a large-scale civil engineering project, steered by National Water Development
Agency (NWDA), aimed at managing India's water resources by connecting various rivers. The project is divided into two primary
geographic segments involving 30 total links proposed (14 Himalayan + 16 Peninsular).


                                             Advantages of River Interlinking

   Parameter                               Description                                           Substantiation
 Equitable           Inter-basin transfer can balance the water-rich regions        Addressing "Water Stress" (India has 18%
 Distribution        with the water-scarce regions to ensure Water Security.        global population with 4% global water
                                                                                    resources).
 Agricultural        Expansion of cultivable area and boosting productivity         Ken-Betwa Link Project (KBLP): Irrigate
 Impact              in rain-fed regions.                                           drought-prone Bundelkhand
 Disaster            Can help in regulating flow to reduce the risk of              Kosi-Mechi River Linking Project;
 Mitigation          seasonal floods in the East and combat droughts in the         Godavari-Krishna-Polavaram project
                     West and South
 Energy              Can provide an opportunity to generate hydropower,             Par-Tapi-Narmada Link; Multi-purpose
 Generation          renewable and clean sources of energy, reducing fossil         river valley projects.
                     fuel reliance
 Transportation      Can improve the navigability of rivers and promote             National Waterway 4 connect the Godavari
                     inland waterways transportation.                               and Krishna rivers


                                         Disadvantages of River Interlinking
 Ecological &        Construction disrupts aquatic ecosystems, harms wildlife     Loss of biodiversity in Panna Tiger Reserve
 Environmental       habitats, and alters natural sedimentation patterns;         due to KBLP
                     Disruption of riparian rights.
 Economic            The project is capital-intensive, requiring massive          Estimated cost of ₹15–17lakh crore;
 Feasibility         budgetary allocations → may lead to cost overruns and        Concerns over Sunk Costs.
                     economic inefficiencies.
 Governance          Can lead to increase in inter-state conflicts between        U.P.-M.P. tussle in Ken-Betwa river-linking
 Issues              "surplus" and "deficit" states regarding water rights.       project
 International       Any Himalayan link has profound implications for             Kosi-Mechi interlinkage implication on
 Hydro-politics      downstream relations with Bangladesh.                        Ganga-Brahmaputra-Meghna (GBM) basin
 Social              Can lead to displacement of local communities; specially     Chita Andolan by tribals against Ken-Betwa
 Disruption          tribal and marginal communities                              river-linking project
 Climate             Monsoon variability and overextraction of groundwater        Ganga Basin: regional deficits despite
 Vulnerability       invalidates static hydrological modeling; siltation          NWDA "surplus" tag; fed by
                     reduces storage over years                                   groundwater in plains

                                                        Way Forward
 y Hydro-social Approach: Move beyond engineering-only solutions to include the valuation of water from social and
   ecological viewpoints using community-led water budgeting.
 y Decentralized Management: Complement NRLP with traditional water harvesting (e.g., Johads, Baolis, and Tankas) and
   watershed development to store water locally.
 y Climate Resilience: Account for Himalayan glacial melt, which initially increases river discharge but leads to long-term
   depletion.
 y Sustainable Infrastructure: Ensure mandatory Environmental Impact Assessments (EIA) to mitigate risks like
   Reservoir Induced Seismicity in fragile tectonic zones.

5. Inland Water Transport
India has a long inland waterways network, with several thousand kilometres of navigable waterways and 111 declared National
Waterways. Despite this vast natural wealth, Inland Water Transport (IWT) currently represents only ~2% of India’s modal mix,
a figure the government aspires to increase to ~5% by 2030 through the Maritime India Vision (MIV)-2030.

Potential and Benefits of Inland Water Transport

    Category                        Description                                      Data/Substantiation
 Cost-Efficiency      IWT is the most economical mode for            Approximately costs ₹0.25–0.30 per tonne/km vs. ₹1.00
                      bulk cargo, with significantly lower           by rail and ₹1.50 on roads.
                      per-unit costs than road or rail.
 Environmental        Waterways are far more fuel-efficient and      Inland vessels emit almost half of CO2
 Impact               produce fewer greenhouse gas emissions.        per tonne-km than trucks.
 Infrastructure       Shifting cargo to rivers reduces the           Helps decongest choked highways and overburdened
 Relief               burden on overcrowded land-based               railways, potentially reducing accidents.
                      networks.
 Regional             Provides vital connectivity to remote,         NW-1 (Ganga-Bhagirathi-Hooghly system) and the
 Development          landlocked, or underserved regions.            India-Bangladesh Protocol Route enhance trade access
                                                                     for Eastern and North-Eastern states.
 Tourism              Offers unique opportunities for eco-           MV Ganga Vilas (the world's longest river cruise)
 Potential            tourism and cultural exploration.


Critical Challenges and Barriers
   Geographical Limitations: Many Indian rivers suffer from variable flow, with significant seasonal depth variations due to
    the monsoon, making year-round navigation unpredictable.
   Silting and Sedimentation: Heavy sedimentation in river channels raises dredging costs and limits navigability.


   Infrastructure Deficiency: Limited terminal infrastructure, night-navigation aids, jetties, mechanised handling, and river
    information systems constrain efficient operations.
   Intermodal Connectivity: Poor integration between waterways and existing road/rail networks creates "last-mile" logistics
    hurdles.
   Competition and Market Integration: Roads and railways are more developed and often faster, leading to private sector
    hesitation to invest in waterways due to perceived low freight volumes.
   Environmental and Social Concerns: Environmental clearances, ecological flow requirements, and river-based livelihoods
    can complicate navigation-oriented interventions.

Initiatives to Boost Inland Transportation
The Budget 2025-26 has introduced several strategic measures to propel the shipping and inland waterways industry.
 Maritime Development Fund: A ₹25,000 crore fund established to provide long-term financing and enhance global
    competitiveness.
 Financial Assistance Policy: Includes credit incentives for shipbreaking and the extension of the tonnage tax scheme
    specifically to inland vessels.
 Tax and Duty Exemptions: A 10-year extension on basic customs duty exemptions for raw materials and consumables to
    reduce shipbuilding costs.
 Jal Marg Vikas Project (JMVP): A World Bank-assisted project focused on improving the navigational capacity of NW-1
    (Haldia-Allahabad).
 Inland Vessels Act, 2021: A modern regulatory framework that establishes uniform standards for vessel registration, safety,
    certification, and navigation across India’s inland waterways.
 Green Tug Transition Program (GTTP): Aiming to replace conventional fuel-powered harbor tugs with environmentally
    friendly tugs by 2040.
Further innovations in vessel design, standardizing regulations across different states to eliminate operational complexities, financial
incentivisation and other fiscal instruments can help transition inland waterways from underutilized natural routes to dynamic
economic corridors.

6. Initiatives for Water Resource Development
India’s river development strategy has evolved from a focus on structural engineering to a multi-dimensional "hydro-social" approach
that integrates ecological rejuvenation, economic connectivity, and disaster resilience.

      Dimension                                   Description                                            Examples
 Ecological                  Shift from reactive cleaning → sustainable river             National Mission for Clean Ganga/
 Rejuvenation                rejuvenation, pollution abatement, biodiversity revival      Namami Gange.
 Nature-Based                Utilises natural ecosystems and geomorphic processes         "Room for the River" (the Dutch model
 Solutions (NbS)             to enhance water security, flood resilience,                 adopted for the Kuttanad region in
                             groundwater recharge, and ecosystem sustainability.          Kerala); focus on Gangetic dolphin
                                                                                          conservation
 Catchment Area              Afforestation, soil conservation → reduced sediment          Silvopasture & Alley Cropping in Mahi
 Treatment                   load, flood moderation                                       ravines (Gujarat).
 Urban Water Body            Revival of lakes, tanks, wetlands → flood buffering,         Mission Kakatiya; Bring Back the
 Restoration                 groundwater recharge                                         Lakes (Bengaluru)
 River-linked                Livelihood integration, sustainable economic                 Arth Ganga model – target ≥3% of
 Economy                     utilisation, river-based GDP                                 India’s GDP from Ganga basin
 Strategic                   Rivers as “economic arteries” → low-cost, low-               National Waterways Act, 2016 – 111
 Infrastructure              carbon transport; multimodal connectivity; River             waterways; Ganga & Brahmaputra
                             Cruise Tourism                                               cruise circuits
 Urban Flood                 AI/IoT-based early warning systems                           iFLOWS (Mumbai); C-FLOWS
 Management                                                                               (Chennai); Digital Twins
 Dam Safety &                Standardised surveillance framework for ageing               Dam Safety Act, 2021 (>1000 dams
 Regulation                  dams                                                         aging)
 GLOF Management             Monitoring glacial lake outburst floods in Himalayas         South Lhonak Lake (Sikkim) early
                                                                                          warning
The Mihir Shah Committee (2016-17) recommended merging the Central Water Commission (CWC) and Central Ground Water
Board (CGWB) into a unified National Water Commission for integrated and sustainable water governance.

3             Natural Vegetation in India

                                               UPSC Mains PYQs
      1.    Identify and discuss the factors responsible for the diversity of natural vegetation in
            India. Assess the significance of wildlife sanctuaries in the rainforest regions of India.
            (2023)

India is one of the recognized mega-diverse countries of the world, harbouring nearly 7-8% of the recorded species of
the world, and representing 4 of the 36 globally identified biodiversity hotspots (Himalaya, Indo-Burma, Western Ghats
and Sri Lanka, Sundaland). India's natural vegetation reflects its vast geographic and climatic diversity, ranging from
tropical rainforests to alpine flora. It plays a crucial role in supporting biodiversity, regulating climate, and sustaining
ecological balance.


1. Types of Natural Vegetation in India
  Major Type                                                     Sub-Type
 Moist Tropical     y Tropical Wet Evergreen: Dense, multi-layered; >250 cm rainfall; found in Western Ghats, A&N
 Forests              Islands and NE India, Flora–Mahogany, Rosewood
                    y Tropical Semi-Evergreen: Mixed moist deciduous and evergreen; transitional type, 150-250 cm
                      rainfall, found in NE India, Western Ghats, Eastern coast, Flora–White Cedar, Hollock, and Kail.
                    y Tropical Moist Deciduous: Most widespread, found in Central India; monsoon forests; 100–200 cm
                      rainfall
                    y Littoral and Swamp: Coastal wetlands marked with soil saturation (eg. Sunderbans): mangroves and
                      marshes, Includes mangrove species like Rhizophora


Dry Tropical     y Tropical Dry Evergreen: Short, hard-leaved trees (eg. Ebony, Palmyra); 75-100 cm rainfall, Areas: TN, AP,
Forests          y Tropical Dry Deciduous: Shed leaves in dry season (eg. Babul, Tendu); moderate rainfall, Areas: RJ,
                   GJ, UP
                 y Tropical Thorn: Scrub and thorny bushes; <75 cm rainfall, Areas: RJ, GJ
Montane          y Sub-tropical Broadleaved Hill: Oak, chestnut dominant; moist slopes, found in Eastern Himalayas,
Sub-tropical       Western Ghats; Rainfall: 120-150 cm
Forests          y Sub-tropical Moist Hill (Pine): Chir pine dominant; drier slopes; found in Himalayas, Western Ghats
                 y Sub-tropical Dry Evergreen: Xerophytic shrubs, small-leaved species
                 y Montane Wet Temperate (Shola Forests): Stunted evergreen broadleaf forests with grasslands; cool,
                   humid high-altitude zones of the Western Ghats
Montane          y Montane Wet Temperate: Mixed coniferous-broadleaf; high rainfall zones
Temperate        y Himalayan Moist Temperate: Oak, deodar, fir; cool climate
Forests          y Himalayan Dry Temperate: Juniper, blue pine; drier inner valleys
Alpine Forests   y Sub-Alpine: Rhododendrons, birch near tree line
                 y Moist Alpine Scrub: Shrubs, mosses, lichens in moist zones
                 y Dry Alpine Scrub: Sparse, hardy plants; cold, dry conditions

2. Changes and Impacts of Natural Vegetation in India
  Change                        Description                                                   Impact
Deforestation    Large-scale clearing of forests for                 Forest loss in Hasdeo Arand (Chhattisgarh) has caused
                 agriculture, infrastructure, and industrial         habitat fragmentation, soil erosion, and local
                 use.                                                microclimate changes.
Degradation      Forest quality declines due to overgrazing,         Mining in the Western Ghats led to reduced biodiversity,
                 mining, and shifting cultivation.                   affecting endemic species.
Encroachment     Human settlements and road building                 Urban sprawl near Bannerghatta (Karnataka) increased
                 inside forest areas.                                man-animal conflict and core habitat shrinkage.
Plantation       Plantation of non-native/exotic species             Eucalyptus plantations in Tamil Nadu caused
forestry         replacing indigenous forest types.                  groundwater depletion and loss of native biodiversity.
Climate          Altered precipitation and rising                    Upward shift in the tree line in the Himalayas shows
Change           temperatures are shifting vegetation zones.         climatic stress on temperate and alpine forests.

3. Factors Responsible for Diversity of Natural Vegetation in India
   Factor                             Description                                                   Example
Latitude         Determines the sun’s intensity, influencing                    Evergreen forests in the Andaman Islands vs
                 vegetation types from tropical to alpine.                      thorn forests in Rajasthan.
Altitude and     Vegetation changes with elevation due to temperature           Montane forests in Nilgiris and Himalayas.
Topography       and moisture gradients.
Aspect (Slope    South-facing slopes in the Himalayas receive more              South: Shimla → warmer and more inhabited;
Orientation)     sunlight and are often drier while North-facing slopes         North: oak, deodar, and conifer forests
                 are more forested and moist.
Climate          Rainfall and temperature patterns shape forest types.          Deciduous forests in MP; evergreen in NE India.
Precipitation    Seasonal monsoonal regime creates spatial and                  Western Ghats: windward side →Tropical
Patterns         temporal variability → precipitation gradients +               Evergreen forests; leeward side → dry
                 vegetation zonation.                                           deciduous or thorn scrub
Soil Types       Different soils support different vegetation                   Lateritic soils support tropical moist forests and
                 communities.                                                   plantation crops like cashew.
Hydrology        Perennial river systems support riparian vegetation,           Riparian → Arjun, Reed Bamboo
                 while deltaic regions facilitate specialized Mangrove          Mangroves → Sundarbans
                 ecosystems
Human            Agriculture, urbanisation, mining, and afforestation           Shola forests cleared for tea estates in Nilgiris.
Activities       alter natural vegetation.


4. Significance of Wildlife Sanctuaries in the rainforest regions
Rainforests in India (Western Ghats, NE India, and Andaman Islands) are high-productivity zones but are ecologically fragile.
Wildlife Sanctuaries (WLS) in these regions serve critical roles:
     Dimension                             Description                                        Examples
 In-situ Biodiversity     Protection of natural habitats, conservation of    Wayanad WLS – Malabar giant squirrel,
 Conservation             endangered & keystone species + endemic            Nilgiri langur
                          flora/fauna
 Habitat Connectivity     Maintains wildlife corridors, ensures gene flow,   Sathyamangalam WLS — elephant corridor
                          reduces conflict                                   connecting the Western and Eastern Ghats via
                                                                             the Nilgiri Biosphere Reserve.
 Ecological Balance       Carbon sinks (high biomass); regulate              Periyar WLS – supports Periyar river basin;
 & Carbon                 microclimate, rainfall, hydrological cycles        Rainforests hold highest per-hectare carbon
 Sequestration                                                               stocks
 Cultural & Tribal        Conservation of Sacred Groves, preservation of     Nokrek Biosphere-linked wildlife sanctuary
 Significance             indigenous knowledge systems                       areas in Meghalaya
 Socio-Economic           Ecotourism + sustainable livelihoods, MFP-         Thattekkad WLS– birdwatching ecotourism
 Value                    based economy
 Climate Change           Enhances ecosystem resilience, acts as climate     Dampa WLS (Mizoram) – dense tropical forest
 Mitigation &             refugia                                            ecosystem with climate-buffering role
 Adaptation
 Disaster                 Vegetation reduces flood risk, prevents            Eaglenest WLS useful for landslide and
 Mitigation               landslides, and controls desertification.          slope-stability in the Northeast.
 Scientific Research      Living laboratories for ecology, conservation      Wayanad WLS – research on human-wildlife
 & Conservation           success models                                     interaction
 Models
