chapters.text:
4             Soils in India

Soils of India are diverse due to variations in geology, climate, topography, and vegetation. They form the bedrock of India’s
primary sector, supporting the livelihoods of over 50% of the population and maintaining ecological balance. Dokuchaev’s Formula
provides for the foundational pedogenic equation: S=f(cl,o,r,p,t) → soil is a function of Climate, Organisms, Relief, Parent Material,
and Time.


1. Factors Influencing Soil Formation (Pedogenesis) in India
    Factor                              Description                                                  Example
 Parent           Determines mineral content and texture and can                 Black cotton soil in India forms from weathered
 Rock             influence soil fertility.                                      basalt in the Deccan Trap region.
 Climate          Affects rate of weathering and leaching → Warmer               y Heavy rain in parts of Northeast leads to
                  temperatures accelerate the rate of chemical weathering          laterite soil.
                  + Cold temperatures slow down the process                      y Low rainfall can lead to salinization in arid
                                                                                   regions
                                                                                 y Desilication: Silica getting washed away,
                                                                                   leaving behind iron and aluminum oxides.


Topography      y Slope impacts erosion and soil depth; Steeper               y Steep Himalayan slopes have thin soils.
                  slopes→ thinner and less developed soils; Well-
                  drained areas → influence nutrient leaching and
                  mineral composition
Biotic          Vegetation adds organic matter and microbes,                    Dense forests in Western Ghats enrich humus.
Factors         enhancing fertility and soil structure; Bacteria and fungi
                break down organic matter, influencing soil structure.
Time            More time allows deeper soil development.                       Bhangar region shows mature alluvial soil.
Human           Primary soil-forming (or deforming) agent →                     Compaction, chemical alteration
Activity        “Anthropogenic forcing"


2. Major Soil Types in India
    Soil Type                               Description                                   Geographical Distribution
Alluvial Soil         Deposited by rivers like Ganga, Brahmaputra during             Indo-Gangetic-Brahmaputra plains, deltaic
                      floods; Rich in Potash, Lime but poor in Nitrogen,             regions, and river valleys. → largest soil
                      Phosphorus                                                     group in India (~40%)
Black Soil (Regur)    Weathering of basaltic lava (Deccan Trap); retains             Maharashtra, Madhya Pradesh, Gujarat,
                      moisture; clayey; "self-ploughing" character due to deep       Andhra Pradesh, parts of Karnataka and
                      cracks.                                                        Tamil Nadu.
Red Soil              Formed by weathering of ancient crystalline igneous            Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh,
                      rocks; high Iron content (turns yellow when hydrated),         Odisha, Chhattisgarh, Jharkhand.
                      poor in humus and nitrogen.
Laterite Soil         Intense leaching under heavy rainfall; rich in iron and        Western Ghats, Odisha, parts of West
                      aluminium, low fertility.                                      Bengal, parts of North-East states.
Arid Soil             Typically deposited by wind in dry areas; sandy                Rajasthan, Gujarat, parts of Haryana and
                      texture; low humus and moisture.                               Punjab.
Forest (Mountain)     Formed under forest cover; varies with altitude; rich          Jammu & Kashmir, Himachal Pradesh,
Soil                  in organic matter.                                             Uttarakhand, North-East Hills; Western/
                                                                                     Eastern Ghats


 Saline & Alkaline       Formed due to poor drainage and excessive                    Arid and semi-arid areas of Rajasthan,
 Soil (Usar, Reh, or     irrigation; salt accumulation at surface.                    Haryana, Punjab, Uttar Pradesh, Gujarat.
 Kallar)

                         Physiological Desert: Conditions where water is physically present (in saline soil) but osmotic
                         pressure prevents plants from absorbing it.
 Peaty & Marshy          Accumulation of organic matter in waterlogged                Kerala, West Bengal, parts of Bihar and
 Soil                    conditions.                                                  Odisha.
Note: According to the US Soil Taxonomy (ICAR system): Alluvial → Inceptisols / Entisols; Black → Vertisols; Arid →
Aridisols; Red → Alfisols

3. Issues with the Soils of India
      Issue                             Description                                           Substantiation
 Soil Erosion        ~32% of India’s land area is undergoing degradation,      Topsoil loss in Chambal ravines and Shiwalik hills
                     with 25% facing desertification, caused by                reduces fertility drastically.
                     deforestation, overgrazing, shifting cultivation,
                     and poor farming practices.
 Soil                Excessive irrigation, poor drainage, and high             South-west Punjab, western Haryana, and parts of
 Salinization        evaporation rates → capillary rise of saline water in     Rajasthan with canal irrigation → poor drainage
                     arid/semi-arid zones.                                     + high evaporation have raised salinity levels.
  Soil               ~30% of cultivable land in India carry acidic soil due    Northeastern (Assam’s tea gardens), Himalayan
 Acidification       to industrial activities and intense farming              regions
 Soil Nutrient       Excessive cropping without replenishing nutrients         Alluvial soils in the Indo-Gangetic belt show
 Depletion           through organic inputs → deficiency in soil nutrients     micronutrient deficiency (Zinc, Boron).
                     → poor yields
 Waterlogging        Caused by over-irrigation and poor drainage               Waterlogged black soil regions in MP,
                     infrastructure.                                           Maharashtra.
 Soil                Use of heavy machinery and poor tillage practices         Common in mechanized zones of Punjab-Haryana
 Compaction          reduce soil porosity and root penetration → affects
                     agricultural yield.
 Soil Organic        SOC is the primary indicator of soil health and a vital   Currently <0.5% in many Indian plains; Ideal
 Carbon (SOC)        part of India's Net-Zero 2070 carbon sequestration        between 1.5% and 3.5%
 Crisis              strategy.
 Soil                Groundwater over-extraction not only dries rivers but     Punjab and Haryana: Reduced soil cohesion, and
 Desiccation         causes soil desiccation, leading to the "structural       increasing vulnerability to land degradation.
                     collapse" of the soil matrix.

4. Soil Acidification
Soil acidification is the process by which soil pH decreases over time, making it more acidic. Over 30% of cultivable land in India
is said to carry acidic soil (Down to Earth).

       Aspect                                   Causes                                              Impacts
 Excessive Use of         Continuous application of ammonium-based                Self-Reinforcing Loop: Declining soil pH
 Chemical                 fertilizers increases hydrogen ion concentration,       lowers nutrient-use efficiency, leading farmers
 Fertilizers              reducing soil pH over time.                             to apply more fertilizers and intensify
                                                                                  acidification.
 Acid Rain                Emissions of SO₂ and NOx from industries and            Damages soil structure, ↓ microbial activity, ↑
 Deposition               vehicles mix with rainwater, producing acidic           nutrient leaching, especially of calcium and
                          precipitation.                                          magnesium.
 Leaching in High         Heavy monsoons in northeastern and coastal              Increases aluminum toxicity, hampers root
 Rainfall Areas           regions wash away basic cations (Ca2+, Mg2+, K+),       development, and reduces crop tolerance to
                          leaving soils more acidic.                              environmental stress.


 Decomposition of          Microbial breakdown of organic residues releases        Alters nutrient cycles, making phosphorus less
 Organic Matter            organic acids that gradually lower soil pH.             available and affecting crop productivity.
 Mining Activities         Exposure of sulfide minerals during mining              Leads to severe soil degradation, harming both
                           generates sulfuric acid through oxidation,              agricultural lands and surrounding
                           contaminating nearby soils.                             ecosystems.

5. Steps Taken by India to Tackle Soil Issues
              Initiative                                                        Description
 Soil Health Card Scheme                   Recommends crop-wise nutrient and fertilizer application based on soil testing;
                                           tackles nutrient imbalance.
 Neem-Coated Urea (NCU)                    Improves nitrogen use efficiency and curbs nitrogen loss; addresses nitrogen
                                           depletion and pollution.
 Nano Urea                                 Reduces the bulk application of nitrogen, mitigating the soil acidification risks →
                                           more efficient, "climate-smart" alternative to traditional NCU
 Pradhan Mantri Krishi Sinchayee           Promotes micro-irrigation (“Per Drop More Crop”); prevents salinization and
 Yojana (PMKSY)                            waterlogging.
 National Mission for Sustainable          Encourages soil conservation, organic carbon improvement, and climate-resilient
 Agriculture (NMSA)                        farming.
 Regenerative Agriculture and              Centred on restoring soil health and enhancing biodiversity → reducing soil
 Natural Farming                           disturbance, promoting crop rotation, cultivating cover crops.
India's varied physiographic divisions not only reflect its complex geological history but also critically influence its biodiversity,
agricultural patterns, and disaster vulnerability, making them vital for sustainable planning and policy.

5             Indian Monsoon

                                               UPSC Mains PYQs
      1.    Why is the South-West Monsoon called ‘Purvaiya’ (easterly) in Bhojpur Region? How has
            this directional seasonal wind system influenced the cultural ethos of the region? (2023)
      2.    What characteristics can be assigned to monsoon climate that succeeds in feeding more
            than 50 percent of the world population residing in Monsoon Asia? (2017)
      3.    How far do you agree that the behaviour of the Indian monsoon has been changing due to
            humanizing landscapes? Discuss. (2015)

The Indian monsoon is a complex seasonal wind system driven by differential heating of land and sea, influencing over 70% of
annual rainfall with high spatial and temporal variability. It has two main branches—Southwest (June–Sept) and Northeast
(Oct–Dec)—and shows high variability in onset, intensity, duration, and spatial distribution.

                                             Thermal Theory of Monsoon
 y The Thermal Theory, proposed by Halley and later refined by Blanford, explains the Indian monsoon as a result of
   differential heating between the Indian subcontinent and the surrounding oceans.
 y During summer, intense heating of land creates a low-pressure zone over northwestern India, drawing in moist
   southwesterly winds from the ocean, leading to monsoon rains. In winter, the process reverses, causing dry northeasterly
   winds.

    Evidence Supporting Thermal Theory                           Challenges and Limitations of the Theory
 Strong surface heating over North West India           Tibetan Plateau heating plays a significant role, but is not addressed
 creates low pressure every summer.                     in this theory.
  Southwest monsoon winds flow from sea to land         Ignores the vertical structure of the atmosphere → Fails to explain
 due to pressure gradient in summer.                    breaks, bursts, and inter-annual variability of monsoon.
  The "heat low" over the Thar Desert is well-          Doesn’t account for influences of El Niño–Southern Oscillation
 documented and attracts moist winds.                   (ENSO) or Indian Ocean Dipole (IOD).
  Observations confirm reversal of wind patterns        Ignores the crucial role of upper atmospheric circulation, such as
 during winter, aligning with theory.                   jet streams.

                                               Origin Of Indian Monsoon
 The Indian monsoon originates from the complex interaction of land-sea heating contrasts, atmospheric circulation, and
 oceanic and topographic influences. It is not caused by a single factor, but emerges from several interlinked systems.


                                Factors Affecting Formation of South West Monsoon

        Factor                                              Impact on Indian Monsoon
Differential Heating of     Land heats faster than sea in summer, forming low pressure over India and drawing moist winds
Land and Sea                from the sea. (Stronger heating → usually strong monsoon winds.)
Tibetan Plateau             The Tibetan Plateau acts like a heat engine → heating of the plateau (at ~4,000m) creates a
Heating                     thermal anti-cyclone in the upper troposphere, which is the physical driver of the Tropical
                            Easterly Jet (TEJ).
Mascarene High              Located at ~30°S near Madagascar as the "high-pressure engine" that pushes the cross-equatorial
                            flow toward India.
El Niño                     El Niño warms the central and eastern Pacific Ocean, disturbing wind patterns. This weakens
(Warm Pacific)              monsoon winds, often causing less rainfall or drought in India (2002, 2015).
La Niña                     The cooling of the central and eastern Pacific strengthens winds that reach India. This brings
(Cool Pacific)              stronger monsoon currents and good rainfall (such as in 2022).
Southern                    The atmospheric component of ENSO, involving pressure differences between the tropical eastern
Oscillation                 Pacific and western Pacific (near Australia/Indonesia). When the usual pressure pattern reverses,
(SO)                        monsoon winds can get disrupted, reducing rainfall.
Indian Ocean               y A positive IOD (warm western Indian Ocean) boosts monsoon rains, especially over central
Dipole (IOD)                 and western India.
                           y A negative IOD often weakens rainfall, especially in the south.
Madden-Julian               When this moving cloud and rain system is over the Indian Ocean, it temporarily increases
Oscillation (MJO)           monsoon rains by creating more low-pressure systems.
Warm Seas near India        Warmer sea surface temperatures (SSTs) increase evaporation and moisture supply, strengthening
                            monsoon depressions and rainfall systems. It can also intensify extreme rain and cyclones.
Jet Streams                 In summer, the Subtropical Westerly Jet moves north, making way for monsoon winds. The
(STWJ & TEJ)                Tropical Easterly Jet helps rainfall by pushing air upward in the atmosphere.
ITCZ                        Zone of maximum convective activity near the equator → Thermal Equator. In summer, it shifts
                            over India, becoming the monsoon trough and bringing rainfall. Dynamic Theory (Flohn’s
                            Theory) explains the seasonal migration of the ITCZ.
Somali Jet                  Primary "conveyor belt" transporting moisture from the Southern Indian Ocean to the Arabian Sea.
                            Its strength is a direct indicator of monsoon intensity.
Arctic Amplification        The faster warming of the Arctic can disturb global wind patterns. This might cause delay or
                            irregular monsoon rains in India. An emerging hypothesis with indirect influence.
Climate Change              Warming is increasing moisture capacity and causing erratic rainfall with intense short spells,
                            frequent droughts, and delayed withdrawal + re-drawing risk map → Decline in wet spells in
                            Ganga, Brahmaputra basin + Floods in Rajasthan (2025)

                             Reason For South West Monsoon being called as Purvaiya

       Factors                                                       Explanation
Geographical              After entering India, the monsoon winds from the Bay of Bengal branch curve and flow from the
Orientation               east/northeast towards the northwest across the Gangetic plains. Hence, in Bhojpur, winds appear to
                          come from the east (Purab).
Dominance of Bay          The region is mainly influenced by the Bay of Bengal branch, which travels westward along the
of Bengal Branch          Ganga plains, giving an easterly orientation.
Historical Usage          Traditional communities relied on observation-based naming, and “Purvaiya” became a cultural
                          term passed through generations.
Local Perception of       Wind direction is named based on where it comes from, not where it goes. The word “Purvaiya”
Wind Direction            stems from Hindi-Bhojpuri, where “Purv” means east and “aiya” implies direction — literally
                          meaning easterly wind.


                                     Influence of Purvaiya on Bhojpur Region
y Agricultural Practices: Marks the onset of the Kharif season (paddy, maize); Farmers associate Purvaiya with fertility and
  prosperity.
y Folk Songs and Literature: Widely referenced in Bhojpuri folk songs, Kajri and Chaiti traditions; Symbolizes longing,
  romance, and emotional connection.
     ♦ Example: Songs describing Purvaiya bringing memories of loved ones.
y Seasonal Festivals and Rituals: Linked with monsoon festivals like Teej and Sawan celebrations; Celebrates rain,
  greenery, and rejuvenation of life.
y Emotional and Symbolic Meaning: Seen as a messenger of rain, hope, and renewal; Often personified in poetry as a
  carrier of emotions and change.
y Settlement and Lifestyle Patterns: Rural life cycles, housing, and clothing adapted to monsoon winds and rainfall.

                                              Impact of Monsoon in India

     Area                                                            Impact
Agriculture         y Monsoon-dependent agriculture dominates India’s Kharif crops like rice, maize, and pulses. (Over
                      50% workforce dependent on agriculture)
                    y Deficit rains can trigger drought, reduce yields, and affect food security.
Water               y Replenish surface and groundwater sources, refilling rivers, reservoirs, lakes, and aquifers.
Resources           y Vital for drinking water, irrigation and hydropower (Sardar Sarovar, Bhakra Nangal).
                    y Monsoon failures lead to increased cooling demand (ACs) while simultaneously reducing hydro-
                      generation, creating a "Power-Water Nexus" crisis.
Economic            y A strong monsoon boosts rural demand, supports GDP growth, and stabilizes food prices.
Activity            y Poor rains raise inflation, especially in food articles, and reduce farm income, impacting rural
                      consumption.
Disaster Risk       y Excess rainfall causes urban flooding (e.g., Mumbai), riverine floods (Assam, Bihar), while deficits
                      trigger droughts (Bundelkhand).
                    y Sudden heavy rain leads to soil erosion and landslides in hilly regions.
                    y Compound Extremes: Heatwaves followed by sudden mini-cloudbursts.
Biodiversity &      y Monsoons support ecosystem cycles by enabling forest regeneration, sustaining wetlands, and
Ecosystems            triggering breeding seasons for wildlife (Western Ghats amphibians, Sundarbans estuarine species).
Cultural            y Influences agro-based festivals (Onam, Teej), folk traditions, and regional music (Kajri in Uttar
Practices             Pradesh, “Purvaiya” songs in Bhojpuri region) → symbolizes renewal and abundance.

1. Retreating Monsoon/ North East monsoon


                                                   Retreating Monsoon
 y The retreating monsoon, also called the northeast monsoon, marks the withdrawal phase of the southwest monsoon
   from the Indian subcontinent, typically from October to December.
 y As the monsoon trough shifts southward, winds reverse direction, skies clear up, and temperatures rise briefly before
   cooling.
 y While north India becomes dry, parts of the southeastern peninsula, especially Tamil Nadu and Andhra Pradesh,
   receive significant rainfall from cyclonic systems and moisture-laden northeasterly winds.
 y This period plays a vital role in agriculture and water security in southern India.
                                        Factors Influencing Retreating Monsoon

         Factor                                                       Explanation
 Shifting of ITCZ           The ITCZ moves south due to the Sun’s southward shift after the equinox, weakening southwest
 Southward                  monsoon winds.
 Subtropical Westerly       Just as its northward retreat triggers the SW Monsoon, its southward return to the south of the
 Jet (STWJ)                 Himalayas is the primary upper-atmospheric trigger for the SW Monsoon's withdrawal and the
                            establishment of the NE trade winds.
 Reversal of Wind           The dominant winds shift from southwest to northeast, as high pressure develops over the Indian
 Patterns                   subcontinent and low pressure persists over the ocean.
 Clear Skies and            As clouds withdraw, skies clear up; strong sunlight and moist land cause high humidity and
 October Heat               temperature—leading to ‘October Heat’ in many regions.
 Formation of Tropical      Warm waters in the Bay of Bengal and Andaman Sea lead to cyclonic depressions that bring
 Cyclones                   rainfall to eastern and southern coasts (e.g., Tamil Nadu).
 Topography and             Eastern coastal areas like the Coromandel Coast face the northeast winds directly, leading to more
 Coastal Orientation        rainfall during retreating monsoon.

                                         Significance of North East monsoon
 y Rainfall in Tamil Nadu: Provides ~50–60% of the total annual rainfall for the Coromandel Coast.
 y Cyclone Formation: Tropical depressions over Bay of Bengal can become cyclones, influencing weather and rainfall.
 y Water Recharge: Post-monsoon rains help recharge reservoirs, lakes, and groundwater, reducing water stress. This
   period plays a vital role in agriculture and water security in southern India.
 y Climatic Transition: Marks shift from humid monsoon to cooler, dry winter, regulating ecosystems and cropping
   patterns.

2. Variability in Indian Monsoon
The Indian monsoon shows year-to-year changes in its onset, duration, and rainfall leading to unpredictable events such as late
arrival, dry spells, sudden heavy rains, or early withdrawal.
                                            Features of Monsoon Variability

      Feature                                                       Description
 Burst of                Sudden and strong start of rainfall when monsoon winds become active, helped by Somali Jet and
 Monsoon                 Arabian Sea moisture.
 Delayed Onset           Monsoon starts later than usual (normal is June 1 in Kerala) due to weak winds or El Niño effects
 Break in                Temporary pause in rainfall (3–15 days), often due to shift in monsoon trough or weak Bay of
 Monsoon                 Bengal systems. Madden-Julian Oscillation (MJO) and the Boreal Summer Intra-Seasonal
                         Oscillation (BSISO) are the "pulses" that drive it.
 Early                   Monsoon ends earlier than normal (before late Sept), reducing rainfall, often due to warming of
 Withdrawal              NW Indian Ocean
 Rainfall Intensity      Increase in short, heavy rain events, linked to Arabian Sea warming and climate change


3. Changing Rainfall Patterns in India
                                 Factors Responsible For Changing Rainfall Pattern in India

        Factor                                   Explanation                                              Example
 Climate Change            Global warming leads to higher evaporation and moisture,        Cloudbursts and flash floods in
                           increasing the intensity and variability of rainfall.           Uttarakhand and Himachal Pradesh.
 Monsoon Trough            North–south shifting of the monsoon trough changes              Punjab and Uttarakhand get more rain
 Shift                     rainfall distribution across India.                             during northward shifts; Telangana
                                                                                           during southward shift
 Offshore Troughs          Low-pressure zones near the coast pull in moisture from         West coast trough from Gujarat to
                           the sea, causing persistent coastal rain.                       Kerala triggered continuous rainfall.
 Warmer Sea Surface        Heating of Arabian Sea and Bay of Bengal boosts                 Cyclone Tauktae (2021) caused heavy
 Temperatures              cyclone activity and associated rainfall.                       rain in Maharashtra and Gujarat.
 Super El Niño             This "Climate Black Swan" is causing the "re-drawing of         Rajasthan Floods of 2025
                           the risk map", leading to unprecedented events.
 Atmospheric Rivers        Increase in "short, heavy rain events" is being driven by       2018 Kerala floods
                           more intense moisture corridors fueled by a warming
                           Indian Ocean.
 Wind Shear Zones          Shear zones cause uplift of moist air, leading to localised     In July (2024), a shear zone over twenty
                           heavy rainfall.                                                 degrees North caused rain from
                                                                                           Maharashtra to Telangana
 Stronger Northeast        The retreating monsoon is becoming more active due to           ~80% of Tamil Nadu blocks recorded
 Monsoon                   climatic changes, especially in the south.                      increased northeast rainfall.(2011-2021)
 Regional Climate          Some dry regions are receiving more rain, while                 Gujarat sees 30% more rainfall, while
 Shifts                    traditional wet zones are drying up due to long-term            Meghalaya receives less since 1991.
                           changes.

                               Impact of Humanised Landscapes on Rainfall Pattern
 “Humanizing" of landscapes refers to anthropogenic modifications such as deforestation, urbanization, and intensive agriculture.
 Such large-scale modifications of the landscape often interact with natural drivers and exacerbate natural variability.

        Impact                                                           Description
 Reduced                     Deforestation induced decreased evapotranspiration → reduces the "recycled component" of
 Evapotranspiration          rainfall → downward trend in monsoon totals.
 Albedo and Surface          Clearing forests exposes darker surfaces, such as asphalt or concrete in "humanized" zones →
 Energy Balance              absorb more heat (decreased albedo) → alters thermal gradient between land and sea that drives the
                             monsoon winds.
 Desertification             ~30% of India’s land is undergoing degradation → reduces soil moisture + exacerbates local
                             heatwaves → destabilizes regional pressure patterns.
 Convective                  The Urban Heat Island effect’s excess heat pushes rain-bearing clouds higher, triggering enhanced
 Amplification               convective activity and causing extreme rainfall events.
 Aerosol                     Industrial and vehicular pollutants act as cloud condensation nuclei, which can modify cloud
 Concentrations              formation → erratic precipitation patterns.
 Moisture Holding            India’s average temperature has risen by 0.7°C since 1901, increasing the atmosphere's capacity to
 Capacity                    hold moisture → shorter, more intense rainfall spells followed by prolonged dry periods.
 Warming of the              The Indian Ocean’s sea surface temperature has risen by 1°C (1951–2015), surpassing the global
 Indian Ocean                average → influences temperature gradient between land and sea → often weakening the overall
                             monsoon flow while fueling more frequent intense cyclones.
As climate change disrupts traditional thermal gradients and shifts spatial-temporal rainfall patterns, India must prioritize localized
landscape adaptation like micro-watershed management and agro-climatic zoning. Re-engineering this spatial resource planning is
the geographic imperative required to shield vulnerable regional ecosystems from escalating atmospheric instability.


  Key Natural
Resources across
   the World

chapters.part: Key Natural Resources Across The World
chapters.text:
1            Natural Resources

                                                UPSC Mains PYQs
     1.     Give a geographical explanation of the distribution of off-shore oil reserves of the world.
            How are they different from the on-shore occurrences of oil reserves? (2025)
     2.     Discuss the multi-dimensional implications of uneven distribution of mineral oil in the
            world. (2021)
     3.     Account for the present location of iron and steel industries away from the source of raw
            material, by giving examples. (2020)
     4.     Petroleum refineries are not necessarily located nearer to crude oil producing areas,
            particularly in many of the developing countries. Explain its implications. (2017)

Natural resources are naturally occurring biotic and abiotic assets that support human well-being, possess intrinsic ecological
value independent of human use, and provide essential ecosystem services such as provisioning, regulating, supporting, and
cultural functions.
Significance of Natural Resources
       Aspect                            Explanation                                          Example
 Economic                Foundation for agriculture, industry, and        Iron ore from Odisha supports India’s steel
 Development             infrastructure.                                  industry.
                         Note: “Resource Curse" (Paradox of Plenty): Regions with abundant natural resources (Sub-Saharan
                         Africa/ certain Indian states) often face slower economic growth and poor governance.
 Employment              Provides livelihoods in mining, farming,         Coal mining in Jharkhand employs thousands in the
 Generation              forestry, and fisheries.                         energy sector.
 National                Strategic access to fuels and minerals           Uranium reserves in Andhra Pradesh support
 Security                enhances sovereignty and defense.                India’s nuclear energy program.
 Strategic and           Critical minerals are indispensable for          Lithium reserves in Jammu & Kashmir and rare
 Technological           advanced technologies, clean energy              earth elements support India's EV, battery, and
 Security                transition, and resilient supply chains,         semiconductor ambitions.
                         reducing dependence on external sources.
 Ecological              Maintains biodiversity, regulates climate, and   Forests in the Western Ghats regulate rainfall and
 Balance                 supports ecosystems.                             conserve biodiversity.
 Human                   Meets essential needs like food, water,          Himalayan rivers provide freshwater to millions
 Welfare                 shelter, and energy.                             across North India.

1. Iron Ore
Iron ore is a naturally occurring mineral from which metallic iron (Fe) is extracted, primarily used in steel production.
It occurs mainly in the forms of Hematite (Fe₂O₃), Magnetite (Fe₃O₄), Limonite and Siderite and is the backbone of
industrial development.
                                   Distribution of Iron Ore in the World


   Western Australia: World's largest reserves in the Pilbara region; Leading exporter to China and East Asia.
   Eastern Brazil: Carajás hosts massive itabirite iron ore deposits; Second-largest global exporter.
   Kursk Region, Russia: One of the world’s largest iron ore basins (Kursk Magnetic Anomaly).
   North-Central China: Major deposits in Hebei and Liaoning; China is world's largest consumer and imports high grade
    ore (domestic ore is low-grade).
   Simandou Mine in Guinea: World's largest untapped high-grade iron ore deposit → Geopolitical Leverage of Africa.

                                        Distribution of Iron Ore in India


   Odisha:
         India’s largest iron ore (~50%) producing state with major high-grade hematite deposits.
         Key mining districts include Keonjhar, Sundargarh, and Mayurbhanj.
         Important mines are Barbil, Joda, and Banspani.
         Supplies to steel plants and exports via Paradip and Haldia ports.
   Chhattisgarh:
         Known for the Bailadila hills in Dantewada and Bastar, which contain some of the best quality hematite.
         It supplies raw material to Bhilai Steel Plant and is a major export hub via Vishakhapatnam port.
   Jharkhand
         Historical centre for iron ore mining rich in both hematite and magnetite ores.
         Key mining districts include Singhbhum (Noamundi, Gua), and Singhbhum Shear Zone.
   Karnataka
         The Ballari-Hospet region is a key mining belt, along with Chitradurga and Tumkur districts.
   Goa
         Known for low-grade lateritic iron ore (mainly for export).
         Mining concentrated in North Goa.
Note: India’s iron ore distribution is significant, but its utility for the steel industry is limited by the geographical absence of
metallurgical coal, necessitating heavy imports from Australia.


                                   Factors contributing to the distribution of Iron Ore

                    Factors                                         India                                 World
Geological Structure: Iron ore is                     Singhbhum Craton                  The Pilbara region in Australia has vast
concentrated in ancient cratons, shields, and         (Jharkhand–Odisha belt) hosts     iron ore deposits within Archean BIFs.
banded iron formations (BIFs) formed during           extensive BIFs rich in
Precambrian times.                                    hematite.
Tectonic & Climatic Stability: Tectonically           The Dharwar Craton in             The Brazilian Shield has remained
stable regions preserve ore deposits over             southern India has supported      geologically stable since the Precambrian
millions of years by protecting them from             long-term preservation of         era.
metamorphism, weathering, and erosion.                iron-bearing rocks.
Topography: Rolling hills and gently                  Keonjhar and Sundargarh           The Minas Gerais highlands in Brazil
sloping terrains allow easy exposure and              districts in Odisha.              provide favorable topography with
extraction of iron ore through open-cast                                                iron-rich ridges supporting extensive
mining methods.                                                                         mining.
Climatic Conditions: Arid/semi-arid regions           Iron ore mining in Bailadila      Western Australia has a dry climate
allow year-round mining, while high rainfall          (Chhattisgarh) is seasonal due    →efficient open-cast mining.
can cause leaching or hinder extraction.              to heavy monsoon rains.
Infrastructure & Accessibility: Mining                Barbil-Joda mining belt in        In South Africa, iron ore from the Sishen
viability improves with proximity to railways,        Odisha is well-connected to       mine is transported via a dedicated rail
roads, and ports, which enable efficient              Paradip Port, enabling bulk       corridor to the Saldanha Bay port.
domestic use and export logistics.                    exports.
Beneficiation Technology: Effective distribution      The Bailadila Iron Ore Mines      In Brazil, Vale extensively processes
of iron ore is expanding because new technologies     and NMDC have adopted             low-grade itabirite ores through
allow for the use of low-grade fines and "slimes,"    beneficiation and slurry          beneficiation.
which were previously considered waste.               pipeline technologies

                                                     Significance of Iron Ore

                         Significance                                                          Example
Industrial Base: Iron ore is the foundation of steel                   Iron ore fuels industrial hubs like Eastern China and
production, which drives construction, infrastructure,                 Germany.
transport, and manufacturing sectors across the globe.
Regional Development: Iron ore mining promotes regional                The Barbil-Joda belt (Odisha) saw road, rail, and township
economic development through infrastructure creation,                  development.
employment, and urbanization in mineral-rich belts.
Strategic Resource: Iron ore is vital for national security as it      SAIL → raw material security for steel sector and supply
supports the defense, railway, and heavy industries that               defence-grade steel for strategic infrastructure and defence
underpin sovereignty and critical infrastructure.                      manufacturing.
Economic Stability: Export earnings from iron ore help                 The mining sector contributes about 7-10% of the real
countries manage trade balances, invest in social sectors, and         GSDP of Odisha.
attract FDI into mining and related industries.
Green Steel Transition: Iron ore is being re-evaluated based on        JSW commissioned India's largest commercial-scale green
its suitability for Direct Reduced Iron (DRI) using Green              hydrogen plant in Vijayanagar, Karnataka
Hydrogen instead of coal.

                                          Environmental Issues of Iron Ore Mining

                              Issue                                                             Example
Land Degradation: Open-cast mining causes deforestation,                In Keonjhar (Odisha, India), mining has caused forest
removal of topsoil, and permanent alteration of natural                 loss and soil erosion.
landforms
Pollution: Mining releases dust, heavy metals, and effluents,           In Bellary (Karnataka, India), unchecked mining led to
polluting air, rivers, and groundwater, with health and                 severe air and water pollution.
ecological impacts


 Biodiversity Loss: Mining in sensitive ecological zones               In the Western Ghats, mining has led to fragmentation of
 destroys habitats, disrupts wildlife corridors, and affects           habitats of endemic species like Lion-tailed macaque.
 endemic and endangered species.
 Waste Disposal: Overburden and tailings, if unmanaged, can            In Goa (India), tailings from iron ore mines have polluted
 leach harmful substances into soil and water, causing long-           paddy fields and rivers.
 term land degradation.
Note: The Shah Commission revealed extensive illegal extraction and environmental violations, triggering mining suspensions and
reforms in Goa and Odisha (Keonjhar–Sundargarh).

2. Petroleum & Mineral Oil
Petroleum (or crude oil) is a naturally occurring fossil fuel found in underground rock formations (Sedimentary Basins),
primarily composed of hydrocarbons. Mineral oil, derived from refined petroleum, is a colorless, odorless oil used in cosmetics,
pharmaceuticals, and lubricants.

                Distribution in the World                                            Distribution in India
 y Middle East (Saudi Arabia, Iran, Iraq): Holds one of          y Mumbai High: Largest oil-producing basin in India;
   the world’s largest proven reserves and remains a major         Produces over ~25% to 35% of India's crude
   production region.                                            y Assam-Arakan Basin: Oldest oil-producing region;
 y Russia: Major oil producer with vast Siberian reserves          Contributes ~14% of national crude oil production and ~10%
   (Western Siberia Basin).                                        of total natural gas production.
 y USA (Texas, Gulf of Mexico, Alaska): World's largest          y Cambay Basin: Significant onshore oil and gas fields
   oil producer due to conventional and shale oil boom.            including Ankleshwar and Kalol.
 y Canada (Alberta): Rich in oil sands and conventional          y Krishna-Godavari Basin: Major offshore exploration zone
   reserves.                                                       with both oil and natural gas fields
 y Venezuela: Largest proven crude oil reserves globally         y Rajasthan: Emerging inland producer with discoveries like
   (Orinoco Belt).                                                 Mangala, Bhagyam, Aishwariya (Cairn India).

2.1 Onshore Vs Offshore oil reserves
Offshore oil reserves cluster along continental shelves due to marine sedimentation and structural traps, contrasting with onshore's
continental basin dominance.


   Aspect                     Onshore Oil Reserves                                     Offshore Oil Reserves
Location           On land (continental interiors, basins, fold belts)   Beneath seabed on continental shelves, slopes & rises
Global Share       Account for majority of proven reserves and           Account for the minority share.
                   production.
Geological         Intracratonic basins, stratigraphic traps on stable   Passive margins, rifted margins, salt domes, pre-salt
Setting            cratons                                               plays
Typical Field      Predominantly Smaller to medium fields; more          Often giant fields (e.g., Brazil pre-salt: oil found
Size               fragmented                                            beneath thick layers of salt) >10 bn barrels
Depth              Shallow to moderate depths (few hundred metres        Shallow (<200m) to ultra-deepwater (>1500m) →
and Cost           to ~5 km)→ Lower Cost                                 Higher Cost
Development        Faster development, lower logistical complexity       Longer lead time, higher technical & weather-related
Time & Risk                                                              risks
Environmental      Affects land, soil & groundwater                      Higher risk of marine oil spills; impacts marine
Impact                                                                   ecosystems
Major              Saudi Arabia (Ghawar), US shale (Permian              Brazil (Santos pre-salt), Gulf of Mexico (Mars), North
Examples           Basin), Russia (Siberia), Venezuela (Orinoco)         Sea (Brent), West Africa (Angola-Nigeria), Persian
                                                                         Gulf (Safaniya); Stabroek Block in Guyana is the
                                                                         world’s most significant new offshore oil frontier


                               Factors Contributing to the Distribution of Petroleum

                       Factors                                            India                                World
Plate Tectonics & Basin Evolution: Ancient passive           Mumbai Offshore Basin evolved         The North Sea Basin in
margins, rift basins, and foreland basins are favorable      along India’s passive western         Europe formed due to rifting.
for oil accumulation.                                        continental margin.
Geological Formation: Petroleum forms in sedimentary         Cambay Basin in Gujarat is            The Persian Gulf region lies
basins with organic-rich source rocks, porous                India’s key onshore sedimentary       over vast Mesozoic
reservoirs, and impermeable traps.                           zone.                                 sedimentary basins.
Subsurface Temperature & Pressure: Petroleum forms at        In Rajasthan’s Barmer Basin           North Sea Basin
depths of 2–5 km under specific temperature-pressure         (India) →discoveries like the
conditions. Organic matter converts to kerogen →             Mangala field.
"cooks" into oil/gas within the “Oil Window”
Technological Access: Deepwater drilling, fracking,          Cairn Oil & Gas, India uses           Brazil’s pre-salt fields in the
and seismic imaging help exploit challenging reserves.       enhanced oil recovery (EOR).          Atlantic require ultra-deep
                                                                                                   drilling.


                         Implications of Uneven Distribution of Mineral Oil in the World

    Aspect                             Description                                               Example
Economic             Countries lacking oil reserves depend heavily         India imports over 85% of its crude oil, impacting
                     on imports, increasing fiscal strain.                 its current account balance.
                     Oil-exporting nations become over-reliant on          Venezuela’s economy collapsed due to oil price
                     petroleum revenues, risking instability.              crashes and poor diversification.


 Geopolitical          Access to oil shapes strategic alliances and         China invested heavily in oil infrastructure and
                       global power dynamics.                               partnerships in Angola and Russia to secure its
                                                                            energy needs.
                       Oil-rich regions often become flashpoints for        The Gulf War (1990-91) was triggered after Iraq
                       conflict and are vulnerable to foreign               invaded Kuwait, partly to control its vast oil
                       intervention.                                        reserves.
 Socio-Political       Unequal oil wealth can lead to inequality,           Nigeria’s “resource curse” results in poverty amid
                       poverty, civil war and social unrest.                oil riches → Paradox of Plenty
 Technological         Importing nations innovate in renewables to          India invests in ethanol blending and EVs to cut
                       reduce oil dependency.                               import reliance.
                       Oil extraction tech like fracking and offshore       Oil extraction tech like fracking and offshore
                       drilling is controlled by a few nations.             drilling are controlled by few nations.

India’s Tool for mitigating the Uneven Distribution risks
 Key                   y NELP (New Exploration Licensing Policy), 1999: Opened India's upstream hydrocarbon sector to
 Hydrocarbon             private and foreign investment through competitive bidding, ending ONGC/OIL's monopoly in
 Exploration             exploration.

                         regime, revenue-sharing model, and marketing freedom to simplify exploration and boost domestic
                         hydrocarbon production.
                       y OALP (Open Acreage Licensing Policy), 2017: Enabled companies to identify and bid for

                       y Policy Framework for Exploration and Exploitation of Unconventional Hydrocarbons, 2018:
                         Allowed operators to explore and produce shale oil, shale gas, CBM, and other unconventional
                         hydrocarbons under existing contracts.
 Strategic             Strategic Petroleum Reserve (SPR) Phase-I: Underground crude oil storage facilities at
 Petroleum             Visakhapatnam (Andhra Pradesh), Mangaluru and Padur (Karnataka) with a combined capacity of 5.33
 Reserves (SPR)        MMT.
 Refining              India is now the "Refining Hub of Asia," with a capacity exceeding domestic demand, allowing for the
 Capacity              export of refined products even while importing 85% of crude.

2.2. Petrochemicals
Petrochemicals (or petroleum distillates) are derived from crude oil/natural gas through refining and include polymers, synthetic
fibres, and performance plastics. In India, they are mainly concentrated in Gujarat (Dahej, Jamnagar), Maharashtra (Raigad,
Thane), and Tamil Nadu (Manali), due to proximity to refineries, ports, and SEZs.

Classification on the basis of Quality

   Sweet vs Sour (Sulphur Content) → Sweet = Less Sulfur = Easier Refining; Sour = More Sulfur = Costlier Refining
           Sweet: Brent Crude (North Sea); Sour: Middle Eastern crude grades
   Light vs Heavy (Density (API Gravity)) → Light = High API = More Valuable Fuels; Heavy = Low API = More Residual
    Products

Note: Indian refineries (like Jamnagar) are world-class specifically because they can process "Heavy-Sour" crude into "Light-Sweet"
products.


                        Factors responsible for location of petrochemical industries

Proximity          plastic, chemical, and fibre intermediates.     textile industries.
Infrastructure &   SEZs, dedicated zones, and government           SEZs and industrial zones, backed by incentives, offer
Policy Support     incentives promote large-scale                  infrastructure, regulatory ease, and tax benefits to boost
                   petrochemical investments.                      large-scale investments.

3. Natural Gas


Natural gas is a fossil fuel primarily composed of methane (CH₄), with smaller amounts of ethane, propane, and other hydrocarbons.
It can be found in conventional reservoirs, shale and tight formations (unconventional gas), and coal beds (CBM).
 Distribution of Natural Gas in the World                                    Distribution of Natural Gas in India

   via major pipelines to Europe and Asia.                                     India’s largest offshore gas-producing basin;

 and cement. By product of extraction → Helium → role in MRIs,                   natural gas as key input.

4. Rare Earth Minerals (REMs)
Rare earth elements (REEs) are 17 special metals, including 15 lanthanides plus scandium and yttrium, that have similar properties
and usually occur together in nature.


      Distribution of REMs in the World                              Distribution of REMs in India
y China: Has ~36-38% of world REM reserves, and      India has the third-largest REMs reserves in the world but it
  accounts for ~60-70% of global REM production;     contributes less than 1 % of global REMs mining.
  Bayan Obo is the world's largest REM deposit.      y Kerala: Highest concentration of monazite is in beach placer
y Australia: Has ~5-6% of global reserves, mainly       sands; estimated 0.892 million tonnes of monazite resources in
  light REEs                                            Kollam district, Kerala; Key sites are Chavara (Kollam) and
y Russia: Has ~4-6% of reserves, mostly in Kola         Alappuzha.
  Peninsula/Murmansk and further east, but           y Tamil Nadu: Manavalakurichi and Kanyakumari are key areas with
  underdeveloped.                                       ilmenite and monazite deposits.
y Africa: Hosts several emerging rare earth mining   y Andhra Pradesh: Coastal districts like Visakhapatnam and Vizag
  sites such as Steenkampskraal (South Africa) and      have monazite-rich beach sands.
  Songwe Hills (Malawi).                             y Odisha: Ganjam and nearby coastal zones have untapped potential
                                                        in placer monazite deposits.

                               Factor                                                       Examples
Geological setting: Occur in specific rock types like carbonatites,    Kerala’s beach sands are rich in monazite.
alkaline intrusives, and placer sands.
Plate Tectonics: Occur in cratons and rift zones with magmatic         Precambrian shields of Canada.
activity.
Geochemical: Substitute into compatible minerals due to their ionic    Hosted in minerals like bastnaesite, monazite, and
size and charge.                                                       xenotime across global deposits.
Weathering: Weathering of primary rocks forms secondary clay-          Ion-adsorption clays in southern China and laterites
rich REE deposits.                                                     in Brazil.

                                 Strategic Significance of Rare Earth Minerals (REMs)

                           Significance                                                      Example
National Security: REMs are critical for advanced defence systems      Neodymium is used in missile actuators, and
such as radars, precision-guided missiles, and stealth technology.     Samarium in fighter jet engines.
Space Sector: Enable development of heat-resistant alloys, high-       Cerium was essential for NASA’s Space Shuttle
performance magnets, and control systems.                              Programme.
Geopolitical Leverage: Nations with control over REMs gain             China controls around 85% of global REM refining,
strategic bargaining power in global politics and trade.               often using it for diplomatic leverage.
Energy Security: REMs are essential for components of wind             Dysprosium and Terbium in wind turbine magnets
turbines, nuclear reactors, and energy-efficient systems.              improve efficiency and reliability.
Critical Infrastructure Resilience: REMs support resilient,            Samarium-Cobalt magnets used in Indian Navy’s
high-tech infrastructure for energy, defense, and transport.           submarine propulsion systems.

                          Challenges to Production of Rare Earth Minerals (REMs) in India

                                Issue                                                    Substantiation
Low Economic Viability: REEs are abundant, but their low               Most Indian REM deposits are beach placers (such
concentration in extractable forms and high environmental costs        as, monazite in Kerala, Tamil Nadu, Odisha), which
make domestic mining unprofitable.                                     are lower-grade.
Global Supply Chain Dependence: India relies heavily on China          Around 85-90% of global REM processing is done
for REM imports, making it vulnerable to geopolitical risks and        by China, including key inputs for the Indian
market volatility.                                                     electronics and defense sectors.
Outdated Processing Technology: India lacks advanced separation        China uses advanced solvent extraction, while India
and purification technologies needed to process REMs efficiently       still relies on older, less efficient techniques for REM
and sustainably at industrial scale.                                   extraction.
Regulatory Constraints: Strict environmental clearances and            Coastal REM-rich states like Kerala and Tamil Nadu
radiation concerns delay mining projects, particularly for monazite-   face protests over environmental and livelihood
bearing beach sands.                                                   concerns.
Lack of Dedicated Policy Framework: India lacks a clear and            Draft National Mineral Policy (2019) mentions
coordinated REM policy, slowing progress on exploration,               REMs, but no targeted, time-bound policy for
production, and global supply chain integration.                       high-tech mineral production exists.

                                        Impacts of Uneven Distribution of REMs

                              Aspect                                                         Example
Global Supply Concentration: China controls most of the global         China accounted for about 70% of rare earth mineral
REM supply, creating dependence and limited access for other           (REM) production in 2023.
nations.

face risks from export bans, trade wars, or political tensions         impacted Western and Japanese chip and defense
affecting REM flow.                                                    sectors.

Indian Initiatives
    Resource Diplomacy: India joined the US-led MSP in 2023–24 specifically to secure REM supply chains and bypass the
     China monopoly.
    2023 MMDR Amendment: Opens up critical mineral exploration and mining to greater private participation.
    Urban Mining for Strategic Autonomy: Recovery of REMs from e-waste is emerging as a key strategy to reduce import
     dependence and enhance India's strategic autonomy in critical technologies.

4.1 Lithium
Lithium is a highly reactive, lightweight alkali metal crucial for energy storage, particularly in lithium-ion batteries used
in electric vehicles and renewable power systems. India's total lithium and battery imports reached US$1.27 billion in Q1
2026 , with China holding a dominant 84.32% share, raising concerns over supply security amid geopolitical tensions.

                                  Distribution of Lithium Reserves in India


     Jammu & Kashmir (Reasi District – Salal-Haimana): Inferred reserves of ~6 million tonnes discovered in 2023, making
      it one of the largest single-site lithium deposits globally.
     Rajasthan (Degana, Nagaur District): Large lithium reserves identified; preliminary studies suggest the site could potentially
      meet up to 80% of India’s future lithium demand.
     Karnataka (Mandya District): Lithium occurrences are traced in the pegmatite belts; currently at the early exploration
      stage.
     Jharkhand, Andhra Pradesh, and Chhattisgarh: Minor lithium deposits found across multiple sites; active exploration
      underway by the Geological Survey of India (GSI) and private entities.


                                       Significance of Lithium Reserves in India
 y Energy Security: Reduces import dependence for lithium, a key mineral for batteries and strategic technologies.
 y Electric Vehicle (EV) Push: Vital for achieving EV adoption targets and developing indigenous EV and battery
   ecosystems.
 y Economic Growth: Promotes domestic battery manufacturing, attracts investment, and generates employment.
 y Renewable Energy Integration: Facilitates grid-scale storage solutions crucial for stable solar and wind power
   utilisation.
 y Trade Deficit Reduction: Minimizes foreign exchange outflow by reducing reliance on lithium imports.

                                         Challenges in Extracting Lithium in India

                           Challenges                                                         Example
 Technical and Geological: Indian reserves are mostly in hard         The J&K Salal-Haimana reserve is clay-based, where
 rock or clay forms, needing advanced technology. India lacks         commercial extraction methods are not yet proven
 battery-grade refining facilities and skilled workforce.             globally.
 Environmental & Social: Lithium mining demands high water            Lithium exploration efforts in the Kamjong district,
 and energy. Causes pollution, land degradation, and                  Manipur, have been stalled due to local resistance.
 community displacement.
 Political & Security: Key reserves like J&K are in                   The Salal-Haimana site lies close to the Line of Control.
 geopolitically sensitive areas. Security risks and local tensions
 delay operations.
 Regulatory & Economic: No clear lithium-specific mining              Policy and clearance delays have stalled lithium mining
 policy. Long gestation periods from exploration to extraction        despite major discoveries in 2023.
 deter investors.

2              Nuclear Resources

Nuclear resources include naturally occurring radioactive elements like uranium and thorium, and artificially produced materials
like plutonium used for energy, defence, and research.


                         Uranium                                                          Thorium
 y Distribution in the World: Kazakhstan, Canada,               y Distribution in the World: India, Australia, Brazil, USA,
   Australia, Namibia, Russia, Uzbekistan                         Norway, and South Africa
 y Distribution in India: Jharkhand (Singhbhum Shear            y Distribution in India: Kerala (Palakkad and Kollam
   Zone, Turamdih), Telangana (Lambapur-Peddagattu,               districts), Tamil Nadu (Manavalakurichi and Kanyakumari),
   Chitrial), Andhra Pradesh (Tummalapalle in Kadapa              Odisha (Brahmagiri coast), Andhra Pradesh (Coastal
   district), Meghalaya (Domiasiat and Wakhyn areas)              stretches in Srikakulam and Visakhapatnam)

                                 Factors Leading to Distribution of Nuclear Resources

                                Factor                                                         Example
 Geological Formations:Uranium and thorium are found in stable,            Uranium deposits in India are found in crystalline
 ancient rocks like granites, sandstones, and quartz-pebble                rocks, with maximum share in Andhra Pradesh
 conglomerates.                                                            (Tummalapalle)
 Plate Tectonics: Stable shields and cratons allow accumulation of         The Indian Shield hosts uranium in Jharkhand.
 nuclear minerals.
 Weathering: Weathering of rocks concentrates radioactive elements         Monazite-rich beach sands of Kerala.
 in coastal sands.
 Geochemical concentration: Uranium deposits may be localized in           Singhbhum shear zone or other uranium-bearing
 zones of structural weakness, alteration, and secondary enrichment        structural zones
 Hydrothermal Activity: Heated water circulation concentrates              Domiasiat/Wahkyn uranium occurrences in
 uranium in fissures and fault zones.                                      Meghalaya


                                           Significance of Nuclear Resources

                          Significance                                                          Example
Energy Security: Nuclear power provides a stable, large-scale, and         Kudankulam Nuclear Power Plant
low-carbon energy source, reducing dependence on fossil fuels.
Clean Energy Transition: Low greenhouse gas emissions help                 India’s Net Zero 2070 commitment includes
meet climate targets.                                                      expansion of nuclear power.
Strategic Importance: Enhances defence capability and acts as a            India’s Pokhran-II nuclear tests (1998) asserted
deterrent.                                                                 strategic autonomy.
Scientific Development: Promotes research in nuclear science,              Bhabha Atomic Research Centre (BARC) is a hub for
materials, and reactor technology.                                         India’s nuclear R&D.
Medical Applications: Radioisotopes used in cancer treatment               Tata Memorial Centre, Mumbai, uses nuclear
and diagnostics.                                                           medicine for cancer care.
Space Utility: used in long-duration space missions and deep-space         NASA’s Voyager missions were powered by
probes                                                                     plutonium-based RTGs (radioisotope thermoelectric
                                                                           generators).
Employment Generation: Boosts the local economy and creates                NPCIL’s Gorakhpur Plant (Haryana) is expected to
skilled jobs.                                                              create around 8,000 jobs during execution.

                              Challenges faced by India in harnessing Nuclear Resources

                              Challenges                                                          Example
Limited Uranium Reserves: India has modest natural uranium                     In FY21 alone, India imported nearly 2,000 MTU
deposits, affecting fuel supply for reactors.                                  from Canada and Kazakhstan.
Underutilized Thorium: India has large thorium reserves but lacks              The Advanced Heavy Water Reactor (AHWR) is
proven commercial thorium reactor technology.                                  still in the prototype stage.
Nuclear Liability Law: Stringent liability norms deter foreign suppliers       Civil Liability for Nuclear Damage Act, 2010
due to high compensation risks.                                                limits foreign investments and collaborations.
High Capital Cost: Setting up nuclear plants requires large investment         Kudankulam Plant facing significant cost
and long gestation periods.                                                    overruns.
Safety Concerns: Public fears over accidents due to global incidents like      Jaitapur Nuclear Power Project has faced
Chernobyl and Fukushima.                                                       persistent protests citing safety risks.

Initiatives Taken by India

           Initiative                                            Substantiation / example
 Three-stage nuclear                 To overcome limited uranium and abundant thorium: PHWRs using natural uranium,
 programme                           fast breeder reactors, and eventually thorium-based systems.
 Fuel import                         India diversified uranium sourcing after the civil nuclear agreement era, with imports
 diversification                     from countries such as Canada and Kazakhstan helping stabilize reactor fuel supply.
 International safeguards and        IAEA safeguards and bilateral fuel agreements has expanded access to technology and
 cooperation                         fuel despite earlier restrictions.
 Fleet-mode construction and         India has adopted fleet-mode construction for reactors to reduce delays, improve
 standardised reactor design         standardisation, and lower unit costs.
 Budgetary support                   The Union Budget 2025–26 announced a ₹20,000 crore mission for Small Modular
 for SMRs                            Reactors (SMRs), signalling state support for advanced nuclear deployment.
 Indigenisation of nuclear           India has promoted progressive indigenisation of components, fabrication, and supply
 industry                            chains to reduce import dependence and capital costs.
 Public outreach and safety          India has strengthened regulatory and public-safety discourse after global accidents like
 regulation                          Chernobyl and Fukushima, helping address safety concerns around nuclear plants.


The Sustainable Harnessing and Advancement of Nuclear Energy for Transforming India (SHANTI) Act, 2025:
It repealed the Atomic Energy Act (AEA), 1962 and the Civil Liability for Nuclear Damage Act (CLNDA), 2010, and has the
following features:
y Statutory recognition of the Atomic Energy Regulatory Board for independent nuclear safety oversight.
y Private sector participation is permitted in nuclear plant construction, operation and power generation, subject to licensing
   and regulatory oversight.
y Activities Under Exclusive Central Government Purview:
      ♦ Enrichment or isotopic separation of prescribed or radioactive substances
      ♦ Management of spent fuel (reprocessing, handling heavy waste, etc.)
      ♦ Any other facilities or activities specifically notified by the Government.
y Licensing and Safety Oversight for nuclear energy production and use.
y Regulation of non-power nuclear applications for peaceful purposes.
y Civil Liability Framework for addressing nuclear damage.
      ♦ A clear, graded liability system for nuclear operators.
      ♦ Liability caps: ₹3,000 crore (large plants), ₹1,500 crore (medium), ₹100 crore (SMRs).
      ♦ The government bears liability beyond the cap through a Nuclear Liability Fund.
      ♦ Removes statutory supplier liability by eliminating the mandatory right of recourse against suppliers.
y Territorial scope of compensation extended to nuclear damage in foreign territories caused by incidents in India, subject to
   specified conditions.
y Dispute Redressal Mechanism through an Atomic Energy Redressal Advisory Council
y Appellate Tribunal for Electricity is designated as the appellate authority.
Dedicated Nuclear Damage Claims Commission for adjudicating severe nuclear damage cases.
