The South China Sea has assumed great geopolitical significance in the present context. Comment. (2016)
What are the economic significance of the discovery of oil in the Arctic Sea and its possible environmental consequences? (2015)

chapters.part: Location Of Industries In The World
chapters.text:
1            Primary Industries

Primary Industries involve the direct extraction and utilization of natural resources from the Earth, such as agriculture, fishing,
forestry, and mining. They form the base of an economy, especially in developing nations like India, and are heavily influenced
by physical geography like climate, soil, and terrain.

                                                  UPSC Mains PYQs
      1.    What are non-farm primary activities? How are these activities related to physiographic
            features in India? Discuss with suitable examples. (2025)
      2.    Despite India being one of the countries of the Gondwanaland, its mining industry
            contributes much less to its Gross Domestic Product (GDP) in percentage. Discuss. (2021)
      3.    Define blue revolution, explain the problems and strategies for pisciculture development in
            India. (2018)
      4.    Mention the advantages of the cultivation of pulses because of which the year 2016 was
            declared as the International Year of Pulses by the United Nations. (2017)


                               Factors Responsible for the Location of Primary Industries

      Factor                                  Explanation                                               Example
 Natural               Presence of raw materials like minerals, forests, fertile        Coal mining in Jharkhand and fishing
 Resources             soil, and marine life.                                           along Kerala coast.
 Climate               Specific crops/livelihoods need suitable rainfall,               Tea in Assam needs a humid, cool climate.
                       temperature, and seasons.
 Topography            Flat plains aid farming, and hills suit plantations or           Rice in Indo-Gangetic plains; coffee in
                       forestry.                                                        Karnataka hills.
 Water                 Rivers, canals, and rainfall influence agriculture, livestock,   Wheat in Punjab via canal irrigation.
 Availability          and fisheries.
 Labour                Manual work in farming, fishing, etc. needs abundant             Tea estates in Darjeeling use local labour.
 Supply                labour.
 Transport             Connectivity helps move produce to markets or                    Fish market connectivity
 Access                processors.
 Capital &             Access to credit, fertilizers, and tools is essential for        NABARD loans support rural agriculture
 Inputs                productivity.                                                    and fishing.
 Government            Subsidies, MSPs, irrigation schemes attract farmers to           PMKSY promotes irrigation in drought-
 Policy                certain regions.                                                 prone Maharashtra.

1.1 Agriculture
Pulses
India is currently the world’s largest producer and consumer of pulses (NITI Aayog report on “Strategies and Pathways for
Accelerating Growth in Pulses towards the Goal of Atmanirbharta”). They are often termed the "Nutritional Anchors" of the
Indian vegetarian diet, have transitioned from a "secondary crop" to a strategic tool for India’s climate-resilient agriculture and
self-sufficiency.
                                               Advantages of Pulse Cultivation

     Aspect                               Description                                            Substantiation
 Soil                Pulses are leguminous plants that fix atmospheric             Pulses can biologically fix about 30–300 kg
 Restoration         nitrogen, enhancing soil fertility and reducing the           nitrogen/ha/year.
                     need for chemical fertilizers.


 Water               They are drought-resistant and consume significantly         Approximately 90-95% of pulses in India are
 Efficiency          less water compared to guzzling crops like rice or           grown under dry or dryland agriculture.
                     sugarcane.
 Nutritional         A critical source of plant-based protein, dietary fiber,     Pulses contain about 20–25% protein (Horse
 Security            and essential minerals for a majority-vegetarian             gram- ~22gm, Cow pea- ~24gm), nearly twice
                     population.                                                  that of cerea ls

 Crop                Ideal for crop rotation and intercropping (e.g., with        The National Food Security Mission
 Diversification     wheat or maize), which maintains soil health and breaks      (NFSM–Pulses) promotes pulse-based crop
                     pest cycles.                                                 diversification to improve soil health and
                                                                                  reduce cereal monoculture.

Contemporary Developments
   Mission for Atmanirbharta in Pulses:
           It aims to scale up domestic pulses production to 350 lakh tonnes and expand the cultivation area to 310 lakh
            hectares by 2030–31.
           It aims to ensure 100 percent procurement of Tur, Urad, and Masoor at MSP for four years.
           A total of 88 lakh free seed kits and 126 lakh quintals of certified seeds will be distributed among farmers.
   Climate-Smart Land Use: As states like Punjab and Haryana face drastic groundwater depletion, there is a deliberate policy
    shift toward pulses to restore aquifers and soil organic carbon.
   Digital Integration: The use of Agri-Tech platforms (e.g., the Cott-Ally app logic being applied to pulses) provides farmers
    with real-time data on pest management and price discovery.
   Tech driven "Protein Revolution": In 2025, pulses are being utilized in biotechnology and food processing (Value Addition)
    to create bio-fortified varieties with higher zinc and iron content.
   Dryland Resilience: Pulse crops are vital to dryland resilience in the Aravalli region, as they help conserve soil moisture,
    enrich degraded soils, and reduce pressure on fragile ecosystems.
The journey from International Year of Pulses 2016 to the "Pulse Self-Sufficiency Mission" of 2026 underscores pulses as the
linchpin of India’s Evergreen Revolution. By scaling up micro-irrigation for pulses and integrating them into Industrial Corridors
for food processing, India can ensure a protein-secure and climate-resilient future.

1.2 Allied Sector Industries
Allied Sector Industries refer to agricultural-supporting activities such as dairy, fisheries, poultry, animal husbandry, sericulture,
and apiculture that supplement farm income, diversify livelihood sources, and enhance nutritional and employment security,
particularly in rural India.

Significance of Allied Sector Industries
    Dimension                               Details                                                Example
 Rural                   Generates non-farm employment for marginal          About 45.76% of the total workforce is engaged in
 Employment              farmers, landless labourers, and women.             agriculture and allied sector during 2022-23.
 Income                  Reduces dependency on seasonal crops and            Fish-rice farming in Bengal enhances resilience.
 Diversification         stabilizes farmer income.
 Export &                India is a leading exporter of buffalo meat,        Seafood exports touched an all-time high of US $8.28
 Economy                 and milk-based products.                            billion in FY 2025-26 (target was to reach USD 14
                                                                             billion by 2025.)
 Sustainability          Promotes circular economy through integrated        Increase in income through Godhan Nyay Yojana in
                         farming, biogas, organic manure, etc.               Chhattisgarh

A. Dairy Farming in India
India ranks 1st in global milk production, contributing 23% of global output, with production rising to 239.3 million tonnes in
2023–24. Dairy contributes ~5% to the national economy and directly employs more than 8 crore farmers. Top milk-producing
states include Uttar Pradesh, Rajasthan, MP, Gujarat, and Andhra Pradesh, together accounting for over half of national output.


                          Factors Influencing the Location of Dairy Farming in India
        Aspect                                  Description                                            Example
Agro-Climatic                Moderate temperatures, humidity, and perennial           Western Maharashtra and Indo-Gangetic
Suitability                  water sources enhance cattle health and fodder           Plains offer ideal climate.
                             cultivation.
Fodder & Water               Regions with irrigated agriculture and                   Punjab and Haryana support green
Resource Base                pasturelands ensure regular fodder and drinking          fodder due to canal irrigation.
                             water supply.
Market Accessibility         Proximity to urban consumption centers reduces           Dairy hubs near Delhi, Mumbai, and
                             transport cost and ensures remunerative returns.         Bengaluru benefit from proximity.
Institutional &              Availability of veterinary services, AI technology,      AMUL in Gujarat and Nandini in
Infrastructure Support       cold chains, rural roads, and milk processing units      Karnataka ensure robust linkages.
                             aids commercial viability.
Socio-Cultural               Traditional animal husbandry practices and               Rural belts of UP, Bihar, and Rajasthan
Practices                    community-based livestock management promote             show cultural affinity.
                             dairy activity.
Policy and Financial         Schemes like Rashtriya Gokul Mission, National           NABARD’s Dairy Entrepreneurship
Support                      Dairy Plan, and easy credit enable expansion and         Development Scheme supports
                             modernization.                                           expansion.

                                Initiatives to Strengthen the Dairy Sector in India
y Rashtriya Gokul Mission: Development and conservation of indigenous bovine breeds to enhance milk productivity.
y National Programme for Dairy Development: Improve milk quality, infrastructure, and increase organized milk
  procurement.
y Livestock Health & Disease Control Programme (LHDCP): Strengthens animal health through vaccination and disease
  surveillance systems.
y Animal Husbandry Infrastructure Development Fund: Encourages private investment in dairy processing and value
  addition infrastructure.
y Kisan Credit Card (KCC) for Dairy Farmers: Provides accessible and affordable institutional credit to livestock and
  dairy farmers.

Challenges Faced by Dairy Farming in India

    Challenge                               Explanation                                              Example
Low Productivity         Predominance of low-yield indigenous breeds,              Average milk yield of Indian cows is 3-8 litres/
                         limited genetic improvement, and poor nutrition.          day vs. 25-40 litres in developed countries.
Input Deficits           Shortage of green/dry fodder, clean water,                India faces a green fodder deficit of about 35%
                         and veterinary services including Artificial              and a dry fodder shortage of around 11%.
                         Insemination (AI).
Infrastructure Gaps      Inadequate cold chain, Bulk Milk Coolers (BMCs),          Only 36% milk is handled by the organized
                         milk testing labs, and rural connectivity hinder          sector.
                         efficiency.
Limited Credit and       Small dairy farmers face difficulty in accessing          Only 15.47% of animals are under insurance
Insurance Access         institutional credit and livestock insurance.             cover.


Fragmented and           Majority of dairy farmers are smallholders with 1–2       70% of India’s milk is produced by small/
Unorganised Sector       animals, lacking scale and bargaining power.              marginal farmers with minimal market
                                                                                   access.
Climatic                 Droughts, heatwaves, and erratic monsoons                 Heat stress reduces milk production of cattle
Vulnerability            impact fodder, water supply, and cattle health.           and buffalo significantly


 Contagious Disease       Frequent outbreaks of infectious diseases reduce         Outbreaks of Foot-and-Mouth Disease
 Threats                  milk yield, increase livestock mortality, raise          (FMD), Brucellosis, and Lumpy Skin
                          treatment costs and affect livestock trade.              Disease (LSD).

B. Fisheries Sector in India
India is the second largest fish producing country with around 8% share in global fish production. Major fish producing states are
Andhra Pradesh, West Bengal, Karnataka. The Union Budget 2025-26, proposed the highest ever total annual budgetary support
of Rs. 2,703.67 crores for the fisheries sector, regarding it as a Sunrise Sector.


Factors Influencing the Location of the Fisheries Sector in India

         Factor                               Explanation                                             Example
 Length & Nature of         Longer coastline and extensive EEZ support             India has 11,098.81 km coastline with
 Coastline                  richer marine fisheries.                               530,000 km² continental shelf area.
 Continental Shelf &        Wider shelves and upwelling promote higher fish        The West coast has a wider shelf, highest
 Upwelling                  productivity.                                          marine landings.
 Inland Water               Abundant rivers, lakes, reservoirs, and floodplains    Inland fisheries contribute more than 75% of
 Resources                  support inland fisheries.                              total fisheries output.
 Infrastructure             Availability of harbours, landing centres, cold        Kerala has 222 marine fishing villages and
                            chains, and transport facilities boosts sectoral       113 inland fishing villages.
                            growth.
 Policy & Institutional     State schemes, subsidies, and the Blue                 Pradhan Mantri Matsya Sampada Yojana
 Support                    Revolution enhance regional capacities.                (PMMSY) drives sectoral growth.

Significance of the Fisheries Industry in India

     Aspect                               Description                                                Example
 Economic           Fisheries contribute significantly to national and            Fisheries contributed around 1.12% of
 Contribution       agricultural GDP, acting as a growth driver in the            national GVA and over 7.26% of agricultural
                    economy.                                                      GVA (2022-23).
 Employment         Provides livelihoods to millions across the country,          Approximately 14 million individuals find
 Generation         especially in rural, coastal, and marginalized                employment directly or indirectly in the
                    communities.                                                  fisheries sector in India.
 Food               Fish is a key source of affordable, high-quality protein      Per capita fish availability rising; inland
 Security           critical to combat malnutrition, especially along coasts      fisheries crucial for nutrition in rural/tribal
                    and riverine areas.                                           areas.


 Exports             India is a major global seafood exporter, contributing           During the financial year 2023-24, India
                     significantly to foreign exchange earnings.                      exported an all-time high volume of seafood
                                                                                      worth Rs.60k crore.
 Blue Economy        Fishery sector is vital to India's Blue Economy vision,          PMMSY and Sagarmala promote marine
 Development         integrating marine resources for sustainable coastal             infrastructure for sustainable fisheries.
                     development.

Challenges Faced by the Fisheries Sector in India
      Aspect                                  Description                                                 Example
 Resource              Unsustainable fishing practices and excessive catch rates          Over 4% of India's fish stocks are
 Depletion             have led to depletion of key marine fish stocks,                   currently facing overfishing, while 8.2%
                       threatening ecosystem balance and fisher livelihoods.              have already been overfished. (Marine
                                                                                          Fish Stock Status Of India 2022 report).
 Climate Change        Rising sea surface temperatures, increased frequency of            Cyclone Yaas (2021) destroyed marine
 Impact                cyclones, marine heatwaves, sea level rise and ocean               fishery assets in Odisha and West Bengal.
                       acidification affect fish distribution, breeding and yields.
 Habitat               Pollution from plastics, industrial effluents, and sewage          Yamuna and Ganga rivers report regular
 Degradation           affects inland and coastal water bodies, harming aquatic           fish kill incidents.
                       ecosystems and reducing fish stock sustainability.
 Shrinking Fish        Urbanization, industrialization, and land use changes              Bengaluru's urban sprawl has led to the
 Farming Areas         have reduced ponds, paddy fields and wetlands                      decline of tanks like Bellandur and Varthur,
                       previously used for aquaculture.                                   reducing inland fish farming space.
 Regulatory and        Fragmented fisheries governance across coastal states              Lack of uniform Marine Fisheries
 Governance            with inconsistent enforcement weakens sustainable                  Regulation Acts (MFRAs) leads to
 Gaps                  management and conservation efforts.                               loopholes exploited by fishers.

                                 Initiative to Strengthen the Fisheries Sector in India
 y Pradhan Mantri Matsya Sampada Yojana (PMMSY): Enhances aquaculture productivity, improves fisheries
   management, establishes integrated aquaparks, and boosts sector growth.
 y Pradhan Mantri Matsya Kisan Samridhi Sah-Yojana (PM-MKSSY): The sub-scheme under PMMSY focused on
   addressing sector weaknesses via financial and technological support to fishers and farmers.
 y Blue Revolution Scheme: An integrated approach aimed at the overall development and sustainable management of
   marine and inland fisheries.
 y Fisheries and Aquaculture Infrastructure Development Fund (FIDF): Provides financial assistance for infrastructure
   creation in both marine and inland fisheries sectors.
 y National Marine Fisheries Policy 2017: A regulatory framework guiding sustainable conservation and management of
   India’s marine fishery resources.
 y The Andaman & Nicobar Tuna Cluster: Aims to develop the islands into a global tuna hub by strengthening deep-sea tuna
   harvesting, modern fishing harbours, cold-chain infrastructure, processing facilities, value addition, and seafood exports
   under the Blue Economy framework.

1.3 Non-farm primary Activities
These are extraction-based pursuits excluding crop cultivation, such as fishing, forestry, mining and quarrying, directly dictated by
India’s physiographic diversity → diversifying rural incomes beyond agriculture.

        Physiographic Feature                                                      Examples
 Coastal Plains & Islands → Intensive          y Tamil Nadu and Gujarat lead in marine fish production.
 marine fisheries and aquaculture due to       y Kerala backwaters and Chilika lagoon enable brackish shrimp farming
 extensive continental shelf                   y Andaman-Nicobar and Lakshadweep yield tuna and pearl collection
 Northern Plains → Inland fisheries            y Ganga-Brahmaputra floodplains and ox-bow lakes
                                               y West Bengal is a leading state in India’s inland fish production
                                               y Riverine sand and clay quarrying thrive on alluvial deposits.


 Peninsular Plateau → mineral wealth           y Jharia-Dhanbad coal, Bailadila iron ore, Singbhum copper, Kolar gold
 due to ancient rock formations                y Granite/quartzite quarrying in Karnataka- Telangana; Bauxite in Chhattisgarh.
 Himalayas & North-Eastern Hills→              y Timber, resin, medicinal herbs and bamboo (conifers in Himachal-
 Temperate and subtropical forests               Uttarakhand; sal-teak in Assam-Meghalaya)
                                               y High-altitude limestone and dolomite mining
                                               y Ladakh yak/pashmina
 Western & Eastern Ghats → evergreen           y Non-timber forest produce (honey, spices, resins, lac)
 forests                                       y Coastal placer deposits enable ilmenite-monazite beach sand mining in Kerala
                                                 and Odisha.
 Thar Desert → gypsum and salt                 y Sambhar Lake supplies ~9-10% national salt
 extraction from inland saline lakes           y Gypsum mining in Jodhpur-Bikaner
                                               y Rajasthan camel dairy

A. Mining
India’s Peninsular Plateau inherits the rich lithospheric legacy of Gondwanaland. While it houses nearly 95% of India's metallic
minerals, the mining industry contributes a stagnant ~2.5% to the national GDP, highlighting a deep-rooted structural and regulatory
paradox.

Potential of India’s Mineral Belts

   Minerals                            Description                                             Substantiation
 Metallic          The Peninsular Plateau is rich in metals due to its       India has the largest iron ore reserves in Asia,
 Minerals          ancient age and igneous/ metamorphic composition.         concentrated in Odisha, Jharkhand, and Chhattisgarh.
 Energy            Coal was formed during the Carboniferous period           Coal accounts for ~55% of India’s energy needs →
 Minerals          when dense forests in Gondwanaland were buried            fuels 74% of power generation.
                   under sediments.
 Non-Metallic      Limestone and mica deposits are ubiquitous across         India is one of the top global producers of mica →
 Resources         the North-Eastern and North-Western belts.                electronics and pharmaceuticals.
 Critical          Significant potential for lithium, REEs, and cobalt       Discovery of 5.9 million tonnes of lithium in Reasi,
 Minerals          in the Himalayan and Peninsular belts.                    J&K, potentially the world's 7th largest reserve.

Factors for GDP-Potential Gap

   Dimension                            Description                                            Impact/Example
 Regulatory &         Complex regulations and delays due to bureaucratic      EIA Notification, Forest Conservation Act 1980 →
 Policy               cholesterol in lease approvals/permits.                 2-3 yr delays for Category A mines
 Environmental        Mining causes severe deforestation, biodiversity        Goa Foundation v UOI (2014 SC): Banned iron
 Concerns             loss, and water pollution, leading to judicial          ore mining post-Shah Commission (illegal
                      intervention.                                           extraction ₹35k Cr)
 Social &             Mineral belts often overlap with tribal habitats and    Niyamgiri Vedanta (2013 SC): Gram Sabha veto
 Conflict             dense forests, leading to displacement and              under FRA 2006
                      resistance.
 Technological        Reliance on outdated techniques and a massive           India’s explored area is minimal compared to its
 Gaps                 gap in deep-seated mineral exploration.                 actual potential, leading to a high mineral import bill.
 Infrastructure       Remote mining sites face poor transportation            Poor last-mile connectivity, Limited port handling
 Deficit              networks and inconsistent power supply, raising         capacity, Inadequate processing.
                      logistics costs.
 Economic             The rapid growth of IT and services has                 Services contribute ~55% to GDP, diluting the
 Diversity            overshadowed traditional industries like mining.        relative percentage share of the primary mining sector.
To bridge this gap, the government has moved from "passive ownership" to "proactive exploration".
 National Mineral Policy 2019: Aims to grant "industry status" to mining and create dedicated Mineral Corridors for cheaper
    transportation.
 MMDR Amendment Act 2023: Removed the government monopoly on 12 atomic minerals (like Lithium and Titanium),
    opening them for private sector exploration via a single "Exploration License".


   National Critical Mineral Mission (2025): Focuses on 30 identified minerals, like Copper, Nickel, and Manganese, essential
    for the green energy transition, aiming for Viksit Bharat 2047 goals.
   PM GatiShakti: Synchronizes infrastructure planning, reduces logistics costs, and speeds up approvals.
   Urban Mining: recycling of metals (e.g., Rare Earth Elements) to reduce the need for fresh primary extraction (Japan’s model)
However, sustainable/Green mining should be adopted to transition from an extractive activity to a "cradle-to-cradle" industrial
process that prioritizes inter-generational equity and ecological restoration.

1.4 Challenges Faced by Primary Industries in India
 Fragmented            Small and scattered landholdings limit          According to NABARD survey, average farm landholding fell
 Land Holdings         mechanization and reduce productivity.          from 1.08 hectares in 2016-17 to 0.74 hectares in 2021-22.
 Low                   Yield levels remain low due to poor seed        Average yield of sorghum in India is 1064 kg/ha which is
 Productivity          quality, input use, and farm practices.         much less than that of the world average of 1460 kg/ha.
 Monsoon               Rain-fed farming dominates, making              Approx 55 per cent of India's net sown area is rain-dependent
 Dependence            output vulnerable to monsoon failure.           and grows 34 of the country's 40-odd major crops.
 Inadequate            Poor logistics, outdated transport, and         Nearly 16% of fruits and vegetables are wasted annually
 Infrastructure        weak storage impact efficiency, waste           due to poor storage and transportation infrastructure.
                       reduction, and market access for primary
                       industries in India.
 Overexploitation      Intensive farming and mining degrade            According to the Central Ground Water Board over 70%
 of Resources          natural resources.                              of Punjab’s groundwater blocks are officially categorized
                                                                       as "over-exploited".
 Lack of               Limited mechanization, especially in            India's farm mechanization level is still between 40 and
 Technology            hilly/tribal areas, reduces efficiency.         45%.
 Policy &              Delayed schemes, red tape, and                  Distribution of soil health card has not been upto mark.
 Implementation        mismatch in policy design vs ground
 Gaps                  needs hamper outcomes.
As the backbone of the real economy, primary industries remain vital for food security, industrial development, and
environmental sustainability. Strengthening productivity while ensuring sustainable resource management will be key to
fostering resilient livelihoods and long-term economic growth.

Define blue revolution, explain the problems and strategies for pisciculture development in India. (2018)

chapters.part: Location Of Industries In The World
chapters.text:
2            Secondary Industries

Secondary industries involve processing or manufacturing of raw materials into finished or semi-finished goods. They are vital
for value addition, employment, and industrial growth, especially in developing economies like India. Hence, while primary
industries provide extraction, secondary industries create value by altering the form of the resource. Examples include textiles, steel,
cement, automobile, and food processing.

Alfred Weber’s Least Cost Theory (1909) is the foundational model for industrial location, aiming to minimize costs to maximize
profits. It determines optimal factory sites based on three primary factors: transportation costs (weight and distance), labor costs,
and agglomeration economies, often utilizing a "locational triangle" to find the lowest cost point.


Material Index (MI) = (Weight of Raw Materials / Weight of Finished Product).
 MI > 1 (Weight-Losing): Industry locates near the raw material (e.g., Steel/Sugar).
 MI < 1 (Weight-Gaining): Industry locates near the market (e.g., Soft drinks/Baking).

Example: cotton has a MI of ~1.0 (finished yarn weighs nearly the same as the raw lint) → the industry is not tied to the fields and
is highly Footloose.
Note: Isodapanes: lines connecting points of equal total transport cost; Critical Isodapane: the point where labor savings outweigh
transport costs.

2.1 Agro-Based Industries
Agro-based industries are those industries that derive their raw materials from agriculture and allied activities, and process
them into finished or semi-finished products. They have the highest employment-to-capital ratio, making them more critical for
Inclusive Growth than heavy industries. Thus, they play a crucial role in transforming the agricultural sector, promoting rural
development, and improving the socio-economic conditions of rural communities.

A. Sugar
India is the largest consumer and the second-largest producer of sugar globally, contributing about 20% to global sugar output,
with sugarcane cultivation spread across both tropical and subtropical regions.

The sugar industry in India is mainly concentrated in two regions: the northern states of Uttar Pradesh, Bihar, Haryana, and Punjab,
and the southern states of Maharashtra, Karnataka, Tamil Nadu, and Andhra Pradesh. Southern India’s tropical climate is more
favourable for sugarcane cultivation, resulting in higher sucrose content and greater yield per unit area than in the north.

B. Rubber
Rubber is a tropical plantation crop primarily cultivated in humid equatorial regions. It represents a climate-sensitive, export-
oriented industrial crop. India is one of the top 5 producers and fourth largest consumer of natural rubber (fluctuating rank
with Côte d’Ivoire and Vietnam ramping up production).
Note: The rubber industry is a mix of Natural Rubber (NR) and Synthetic Rubber (SR), derived from petroleum. India is a major
producer of SR (Panipat) and SR price volatility linked to crude oil impacts the demand for NR.

C. Coffee
Coffee is a significant commercial plantation crop grown in tropical highland regions. As of early 2026, India has breached the $2
billion mark in coffee export revenue. India is the world's top producer of "Shade-Grown" coffee, a premium niche that attracts
higher prices in Europe.
Largest Importing Countries: Italy, Russia, Germany, Belgium, UAE
Largest Producing states: Karnataka (~70%), Kerala (~20%) and Tamil Nadu (~5%) → production region expanding toward the
Eastern Ghats.
Geographical Indications (GI): Coorg Arabica (Karnataka); Wayanad Robusta (Kerala); Araku Valley Arabica (Andhra Pradesh)

 Flavor Profile                  Mild, aromatic, higher acidity.               Strong, full-bodied, higher caffeine.
 Resistance                      Susceptible to Leaf Rust.                     Highly resilient to pests and heat.

The industry operates on JIT (Just-In-Time) manufacturing → Parts must arrive at the assembly line exactly when needed to
avoid warehouse costs. This has evolved into "Just-In-Sequence" (JIS), where components are delivered in the specific order
they will be used on the assembly line, making physical proximity even more non-negotiable.
These models have reduced the dominance of Weight as the primary locational driver, shifting focus to Logistics Reliability.

2.6 Footloose Industries
Footloose industries in India have emerged as a key feature of the evolving industrial landscape, driven by globalization, liberalization,
and advancements in technology→ reflects a shift towards more flexible, market-oriented production systems, contributing significantly
to urbanization, employment generation, and integration with global value chains.


                                         Characteristics of Footloose Industries

 Characteristic                           Description                                                Example
Location                Not tied to raw material sources or markets              An IT company like Infosys can set up a campus
Flexibility             (spatial indifference) and can operate in diverse        in Bengaluru, Pune, or even a smaller city like
                        geographies with adequate infrastructure.                Mysuru, yet Spatial Stickiness due to "Talent
                                                                                 Density" and "Innovation Ecosystems"
Light Inputs            Use light, easily transportable raw materials            Rough diamonds imported to Surat for cutting
                        with negligible impact of transport cost (no             and polishing from Africa and Russia.

                        allowing efficient logistics even from remote            chip assembly and testing facility in Sanand,
                        areas.                                                   Gujarat in 2025, primarily for export markets.
Infrastructure-Led      They gravitate toward "Plug-and-Play"                    EMC 2.0 (Electronic Manufacturing Clusters):
                        infrastructure where electricity, water, and             Units in Noida or Sri City
                        connectivity are pre-established.
Minimal                 Small-scale, low-pollution units that require            TIDEL Park in Chennai.
Footprint               limited land and capital; generally eco-friendly.

                                 Factors Influencing Location of Footloose Industries

    Factor                              Description                                                  Example
Connectivity        Require efficient road, rail, air, and digital               NCR’s expressways and metro supported IT-BPO
                    connectivity (Low-latency 5G and fiber-optic density)        growth in Gurugram and Noida.
                    for mobility of people and goods.
Affordable          Prefer outskirts or peri-urban zones due to cheaper          Electronics firms established units in the outskirts

                    commute via public transport networks.                       daily workforce mobility in tech hubs.

Clusters            infrastructure, talent, and ideas.                           biotech firms in a cluster.
Proximity to        Need to be near research institutes and universities         Infosys and Wipro operate near IISc and IIIT-B in
R&D                 for innovation and skilled talent.                           Bengaluru.
CER Subsidies       Proximity to City Economic Regions (CERs) which              Pune and Surat among the new 7 7 City
                    receive up to ₹5,000 crore in modern infrastructure          Economic Regions
                    funding.

"Suburbanization" of Footloose Industries
In 2026, we are witnessing a de-densification of traditional hubs like Bengaluru and Mumbai. Footloose industries are leading the
charge into Tier-2 and Tier-3 cities for three primary reasons:
  Arbitrage on Talent: Companies like Infosys and Wipro are setting up satellite offices in cities like Mysuru, Coimbatore,
     and Lucknow to leverage local talent without the high "Metro-Premium" on salaries.
  "Temple Town" Economic Push: The 2026 government focus on developing infrastructure in Ayodhya, Varanasi, and
     Madurai has unexpectedly attracted digital services and artisanal luxury brands (Footloose) to these regions.
  Reverse Migration Resilience: Post-2024 hybrid work trends have solidified. Footloose firms now choose locations where
     employees want to live, rather than where they have to live→ Reverse Brain Drain or Circular Migration where senior
     tech talent is moving back to hometowns.

                                   Challenges Faced by Footloose Industries in India

    Aspect                             Description                                                 Example
Skilled Talent       High attrition in sectors like IT and BPOs due to        Infosys’ voluntary attrition rate was 14.4% as of
Retention            competitive job markets and frequent job-                June 2025, reflecting ongoing retention challenges
                     hopping.                                                 in IT-BPO.


 AI                  AI is now moving beyond "low-skill" chatbots to       Economic Survey 2025–26 warned that unchecked
 Displacement        automating specialized tasks like legal research      AI adoption could destabilize the workforce,
                     and basic software coding.                            replacing high-skill, labor-intensive roles.
 Global              Intense competition from countries with lower         Indian BPOs face intense price-based competition
 Competition         costs or better infrastructure reduces                from the Philippines, which has gained global share
                     profitability.                                        in voice and customer support services
 Infrastructure      Inadequate digital connectivity, power supply,        Startups in small towns struggle due to inconsistent
 Gaps                or transport in Tier-2/3 cities hampers operations.   internet speed and tech support.
 Data Security       Vulnerability to cyber-attacks, especially in IT/     CoWIN alleged data breach exposes India's gaps in
 Risks               BPO industries, undermines global client trust.       cyber infrastructure.
As global competition for energy, critical minerals, and strategic maritime spaces intensifies, resource-rich regions will
increasingly shape the geopolitical and economic landscape. For India, securing sustainable access to these resources through
strategic partnerships, responsible resource governance, and adherence to international law will be vital for ensuring energy
security, economic resilience, and strategic autonomy.

chapters.text:
3               Tertiary Industries

Tertiary industries refer to the service sector that provides intangible goods and services such as retail, transport, finance, education,
healthcare, etc. (Clark-Fisher Hypothesis→the structural shift from primary to tertiary as economies mature). In India, the services
sector contributed a record 56.4% of Gross Value Added (GVA) in FY 2025–26 (FAE), making it the dominant driver of growth.
However, India is often cited as having "leapfrogged" from an agrarian to a service-led economy without a proportionate expansion
of manufacturing. Increasingly, advances in Industry 4.0 technologies are enabling the tertiary sector to catalyse and support
manufacturing competitiveness through digitalization, automation, and innovation.

                                                    UPSC Mains PYQs
      1.     What are the main socio-economic implications arising out of the development of IT
             industries in major cities of India? (2021)


                                Factors Responsible for the Location of Tertiary Industries

      Factor                             Description                                                 Example
 Market               High population concentration drives demand             Urban centres generate 63% of GDP (projected 75%
 Demand               for services like retail, healthcare, and education.    by 2030)—driving demand in health, education,
                                                                              retail.
                                                                              Servicification of Manufacturing: products like cars
                                                                              or planes are sold with lifelong service/software
                                                                              contracts
 Skilled Labour       Tertiary industries like IT, consulting, and            The service sector provides employment to
 Availability         healthcare need an educated, tech-savvy                 approximately 30% of the workforce.
                      workforce → Specialized Expertise (AI,
                      Bio-informatics, ESG reporting).
 Infrastructure       Reliable internet, roads, and airports support          Hyderabad’s HITEC City thrives due to strong digital
 Availability         services like IT, logistics, and tourism.               infrastructure and good connectivity with global clients.
 Proximity to         Nearness to domestic and global clients                 Mumbai’s financial sector benefits from its closeness
 Markets              enables faster service delivery and reduced cost.       to corporate headquarters and stock exchanges.
 Data Center          Location increasingly dictated by the proximity         Mumbai, Chennai, and the new station in Kochi
 Geography            to Data Centers and Submarine Cable
                      Landing Stations
 Institutional        SEZs, STPIs, and FDI liberalisation attract             GIFT City in Gujarat was developed with tax
 Support              investments and boost global competitiveness.           incentives and relaxed norms to attract financial
                                                                              services firms.

 Global Capability Centers (GCCs )
 In recent times, the location of tertiary industries is being reshaped by GCCs. They represent the evolution of multinational
 operations in India, transitioning from cost-arbitrage centers to strategic "knowledge hubs" that anchor India’s integration into
 the global knowledge economy.
     The "Global Office" Concept: India has 2,117 GCCs employing over 2.3 million professionals, generating nearly $100
      billion in revenue. They choose locations based on "Innovation Ecosystems"—where they can collaborate with startups
      and academic labs (e.g., IIT-Madras Research Park).
     The Rise of "Shadow Boards": Many MNCs are basing their global strategy leaders in NCR and Bengaluru, transforming
      these cities from delivery centers into global decision-making hubs.


                                     Challenges Faced by Tertiary Industries in India

      Aspect                          Description                                              Example
 Infrastructure       Inadequate digital and physical infrastructure    Tier-2 cities lack robust airport and internet
 Gaps                 hampers service delivery, especially in rural     infrastructure, limiting IT and BPO expansion beyond
                      areas.                                            metros.

                      the evolving needs of service industries like     roles are expected to change as automation and AI
                      IT, finance. The gap goes beyond "tech" to        increase productivity.
                      "Deep AI" and "Creative Strategy."
 Regulatory           Licensing, FDI norms, data privacy laws,          E-commerce and fintech startups face delays due to
 Complexity           and sectoral regulations can hinder growth        unclear norms on FDI, data localisation, and taxation.
                      and compliance.
 Cybersecurity        Growing digitalisation raises risks related to    In 2026, banking frauds rose by 30% in value, reaching
 & Data Issues        data theft, privacy breaches, and cybercrime.     ₹21,515 crore, often via "Digital Arrest" scams.
 Uneven               Tertiary growth is concentrated in metros;        Tier-1 cities (Eg: Bengaluru, Mumbai) dominate IT/
 Regional             smaller cities and rural areas lag due to lack    ITES, while Tier-2, Tier-3 cities have fewer
 Development          of facilities.                                    opportunities.

3.1 IT Industry in India
According to NASSCOM, total revenue from the IT tech industry increased from USD 118 billion in FY 2014–15 to an estimated
USD 283 Billion in FY2024-25(E). Export revenue surged from USD 100 billion to USD224 billion , reinforcing India’s status
as a global IT powerhouse. The transition from "Scale-led growth" (adding more people) to "Value-led growth" (selling intellectual
property and software products) is reflected in the transition from BPO (Outsourcing) to BPM (Management) and KPO (Knowledge
Process Outsourcing, defining India's IT Sector 2.0 in 2026.

                                           Driving Factors of India’s IT Industry

      Factor                            Description                                              Example
 Skilled               A large, English-speaking talent pool with strong     According to report by TeamLease,India produces
 Workforce             technical education supports global IT services.      over 1.5 million engineers annually.
 Policy                Government initiatives promote IT infrastructure,     Digital India and Software Technology Parks of India
 Support               innovation, and digital penetration.                  (STPI) schemes.
 Emerging              Rapid adoption of AI, cloud, blockchain, and          TCS launched a Quantum Computing Lab on AWS
 Technologies          cybersecurity enhances India’s global                 to support enterprise solutions using Amazon
                       competitiveness.                                      Braket.
 Global Client         Strong Indian MNCs serve Fortune 500 clients,         Infosys has bagged a digital transformation deal
 Base                  driving exports and reputation.                       with Nordic-based Danske Bank.

                                       Challenges Faced by the IT Industry in India

    Challenge                               Description                                             Example
 Skill Gap and          Lack of skilled manpower in emerging technologies         NASSCOM found only 25% of Indian
 Talent Mismatch        hinders growth and forces firms to train fresh recruits   engineering graduates employable in AI and
                        heavily.                                                  cloud computing.
 Automation and         Rapid automation, AI, and machine learning reduce         Infosys and TCS have reskilled thousands due
 Job Displacement       the demand for entry-level coding and BPO jobs.           to automation replacing manual IT processes.
 Jobless                AI-driven productivity gains allow fewer people to        While revenue is growing by 6.1%, net hiring
 Growth                 do more work.                                             is only at 2.3% (~135,000 jobs)
 Rising Global          Indian IT firms face stiff competition from countries     MNCs are outsourcing to Vietnam due to
 Competition            like the Philippines, Vietnam, and Eastern Europe in      lower costs and increasing English
                        outsourcing.                                              proficiency.


Dominance of            India lacks domestic equivalents of Baidu or              Google, Meta, Amazon dominate India’s
Foreign Tech            Alibaba and markets are captured by US firms.             digital economy with minimal domestic
Firms                                                                             alternatives.
Cyber                   Key risks include ransomware, phishing, and supply        Cyber Threat Landscape Report 2026
Vulnerability           chain attacks, with many of Indian firms ranking          highlights more sophisticated AI-led phishing.
                        cyber breaches as a top threat.
Digital Divide          Unequal internet access and digital literacy hinder       BharatNet progress remains uneven; rural
                        equitable participation in IT economy from rural          youth lack access to coding and digital
                        regions.                                                  training; urban centers have 5G-Advanced
                                                                                  while rural areas still struggle with basic
                                                                                  fiber-optic latency.
Urban                   IT industry remains highly concentrated in metros,        Bengaluru and Hyderabad dominate IT jobs,
Concentration           leading to urban crowding and neglect of smaller          while cities like Ranchi and Imphal lag
                        cities.                                                   behind.

Way Forward
   IndiaAI Mission provides for the "sovereign compute" and "GPU-clusters" that allow the IT industry to move into Deep AI.
   Better implementation of Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act and the National Cyber Security Reference Framework
    (NCRF) 2025, the primary regulatory driver.
   Aggressive pivot towards emerging technologies to maintain global competitiveness. Upskilling the workforce in AI, Machine
    Learning (ML), Blockchain, and IoT to bridge the current high skill gap.
   Shifting the growth engine from saturated Tier-1 metros to Tier-2 and Tier-3 cities. Developing research hubs in cities like
    Coimbatore, Kochi, and Bhubaneswar to reduce urban strain in Bengaluru.
   Strengthening Industry-Academia collaboration for a "job-ready" workforce. Aligning education with industry needs to address
    the >50% non-job-ready graduate rate.
   Enhancing Intellectual Property (IP) and data security frameworks. Streamlining the patent process for tech startups to protect
    innovations and comply with international regulations.
pyq: What are the main socio-economic implications arising out of the development of IT industries in major cities of India? (2021)

chapters.part: Location Of Industries In The World
chapters.text:
4              Industrial Corridors

Industrial corridors are strategic linear development zones that connect major economic centres through integrated multimodal
infrastructure such as roads, railways, ports, and airports, enabling seamless freight and passenger movement. The Union Budget
2026-27 has accelerated India’s infrastructure vision by announcing the Integrated East Coast Industrial Corridor with a key
node at Durgapur and allocating ₹3,000 crore to the NICDIT (National Industrial Corridor Development and Implementation Trust
under DPIIT) for ongoing projects. The body is responsible for coordinating the 11 Industrial Corridors under the National
Industrial Corridor Development Programme (NICDP).


                                                 UPSC Mains PYQs
       1.    What is the significance of Industrial Corridors in India? Identifying industrial corridors,
             explain their main characteristics. (2018)


                             Factors Influencing Location of Industrial Corridors in India

       Factor                            Description                                             Example
 Connectivity           Proximity to Dedicated Freight Corridors              Delhi-Mumbai Industrial Corridor aligns with
                        (DFCs), major highways, ports, and airports           Western DFC.
                        reduces logistics cost and time.
 Proximity to           Urban agglomerations provide skilled                  Bengaluru-Chennai corridor benefits from
 Urban Centres          workforce, consumer markets, and service              nearby tech and manufacturing hubs.
                        ecosystems critical for industrial growth.
 Land and Utilities     Availability of large, non-arable land parcels with   Gujarat offers industrial land banks for Dholera
                        assured power and water is critical for               Special Investment Region (SIR).
                        industrial hubs.


Industrial Base         Corridors often leverage existing industrial hubs          Visakhapatnam-Chennai Industrial Corridor
                        and urban agglomerations for quicker                       (VCIC) builds on Visakhapatnam’s existing
                        integration and growth.                                    petroleum, chemicals, and pharma industries.
Policy and              Investor-friendly policies, fast-tracking of               Gujarat and Andhra Pradesh offer single-window
Governance              clearances, and institutional readiness                    clearances and incentives.
                        influence corridor viability.
                                              Significance of Industrial Corridors

     Aspect                                 Description                                               Example
Industrial and          Facilitates planned industrialization, boosts              Delhi-Mumbai Industrial Corridor spans across 7
Economic Growth         manufacturing and enhances GDP contribution.               states with investment of USD 100 billion.
Infrastructure          Promotes integrated development of transport,              Krishnapatnam Node in Andhra Pradesh under
Modernization           energy, logistics, and urban infrastructure.               the Chennai-Bengaluru Industrial Corridor.
Employment and          Generates large-scale direct and indirect                  AURIC (Aurangabad Industrial City) has
Skill Development       employment and supports skilling through                   generated over 60,000 jobs through smart city
                        industrial training.                                       and industrial synergy.
FDI and                 Attracts foreign investment by offering                    Dholera Special Investment Region (Gujarat)
Technology              plug-and-play facilities, stable policies, and             attracted companies like Tata Power, Vedanta,
Transfer                SEZ incentives.                                            and global tech firms.
Balanced Regional       Aims to reduce regional disparities by focusing            Amritsar-Kolkata Industrial Corridor (AKIC).
Development             on underdeveloped and interior regions.

                                    Challenges Faced by Industrial Corridors in India

     Aspect                                 Description                                               Example
Land                    Land acquisition delays, ecological concerns, and          Dholera SIR witnessed prolonged protests
Constraints             clearance hurdles obstruct timely development.             affecting land acquisition timelines.
Infrastructure          Gaps in last-mile rail, road, power, and utilities delay   Bengaluru-Mumbai corridor struggles with
Deficits                node activation and affect investor confidence.            inadequate feeder roads and utility access.
Slow Regulatory         Environmental, forest, and zoning approvals                Chennai-Bengaluru corridor stalled due to
Clearances              are time-consuming, delaying project timelines             pending environmental and forest clearances.
                        and deterring investment.
Financial Gaps          High capital cost with insufficient private/foreign        Several corridor projects face funding crunch
                        investment causes slow implementation.                     leading to stalled development phases.

                    Reasons for the rise of industrial corridors in Tier-2 and Tier-3 cities in India

     Aspect                                 Description                                               Example
Affordable Land         Land, labour, and operational costs are                    Dholera (Gujarat) and Aurangabad (Maharashtra)
and Lower Costs         significantly lower compared to Tier-1 cities.             attract industries with cost advantages.
Metro                   Shifting focus reduces pressure on                         AURIC near Aurangabad diverts manufacturing
Decongestion            overcrowded Tier-1 cities and promotes                     investment from crowded Mumbai and Pune
                        balanced development.                                      regions.
State                   States offer tax breaks, SEZ benefits, and single-         Sri City in A.P. attracted ₹3750 crore investments
Incentives              window clearances to attract investment.                   and created 17000 jobs.
Improved                Expansion of road, rail, power, and digital                Amritsar-Kolkata and Bengaluru-Mumbai
Infrastructure          infrastructure supports industrial expansion.              corridors include Tier-2/3 nodes with new infra.
Urbanisation and        Rising urban middle class in smaller cities                Indore and Bhubaneswar see growth in FMCG,
Market Expansion        creates local demand and investment                        auto, and electronics manufacturing.
                        opportunities.


Government Initiatives
   PM GatiShakti National Master Plan: Industrial corridors are now planned via the GIS-based GatiShakti platform to ensure
    synchronized infrastructure and eliminate the challenge of “silos”.
   Shift from "Linear Zones" to Hub-and-Spoke system: The "Hubs" are the Investment Regions (IRs) or Industrial Areas
    (IAs), and the "Spokes" are the DFCs (Dedicated Freight Corridors) or High-Speed Rail links.
   Energy Corridors Linkage: Industrial corridors are increasingly aligned with Green Energy Corridors. New nodes (like
    Dholera) are being branded as "Net-Zero Industrial Clusters" to attract global ESG-conscious FDI.
   National Single Window System (NSWS): Direct policy tool to solve delayed approvals.
   Corridors must move beyond "Low-end Assembly" to "High-end R&D" (GCCs)” to ensure they don't just become sites for
    low-cost labor exploitation.

As India aspires to become a global manufacturing and supply chain hub, industrial corridors will play a pivotal role in enhancing
competitiveness, regional development, and employment generation. Strengthening multimodal connectivity, ease of doing business,
and green industrial ecosystems will be key to their long-term success.

     &
  Density
     of
Population

chapters.text:
1               Population in Ganga Basin

The Ganga River Basin, a part of the Indo-Gangetic Plain, represents one of the most extensive depositional plains in the world.
It acts as a "physio-cultural region" where a unique confluence of land, soil, and water has created one of the highest concentrations
of human population on Earth.
                                                   UPSC Mains PYQs
      1.     Discuss the distribution and density of population in the Ganga River Basin with special
             reference to land, soil and water resources. (2025)

                                                  Land (Topography & Relief)
 Level/gently undulating lowlands → facilitates transport networks (road, rail),        Very high rail density in UP-Bihar.
 industrial clustering → high settlement density
 Dominance of compact/nucleated settlements due to fertile land &                       Dense rural settlements across UP–Bihar
 favourable climate → maximizes agricultural land use                                   belt
 Regional Variations                                                                    y Bihar ~1,106 persons/sq km
 y Middle Ganga Plain → Extreme population density, high demographic
   pressure on land and resources
 y Deltaic fertility + maritime influence → dense population concentration

                                                            Soil Fertility
 Thick alluvial deposits (depositional plain) → highly fertile, supports intensive      Indo-Gangetic alluvium (globally productive
 agriculture → Supports rice–wheat–jute system, enabling surplus production             soils); Historical base of Magadha Empire
 Khadar vs Bhangar: Khadar (new alluvium) → annually renewed, high                      Gorakhpur Floodplains vs Allahabad Khera
 fertility; Bhangar (old alluvium) → elevated, stable for settlements                   terrace settlements
 Salinity Trap: Intensive irrigation has led to soil alkalinity (Reh/Usar lands),       Western Gangetic belt (UP/Haryana)
 which is causing a reverse migration or stagnation in agricultural settlement
 density.
 Land fragmentation, marginal holdings, limits economies of scale despite               Marginal farmers are heavily concentrated
 fertility                                                                              in Middle Ganga Plain

                                     Water Availability (Hydrological Endowment)
 Perennial river system (glacial-fed) ensures year-round water for irrigation,          Ganga River and tributaries: Yamuna,
 domestic & industrial use                                                              Gandak, Son
 National Waterway-1 (NW-1) → enhances trade, connectivity, urban growth                Allahabad–Haldia stretch; cities like
                                                                                        Varanasi, Patna, Kolkata
 Upper reaches support hydroelectricity → industrial growth                             Rihand Dam (UP): Son River; Tehri
                                                                                        (Uttarakhand): Bhagirathi

Emerging Challenges
While the basin is resource-rich, it faces a developmental paradox:
 Groundwater–Baseflow Paradox: Excessive groundwater extraction in the Gangetic Valley is depleting aquifers that maintain
    river baseflows, creating the risk of rivers "drying from below" with serious implications for agricultural and food security.
 Downstream Siltation: Downstream sediment trapping by upstream dams is the primary cause of fertility loss in the Gangetic
    delta.
 Environmental Hazards: The flat gradient makes the middle and lower courses highly prone to recurring floods (e.g., the
    Kosi "Sorrow of Bihar"), which displace millions annually.
 Climate change: Glacial retreat (3-5% Himalayan ice loss/decade), erratic monsoons and intensified floods/droughts, threatening
    alluvial fertility and irrigation security.
           A recent study by CEEW , which mapped monsoon trends at the tehsil level from 1982–2022 revealed that 64% of
            tehsils reported an increase in the frequency of heavy rainfall days, which can lead to flash floods, particularly in states
            like Maharashtra, Tamil Nadu, Gujarat, and Karnataka.


Way Forward
   Transitioning to the Arth Ganga Model is essential—aiming to contribute 3% of India's GDP from within the basin through
    sustainable river-centric economic activities.
   Adaptation via Namami Gange catchment treatment, drought-resistant rice-wheat varieties (Sahbhagi Dhan), and Jal Shakti
    Abhiyan micro-irrigation can sustain Ganga Basin's 600 million population amid climate stressors.
   Digital Twin modeling: Ganga River Basin now requires a basin-wide Digital Twin to manage the "Environmental Hazards"
    of floods.
pyq: Discuss the distribution and density of population in the Ganga River Basin with special reference to land, soil and water resources. (2025)

chapters.text:
2           Population in Himalayan
                       Region
The Indian Himalayan Region (IHR), spanning 13 States and Union Territories—including Ladakh, Sikkim, Arunachal Pradesh, and
the hill districts of West Bengal and Assam is often called the “Water Towers of India” due to its critical cryosphere–hydrosphere
linkages. The region’s water security depends not merely on the volume of glacial melt but also on its seasonal timing, which is
increasingly being altered by climate change and Arctic Amplification. Simultaneously, rising tourism and developmental
pressures are pushing these ecologically fragile mountains towards a tipping point, where resource demand increasingly exceeds
the ecosystem’s carrying and regenerative capacity.
                                                UPSC Mains PYQs
      1.    The states of Jammu and Kashmir, Himachal Pradesh, and Uttarakhand are reaching
            the limits of their ecological carrying capacity due to tourism. Critically evaluate. (2015)


Impacts of Tourism in the Indian Himalayan Region (IHR)


                                          Natural Resources and Biodiversity
 Resource Stress     Structural mismatch between resource availability & tourist       Shimla faced “Day Zero” crisis (2018);
 (Water)             demand; episodic peak-season pressure leads to water              Leh meets ~80% water needs from
                     scarcity and over-extraction of groundwater                       groundwater
 Biodiversity        Tourism infrastructure → habitat fragmentation, forest            15–20% decline in forest density in
 Fragmentation       degradation, spread of invasive species                           high-tourism zones
 Waste Crisis        High solid waste generation (plastic-heavy) in ecologically       India generates more than 25,000 tonnes
                     fragile zones; poor waste management contaminates ecosystems      of plastic waste everyday

                                  Geomorphological Instability and Disaster Risks
 Land                Exceeding carrying capacity + construction on fragile             Joshimath crisis (2023) Multi-Hazard
 Subsidence          geology (moraines) → ground instability                           Trap: Peak tourist season overlaps with
                                                                                       monsoon-driven slope saturation and
                                                                                       convective instability.
 Slope               Haphazard urbanisation + infrastructure expansion                 Char Dham & all-weather road projects
 Destabilization     (roads, hotels) → landslides, flash floods                        linked to increased landslide frequency
 Glacial             Black carbon deposition from vehicles accelerates glacial         Increased GLOF threats in Himalayan
 Vulnerability       melt, raising GLOF risks                                          glacial lakes


                                   Socio-Cultural and Infrastructure Constraints
 Traffic & Air       Excess tourism → traffic congestion, vehicular emissions,        Manali & Mussoorie frequently
 Pollution           degrading fragile mountain air quality                           experience multi-hour traffic congestion
                                                                                      during peak seasons
 Carrying            Overtourism exceeds infrastructural limits (healthcare,          Kedarnath Yatra for 2026 has witnessed
 Capacity Stress     sanitation, accommodation)                                       an unprecedented, record-breaking surge
                                                                                      in devotees, regularly exceeding the
                                                                                      established daily limits of 13,000 to
                                                                                      14,000 pilgrims.
 Demographic         Seasonal tourist influx → Seasonal Transient Demographic         Hill towns experience sharp seasonal
 Pressure            Density, stressing local services and population.                population spikes; affected Migratory
                     Disrupted transhumance movement due to Tourism                   pastoral populations Gujjars/Bakarwals
                     Infrastructure→ socio-cultural erosion.
                     Permanent Out-migration: Himalayan "ghost villages", abandoned or depopulated mountain settlements
                     are driven by the lack of basic amenities, inaccessible healthcare, and failing agriculture due to climate
                     change.

Way Forward: Sustainable Tourism in the Himalayan Region
   Decentralised Tourism Planning: Address spatial concentration by promoting lesser-known destinations beyond existing
    “tourism islands” like Shimla, Nainital, and Srinagar to reduce ecological pressure and ensure balanced regional development.
 Sustainable Economic Integration: Recognise tourism’s 10–15% contribution to GSDP in Himalayan states and shift focus
    from restriction to regulated, sustainable tourism models.
 Scientific Carrying Capacity Assessment: Institutionalise region-specific carrying capacity studies to guide infrastructure
    development and tourist inflow, as highlighted by the Ashok Kumar Raghav vs. Union of India (2023).
 Strengthening Governance & Regulation: Bridge policy and enforcement gaps through stricter land-use planning,
    environmental clearances, entry permits for sensitive zones and real-time monitoring.
 Economic Instruments & Performance Indexing: Implement a Green Cess (Payment for Ecosystem Services" (PES)) on
    tourists to fund ecological restoration alongside adopting a Himalayan Sustainable Development Index (as proposed by NITI
    Aayog) to rank and guide states on sustainability outcomes.
To prevent an ecological collapse, the transition from "Mass Tourism" to "Value-based Eco-tourism" is imperative, aligning with
SDG 6.6 (Protecting water-related ecosystems) and SDG 11.4 (Protecting world cultural and natural heritage) and the
National Mission for Sustaining the Himalayan Ecosystem (NMSHE).


  Geographical features and their
     location-changes in critical
       geographical features
(including water-bodies and ice-caps)
and in flora and fauna and the effects
           of such changes
pyq: The states of Jammu and Kashmir, Himachal Pradesh, and Uttarakhand are reaching the limits of their ecological carrying capacity due to tourism. Critically evaluate. (2015)
